llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces robust SDE/BSDE-based variational formulations for solving high-dimensional linear Kolmogorov PDEs with deep learning. The paper (i) provides a systematic numerical and theoretical comparison of FeynmanвҖ“Kac (FK) versus BSDE-based losses, (ii) proves that FK losses yield inherently higher estimator and gradient variances (non-robust), while BSDE formulations achieve zero-variance gradients at the solution, (iii) proposes new practical, low-variance losses (LBSDE, Ldetach_BSDE, Lgrad_BSDE) and memory-efficient implementations (LBSDE,eff and Lgrad_BSDE,eff), and (iv) demonstrates substantial performance and robustness improvements across several high-dimensional PDE benchmarks.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Formulates PDE solving as minimizing variational losses derived from SDE representations. Baseline FK loss: L_FK(u)=E[(g(X_T)-u(Оҫ,П„))^2], with X solving the forward SDE. Proposes BSDE/control-variate loss: L_BSDE(u)=E[(О”_u - S_u)^2], where О”_u=g(X_T)-u(Оҫ,П„), S_u=вҲ«_П„^T (Пғ^T вҲҮ_x u)(X_s,s) В· dW_s. Key theory: Lemma 2.1 (optimal control variate О”_V=S_V), Proposition 2.4 (L_FK has nonzero loss/variance at optimum; L_BSDE zero), Proposition 2.6 (gradient variance at solution: nonzero for L_FK, zero for L_BSDE), and Proposition 2.7 (stability of L_BSDE gradient variance near solution when both u and вҲҮu are well-approximated). Practical variants: (1) Ldetach_BSDE(u,w)=E[(О”_u - S_w)^2] with differentiation only w.r.t. u (w detached), eliminating second derivatives; (2) Lgrad_BSDE(u,r)=E[(О”_u - вҲ« Пғ^T r dW)^2] with a separate network r(x,t) approximating вҲҮu to avoid second derivatives. Efficient implementation: recompute path-wise stochastic integral gradients on-the-fly and accumulate G2,j per time step to keep GPU memory independent of time discretization steps. Training uses EulerвҖ“Maruyama discretization, Monte Carlo estimators, autodiff, and Adam; networks are multilevel architectures with SiLU activations.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Benchmarks: (1) Heat equation with paraboloid terminal condition in d=50, T=1, ОҪ=0.5 (and ablations over ОҪ). Оҫ~Unif([-0.5,0.5]^d), П„~Unif([0,1]). Closed-form V and S available for analysis. (2) BlackвҖ“Scholes rainbow put with correlated noise in d=50, T=1, bМ„=-0.05, Оә=5.5, ОІ_i=0.1+i/(2d), Оҫ~Unif([4.5,5.5]^d), П„~Unif([0,1]), Пғ from Cholesky of Q with corr 0.5. No closed form; evaluation via FeynmanвҖ“Kac Monte Carlo. (3) HJB equation (d=10) with double well potential ОЁ(x)=ОәвҲ‘(x_i^2-1)^2 (Оә=0.1), f=0, Пғ=I, terminal g(x)=exp(-О·вҲ‘(x_i-1)^2) with О·=0.04; linearized via HopfвҖ“Cole V=exp(-б№ј). Metrics and validation: mean squared error (MSE) of u vs reference V on independent evaluation points (N=10В·2^17 samples of (Оҫ,П„)); when V not closed-form (BlackвҖ“Scholes), inner conditional expectations are MC-estimated with 2^16 samples. Also report estimator loss values, estimator and gradient standard deviations over 30 independent batches. Training details: Adam optimizer; Multilevel NN (L=3, amplification qвҲҲ{3,5}, residual connections), SiLU activations; batch sizes KвҲҲ{2^4,вҖҰ,2^19}; SDE step-size О”tвҲҲ[1e-2,1e-3]; learning-rate schedules (two variants); GPU memory limit 8 GiB. Comparisons include FK, BSDE, grad-BSDE, their efficient variants, and detach-BSDE; also memory and runtime scaling studies.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Focuses primarily on linear Kolmogorov PDEs (with an HJB case handled via HopfвҖ“Cole transformation); broader classes of nonlinear PDEs and general boundary value problems are not fully explored experimentally. BSDE-based losses require SDE discretization and introduce additional computational cost; despite efficient implementations, training time can increase versus FK. Ldetach_BSDE can be sensitive to poor initialization, potentially increasing variance and harming convergence. Assumes smooth solutions (VвҲҲC^{2,1}) and neural networks capable of jointly approximating function and gradient; viscosity solution settings and weaker regularity are not treated in depth. Discretization bias (EulerвҖ“Maruyama) and its interaction with variance reduction are not fully analyzed. Experiments are limited to synthetic/high-dimensional setups without complex boundary conditions (e.g., exit times) or real-world datasets.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend robust BSDE-based, memory-efficient estimators to elliptic/parabolic boundary value problems on bounded domains, handling exit times efficiently (e.g., walk-on-spheres).
- Generalize to broader nonlinear PDEs (beyond HopfвҖ“Cole transform) and fully nonlinear settings via BSDEs; integrate with PINNs or hybrid residual/variational training.
- Develop advanced control variates and adaptive variance reduction (e.g., multilevel Monte Carlo, higher-order SDE schemes) and study biasвҖ“variance trade-offs rigorously.
- Improve optimization robustness: better initializations, curriculum strategies, adaptive batch sizing, and stabilization for Ldetach_BSDE.
- Provide theory for viscosity solutions, weaker regularity, and generalization error bounds when approximating both function and gradient with neural networks.
- Apply to real-world problems in finance, molecular dynamics, and diffusion-based generative modeling; couple with importance sampling and optimal control techniques.

research_study_list.title: Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning
research_study_list.abstract:
The combination of Monte Carlo methods and deep learning has recently led to
efficient algorithms for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) in high
dimensions. Related learning problems are often stated as variational
formulations based on associated stochastic differential equations (SDEs),
which allow the minimization of corresponding losses using gradient-based
optimization methods. In respective numerical implementations it is therefore
crucial to rely on adequate gradient estimators that exhibit low variance in
order to reach convergence accurately and swiftly. In this article, we
rigorously investigate corresponding numerical aspects that appear in the
context of linear Kolmogorov PDEs. In particular, we systematically compare
existing deep learning approaches and provide theoretical explanations for
their performances. Subsequently, we suggest novel methods that can be shown to
be more robust both theoretically and numerically, leading to substantial
performance improvements.
research_study_list.full_text: Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning Lorenz Richter * 1 2 3 Julius Berner * 4 Abstract The combination of Monte Carlo methods and deep learning has recently led to efп¬Ғcient algo- rithms for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) in high dimensions. Related learning prob- lems are often stated as variational formulations based on associated stochastic differential equa- tions (SDEs), which allow the minimization of corresponding losses using gradient-based opti- mization methods. In respective numerical im- plementations it is therefore crucial to rely on adequate gradient estimators that exhibit low vari- ance in order to reach convergence accurately and swiftly. In this article, we rigorously investigate corresponding numerical aspects that appear in the context of linear Kolmogorov PDEs. In par- ticular, we systematically compare existing deep learning approaches and provide theoretical ex- planations for their performances. Subsequently, we suggest novel methods that can be shown to be more robust both theoretically and numerically, leading to substantial performance improvements. 1. Introduction In this paper we suggest novel methods for solving high- dimensional linear Kolmogorov PDEs. In particular, those methods allow for more efп¬Ғcient approximations by reduc- ing the variance of loss estimators, as illustrated by numeri- cal experiments like the one displayed in Figure 1. High-dimensional PDEs are ubiquitous in applications ap- pearing in economics, science, and engineering, however, *Equal contribution (the author order was determined by numpy.random.rand(1)) 1dida Datenschmiede GmbH, Ger- many 2Zuse Institute Berlin, Germany 3Institute of Mathe- matics, Freie Universit ВЁat Berlin, Germany 4Faculty of Math- ematics, University of Vienna, Austria. Correspondence to: Julius Berner <julius.berner@univie.ac.at>, Lorenz Richter <lorenz.richter@fu-berlin.de>. Proceedings of the 39 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Baltimore, Maryland, USA, PMLR 162, 2022. Copy- right 2022 by the author(s). 102 104 batch size 10 4 10 3 10 2 10 1 100 estimator std. 102 104 batch size 10 4 10 3 10 2 10 1 MSE FK detach BSDE grad BSDE grad BSDE, eff BSDE BSDE, eff Figure 1.Standard deviation and performance (in terms of the mean squared error (MSE) on evaluation data) of different losses as a function of the batch size after training a neural network for 30ksteps, where due to memory constraints different losses allow for different maximal batch size values. their numerical treatment poses formidable challenges since traditional grid-based methods suffer from the curse of di- mensionality. Recently, the combination of Monte Carlo methods and deep learning has led to feasible algorithms (E et al., 2017; Sirignano & Spiliopoulos, 2018; Han et al., 2018; Raissi et al., 2019; Pfau et al., 2020; Berner et al., 2021), which, at least in some settings, are able to beat this curse (Jentzen et al., 2018; Reisinger & Zhang, 2019; Berner et al., 2020b). The general idea is to rely on stochas- tic representations of the PDE which may be interpreted as variational formulations that can subsequently be minimized by iterative gradient decent methods (N ВЁusken & Richter, 2021a). In respective algorithms it is therefore crucial to rely on adequate gradient estimators that exhibit low vari- ance and eventually lead to robust numerical optimization routines, where robustness refers to the ability of coping with stochasticity in the optimization process. In this article we focus on linear Kolmogorov PDEs for which variational formulations may be based on the cel- ebrated Feynman-Kac formula (Beck et al., 2021). This method is handy from an implementational point of view, however, we can demonstrate potential non-robustness is- sues that might deteriorate the numerical performance. To overcome these issues, we suggest alternative approaches resting on backward stochastic differential equations which lead to variance-reduced estimators and robust algorithms. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: вҖў We provide a systematic numerical comparison of loss arXiv:2206.10588v2  [cs.LG]  5 Aug 2022Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning functions for solving linear Kolmogorov PDEs via deep learning. вҖў We can show both theoretically and numerically why certain methods are non-robust and might therefore incur numerical instabilities. вҖў We provide new loss functions and optimization meth- ods which are provably robust and ultimately yield signiп¬Ғcant performance improvements in relevant high- dimensional numerical experiments. 1.1. Related Work Solving linear Kolmogorov PDEs via the Feynman-Kac for- mula by means of deep learning has been suggested in Beck et al. (2021) and further analyzed in Berner et al. (2020a). For an analysis of certain kinds of elliptic linear PDEs we re- fer to Grohs & Herrmann (2020). Variational formulations of nonlinear PDEs based on backward stochastic differ- ential equations (BSDEs) have been pioneered in E et al. (2017) and extensions that aim for approximations on entire domains have, for instance, been suggested in N ВЁusken & Richter (2021a). We refer to Richter (2021) for a comprehen- sive introduction and further perspectives, and to Beck et al. (2019) for the treatement of fully nonlinear equations. An al- ternative method for the approximation of high-dimensional PDEs is termed physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) (Raissi et al., 2019; Sirignano & Spiliopoulos, 2018), which relies on iterative residual minimizations. Note that in con- trast to most SDE-based methods it relies on the explicit computation of Hessians and involves additional loss terms for the boundary values, which need to be tuned carefully. In terms of variance reduction of Monte Carlo estimators we refer to Vidales et al. (2018) for a control variate attempt via deep learning. Robustness and variance analyses of certain loss functions related to PDE problems have been conducted in NВЁusken & Richter (2021b). The related observation that including a certain ItЛҶo integral term into the objective func- tion can lead to variance-reduced estimators has been made in Zhou et al. (2021). Both works, however, consider differ- ent settings and focus only on HamiltonвҖ“JacobiвҖ“Bellman (HJB) equations. Furthermore, they do not provide a de- tailed analysis of the interplay between robustness proper- ties, performance, and computational feasibility, which is crucial for practical applications. 1.2. Notation We assume that our random variables are deп¬Ғned on a suit- able underlying п¬Ғltered probability space (в„Ұ,F,(Ft)t,P ) satisfying the usual conditions (Klenke, 2013). We de- note the expectation of a random variable A: в„Ұ вҶ’ R by E [A] := вҲ« AdP . Given another random variable B: в„Ұ вҶ’ Rk with k вҲҲ N, we also consider the condi- tional expectation E [A|B] of Agiven Band the conditional expectation b вҶҰвҶ’ E [A|B = b] of A given B = b, see, e.g., Ash & DolВҙeans-Dade (2000) for the deп¬Ғnitions. We fur- ther deп¬Ғne the variance of Aby V [A] := E [ (AвҲ’E [A])2] and the conditional variance of Agiven B by V [A|B] := E [ (AвҲ’E [A|B])2|B ] . Throughout the article, W is a stan- dard d-dimensional Brownian motion which is adapted to the underlying п¬Ғltration and we consider stochastic integrals w.r.t. W as, e.g., deп¬Ғned in Gall (2016). Further remarks on the notation can be found in Appendix A. 2. SDE-Based Variational Formulations of Linear PDEs In this paper we consider linear Kolmogorov partial differ- ential equations1 of the type (вҲӮt + L)V(x,t) = 0, (x,t) вҲҲRd Г—[0,T), (1a) V(x,T) = g(x), x вҲҲRd, (1b) where gвҲҲC(Rd,R) is a given terminal condition and L:= 1 2 dвҲ‘ i,j=1 (ПғПғвҠӨ)ij(x,t)вҲӮxiвҲӮxj + dвҲ‘ i=1 bi(x,t)вҲӮxi (2) is a differential operator based on given coefп¬Ғcient functions ПғвҲҲC(RdГ—[0,T],RdГ—d) and bвҲҲC(RdГ—[0,T],Rd). For simplicity, let us assume the existence of a unique strong so- lution V вҲҲC2,1(RdГ—[0,T],R) to the PDE in (1) and refer to Assumption 1 in Appendix B for further technical details. At the same time, let us note that most of our results also hold for the more general concept of viscosity solutions that are continuous but not necessarily differentiable and require weaker conditions on the functions b,Пғ, and g, see Hairer et al. (2015). Furthermore, we can also consider more gen- eral linear PDEs as well as elliptic and parabolic problems on bounded domains, as elaborated on in Appendix C. This covers a broad spectrum of PDEs, for which accurate and reliable solvers are of great importance to practitioners. For instance, such PDEs frequently appear in physics for the modelling of heat п¬Ӯow and diffusion processes (Widder, 1976; Pascucci, 2005). Moreover, the PDE in (1) includes the Black-Scholes equation and extensions thereof, used for pricing п¬Ғnancial derivative instruments (Black & Sc- holes, 1973; Pironneau & Achdou, 2009; EkstrВЁom & Tysk, 2010). Finally, we want to emphasize appearances of such PDEs in the п¬Ғeld of machine learning, e.g., in the context of reinforcement learning (Theodorou et al., 2010; Kiu- marsi et al., 2017) and diffusion-based generative model- ing (Huang et al., 2021). 1PDEs of this type are often also referred to as Kolmogorov (backward) equations and their adjoints are known as Fokker- Planck equations or Kolmogorov forward equations.Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning In order to design machine learning algorithms aiming to approximate solutions to (1), we consider loss functions L: UвҶ’ RвүҘ0, (3) which shall be minimal if and only if u вҲҲU fulп¬Ғlls (1), thereby offering a variational formulation that allows for iterative minimization strategies. Here, Uis an appropri- ate function class holding sufп¬Ғcient approximation capacity and containing, for instance, a class of certain neural net- works. Moreover, we implicitly assume that Uis chosen such that V вҲҲU and, for our theoretical results, we require the functions in Uto be sufп¬Ғciently smooth. A п¬Ғrst obvious choice for the loss could be the squared difference between the approximating function uand the solution V, i.e. LEval(u) := E [ (V(Оҫ,П„ ) вҲ’u(Оҫ,П„ ))2 ] , (4) where Оҫand П„ are suitable2 random variables with values in Rd and [0,T], respectively. At п¬Ғrst glance, this loss seems intractable since the solution V is just the quantity we are after and is therefore not known. However, let us recall the Feynman-Kac formula, which connects the deterministic function V to a stochastic process by stating that almost surely it holds that V(Оҫ,П„ ) = E [g(XT)|(Оҫ,П„ )] , (5) where X is the solution to the SDE dXs = b(Xs,s) ds+ Пғ(Xs,s) dWs, X П„ = Оҫ, (6) see Appendix C for details. Algorithmic Details and Neural Network Approximations So far our analysis has been agnostic of the choice of the approximating function spaceU. In practice, we usually rely on functions represented by a neural network ОҰОё: Rd+1 вҶ’ R with parameters ОёвҲҲRp. The training of such a network is then conducted by minimizing a suitable estimated and time-discretized loss ЛҶL(K) using some variant of gradient descent. In its simplest form, this corresponds to the update rule Оё(m+1) = Оё(m) вҲ’О»вҲҮОё ЛҶL(K)(ОҰОё(m) ), (26) where О»вҲҲR>0 is a learning rate and Оё(0) вҲҲRp is a random initialization. Let us refer to Algorithm 1 in Appendix E for further details. Relating to the Gateaux derivatives from the previous sec- tion, let us note that we are particularly interested in the directions5 ПҶ = вҲӮОёiОҰОё for i вҲҲ{1,...,p }. This choice is motivated by (26) and the chain rule of the Gateaux deriva- tive, which, under suitable assumptions, states that вҲӮОёi ЛҶL(K)(ОҰОё) = Оҙ Оҙu вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ u=ОҰОё ЛҶL(K) (u; вҲӮОёiОҰОё) . (27) Now, in order to elaborate on some computational aspects, let us state the actual gradients of the corresponding losses. For a neural network u= ОҰОё, we compute вҲҮОё ЛҶL(K) FK (u) = вҲ’2 K KвҲ‘ k=1 ЛҶвҲҶ(k) u вҲҮОёu, (28a) вҲҮОё ЛҶL(K) BSDE(u) = вҲ’2 K KвҲ‘ k=1 e(k) ( вҲҮОёu+ вҲҮОёЛҶS(k) u ) , (28b) where e(k) := ЛҶвҲҶ(k) u вҲ’ЛҶS(k) u is the sample-wise error. From a computational point of view, note that the derivative of ЛҶL(K) FK as displayed in (28a) only relies on a single gradient computation of the approximating function u (w.r.t. the model parameters Оё), whereas the derivative of ЛҶL(K) BSDE as displayed in (28b) needs evaluations of вҲҮОёs(k) j , see (16) and (17). This might be costly for automatic differentiation (autodiff) tools and we will suggest computational speed- ups and memory-efп¬Ғcient versions in the next section. Further, note that due to the term ЛҶS(k) u the loss ЛҶL(K) BSDE al- ways needs to rely on discretized SDEs, whereas ЛҶL(K) FK can be computed without SDE simulations whenever explicit solutions of the stochastic processes are available (which, admittedly, is often not the case in practice вҖ“ see, however, the examples in Sections 3.1 and 3.2). 5We assume that the functionsОҰОё, ОёвҲҲRp, as well as all partial derivatives вҲӮОёiОҰОё lie in U. 2.3. Further Computational Adjustments For the loss LBSDE as deп¬Ғned in (11) we can make further adjustments that will turn out to be relevant from a com- putational point of view. In particular, let us suggest two alternative versions that are based on the fact that the func- tion uin (11) appears in two instances, which can be treated differently. One idea is to differentiate only with respect to one of its appearances. To this end, let us deп¬Ғne the loss Ldetach BSDE (u,w) := E [ (вҲҶu вҲ’Sw)2 ] , (29) now depending on two arguments. We then compute deriva- tives according to Оҙ Оҙu вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ w=u Ldetach BSDE (u,w), (30) where in automatic differentiation tools setting w= uafter computing the derivative is usually obtained by detaching wfrom the computational graph6. One can readily check that Ldetach BSDE is a valid loss for problem (1) that keeps the robustness property stated in Propositions 2.4 and 2.6 intact. At the same time we expect a great reduction of the compu- tational effort since the second-order derivatives of the form вҲҮОёвҲӮxiu, appearing in вҲҮОёЛҶS(k) u , do not need to be computed anymore вҖ“ in particular note that multiple such derivatives would need to be evaluated in actual implementations due to the discretization of the integral, as stated in(16b). We refer to Section 3 where we can indeed demonstrate substantial computational improvements in several settings. Another version of LBSDE relies on explicitly modelling the gradient of the approximating function uby an extra function, see also Zhou et al. (2021). We can therefore deп¬Ғne the loss Lgrad BSDE(u,r) := E [( вҲҶu вҲ’ЛңSr )2] , (31) where now ЛңSr = вҲ« T П„ ( ПғвҠӨr ) (Xs,s) В·dWs. (32) The additional function r : RdГ—[0,T] вҶ’Rd can be mod- elled with a separate neural network. The loss (31) is then minimized with respect to the parameters of the functions u and rsimultaneously, which again avoids the computation of second-order derivatives. Finally, let us introduce another viable option for decreas- ing computational resources, which is essentially a more memory-efп¬Ғcient way of computing the derivative formula stated in (28b). Usually, one performs a forward pass to 6In PyTorch and TensorFlow this can be achieved by the detach and stop gradient operations.Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning compute ЛҶL(K) BSDE(u) and then uses automatic differentiation to obtain the gradient вҲҮОё ЛҶL(K) BSDE(u). However, this requires to keep track of all the discretization steps for the stochastic integral (s(k) j )J(k) j=1 , as they all depend on Оё. This leads to a GPU memory consumption which scales linearly in the number of steps J(k) and, already for small K, exceeds common capacities, see Figures 5 and 7 in the appendix. To circumvent this issue, we observe that the derivative in (28b) can be decomposed into G1 := вҲ’2 K KвҲ‘ k=1 e(k)вҲҮОёu, (33) which equals the derivative of the loss Ldetach BSDE , and JвҲ‘ j=1 G2,j := JвҲ‘ j=1 вҲ’2 K KвҲ‘ k=1 e(k)вҲҮОёs(k) j , (34) where J = maxK k=1 J(k) and s(k) j = 0 for j >J(k). There- fore, we can п¬Ғrst compute and cache the errors (e(k))K k=1 and the gradients G1. Then, we repeat the same simula- tion, i.e., use the same realization of (Оҫ(k),П„(k),W(k)), to compute and accumulate the gradients (G2,j)J j=1 on-the-п¬Ӯy. In doing so, automatic differentiation only needs to track a single discretization step s(k) j . This keeps the memory footprint independent of the number of steps J(k) or, equiv- alently, the step-size t(k) j+1 вҲ’t(k) j вҖ“ however, at the cost of an increased computational time7. Of course, the same trick can also be applied to the loss Lgrad BSDE. Even though from a mathematical perspective the losses are still the same as LBSDE and Lgrad BSDE, we give them the new names LBSDE,eп¬Җ and Lgrad BSDE,eп¬Җ (35) in order to relate to their signiп¬Ғcant computational improve- ments, which we will illustrate in the next section. 3. Numerical Examples In this section we aim to illustrate our theoretical results on three high-dimensional PDEs 8. Speciп¬Ғcally, we con- sider a heat equation with paraboloid terminal condition, a HJB equation arising in molecular dynamics, and a Black- Scholes model with correlated noise. The respective refer- ence solutions are given as a closed-form expression, as a 7For most practical use-cases one is interested in obtaining the most accurate solution and there is only little restriction on the time for training, which needs to be done once per PDE. However, for time-critical applications, we present the performance w.r.t. the training time in Figures 11 and 12 in the appendix. 8The associated code can be found at https://github. com/juliusberner/robust_kolmogorov. tensor-product of one-dimensional approximations obtained by п¬Ғnite-difference methods, and as a Monte-Carlo approxi- mation using the Feynman-Kac formula, see Appendix C. In all of our numerical experiments we use comparable setups which are detailed in Appendix E. We emphasize that, instead of using a train/val/test split on a given п¬Ғnite data set, our setting allows us to simulate new i.i.d. samples (Оҫ(k),П„(k),W(k)) for each batch on demand during training. To evaluate our methods, we compute the MSE between the neural network approximation and the reference solution at points (x,t) which are sampled independently of the train- ing data, i.e., on previously unseen points, see Appendix E. We will show that all considered losses are viable in the sense that they yield appropriate approximations of solu- tions to the associated PDEs. At the same time we will see substantial performance differences, which can be attributed to the different variance properties outlined before. 3.1. Conclusion The most prominent method for solving general high- dimensional linear PDEs of Kolmogorov type is based on minimizing a variational formulation inspired by the Feynman-Kac formula over a class of neural networks. Building upon results on BSDEs and control variates for Monte Carlo estimators, we suggest an alternative formula- tion and prove that the corresponding loss estimator enjoys lower variance. Lending to the notion of Gateaux deriva- tives, we can further show that this leads to gradient estima- tors with lower variance, which is crucial for gradient-based optimization. Importantly, we also develop a novel, more efп¬Ғcient estimator that is adapted to current deep learning frameworks and show that resulting methods yield signiп¬Ғ- cant performance gains. It is up to future research to exploit such efп¬Ғcient estimators for boundary value problems, see Appendix C, and non-linear PDEs, which can naturally be approached with BSDE-based losses, see Appendix D. In summary, we suspect that the techniques used in this paper can be transferred to a number of related deep learn- ing methods that rely on Monte Carlo sampling. Providing important theoretical guarantees and performance improve- ments, our work is a fundamental step in developing robust and reliable algorithms for the solution of high-dimensional PDEs. Let us deп¬Ғne Оҙ(x,t) = u(x,t) вҲ’V(x,t). (64) Analogously to (61) we can compute V [ Оҙ Оҙu вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ u=V+Оҙ L(K) BSDE(u; ПҶ) ] = 4 K V [(Оҙ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SОҙ) (ПҶ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SПҶ)] . (65) Now, properties of the variance as well as the Cauchy- Schwarz inequality yield V [(Оҙ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SОҙ) (ПҶ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SПҶ)] вүӨE [ (Оҙ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SОҙ)2 (ПҶ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SПҶ)2 ] вүӨE [ (Оҙ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SОҙ)4 ]1 2 E [ (ПҶ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SПҶ)4 ]1 2 . Each of the above factors can be bounded by using the Burkholder-Davis-Gundy inequality (Da Prato & Zabczyk, 2014, Section 4.6), HВЁolderвҖҷs inequality, and the consistency of the Euclidean norm, i.e. E [ (Оҙ(Оҫ,П„ ) + SОҙ)4 ] вүӨ8 ( E [ Оҙ(Оҫ,П„ )4] + E [ S4 Оҙ ]) вүӨ8 ( Оө4 + 36T вҲ« T 0 E [ вҲҘ(ПғвҠӨвҲҮxОҙ)(Xs,s)вҲҘ4] ds ) вүӨ8Оө4 ( 1 + 36T вҲ« T 0 E [ вҲҘПғ(Xs,s)вҲҘ4 F(1 + вҲҘXsвҲҘОі)4] ds ) . Deп¬Ғning C := 32 ( 1+36T вҲ« T 0 E [ вҲҘПғ(Xs,s)вҲҘ4 F(1 + вҲҘXsвҲҘОі)4] ds ) , we thus showed that V [ Оҙ Оҙu вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ u=V+Оҙ L(K) BSDE(u; ПҶ) ] вүӨCОө2Оә2 K , (66) which proves the claim. C. Feynman-Kac Theorem and More General Linear PDEs As outlined in Section 2 and for instance stated in (5) as well as (13), the Feynman-Kac formula brings a stochastic representation of the linear PDE in (1) via V(x,t) = E [g(XT)|Xt = x] (67a) = E [g(XT)|(Оҫ,П„ ) = (x,t)]. (67b) Note that this can be shown using the identity in (9). More speciп¬Ғcally, the stochastic integral SV has a vanishing ex- pectation conditioned on (Оҫ,П„ ) and we obtain that E [вҲҶV вҲ’SV|(Оҫ,П„ )] = E [вҲҶV|(Оҫ,П„ )] = 0. (68) Let us make this observation precise and at the same time state a slightly more general version of the Feynman-Kac theorem. Theorem C.1 (Feynman-Kac formula). Let gвҲҲC2(Rd,R), k вҲҲ C(RdГ—[0,T],R), and V вҲҲ C2,1(RdГ—[0,T],R) be at most polynomially growing functions. Further, letRobust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning f вҲҲ C(RdГ—[0,T],RвүҘ0) and assume that V solves the parabolic problem (вҲӮt + LвҲ’f(x,t))V(x,t) + k(x,t) = 0 (69) on (x,t) вҲҲRdГ—[0,T) with terminal condition V(x,T) = g(x), x вҲҲRd. (70) Then V(x,t) = E [вҲ« T t eвҲ’ вҲ«r t f(Xs,s)dsk(Xr,r)dr + eвҲ’ вҲ«T t f(Xs,s)dsg(XT) вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗXt = x ] , (71) where X is a strong solution to (6). Proof. The proof, whose main ingredient is It ЛҶoвҖҷs Lemma, can, for instance, be found in Karatzas & Shreve (1998, Theorem 5.7.6) and Baldi (2017, Theorem 10.5). Let us recall that our loss LFK as deп¬Ғned in (10) readily follows from the Feynman-Kac formula вҖ“ see also Beck et al. (2021). Note that a crucial point in the derivation of the Feynman-Kac formula is the elimination of the stochastic integral SV, as deп¬Ғned in (8b), via its martingale property, see (68). Ironically, as elaborated on in Section 2, it turns out that this elimination is just the reason for larger variances of estimators of LFK. Remark C.2 (Initial value problems). Note that time can be reversed and initial value problems (as opposed to termi- nal value problems as in Theorem C.1) can be formulated. As an example, we can consider the time-homogeneous case, where b,Пғ,f , and k do not depend on time. Let V вҲҲC2,1(RdГ—[0,T],R) solve the parabolic PDE (вҲӮt вҲ’L+ f(x))V(x,t) вҲ’k(x) = 0 (72) on (x,t) вҲҲRdГ—(0,T] with initial condition V(x,0) = g(x), x вҲҲRd. (73) The associated stochastic representation is then given by V(x,t) = E [вҲ« t 0 eвҲ’ вҲ«r 0 f(Xs)dsk(Xr)dr + eвҲ’ вҲ«t 0 f(Xs)dsg(Xt) вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗX0 = x ] . (74) Remark C.3 (Bounded domains). We can restrict ourselves to open, bounded domains D вҠӮRd and consider the parabolic PDE in (69) on D, adding the additional boundary condition10 V(x,t) = g(x) for (x,t) вҲҲвҲӮDГ— [0,T]. The stochastic representation then becomes V(x,t) = E [вҲ« TвҲ§T t eвҲ’ вҲ«r t f(Xs,s)dsk(Xr,r)dr + eвҲ’ вҲ«T вҲ§T t f(Xs,s)dsg(XTвҲ§T) вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗXt = x ] , where T вҲ§T := min{T,T} and T := inf {t вүҘ0 : Xt /вҲҲ D}is the п¬Ғrst exit time of the stochastic process from the domain D, for which we usually assume T < вҲһalmost surely. Likewise, we can consider the elliptic boundary value problem (LвҲ’f(x))V(x) + k(x) = 0, x вҲҲD, (75a) V(x) = g(x), x вҲҲвҲӮD, (75b) where now the solution V, the coefп¬Ғcients band Пғ in the SDE (6), as well as f and kdo not depend explicitly on time anymore, yielding the stochastic representation V(x) = E [вҲ« T 0 eвҲ’ вҲ«r 0 f(Xs)dsk(Xr)dr + eвҲ’ вҲ«T 0 f(Xs)dsg(XT) вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗX0 = x ] , (76) again with T := inf{tвүҘ0 : Xt /вҲҲD}, see e.g. Proposition 5.7.2 in Karatzas & Shreve (1998). Leveraging the above representations, our proposed meth- ods can readily be applied to a range of elliptic and parabolic PDEs on bounded domains. Note, however, that one needs to take into account the hitting times T, for instance, us- ing naive stopping criteria (N ВЁusken & Richter, 2021a) or more elaborate walk-on-the-sphere algorithms (Grohs & Herrmann, 2020). As this might obscure the comparisons between different loss functions, we focus on unbounded domains in our experiments. D. Backward Stochastic Differential Equations and Semi-Linear PDEs Backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs) have been studied extensively in the last three decades and we refer to Pardoux (1998), Pham (2009), Gobet (2016), and Zhang (2017) for good introductions to the topic. Even though in this paper we only consider linear PDEs as stated in (1) or (69), BSDEs are typically associated to nonlinear (parabolic) PDEs of the type (вҲӮt + L)V(x,t) = h(x,t,V (x,t),(ПғвҠӨвҲҮxV)(x,t)) (77) 10One can also consider boundary conditions that are different from the terminal condition in (70), see, for instance, Baldi (2017, Theorem 10.4) and Lelievre & Stoltz (2016, Proposition 6.1).Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning for (x,t) вҲҲRd Г—[0,T], a nonlinearity h : RdГ—[0,T] Г— RГ—Rd вҶ’R, and differential operator Ldeп¬Ғned as in (2). The terminal value is given by V(x,T) = g(x), (78) for a speciп¬Ғed function gвҲҲC(Rd,R). BSDEs were п¬Ғrst introduced in Bismut (1973) and their sys- tematic study began with Pardoux & Peng (1990). Loosely speaking, they can be understood as nonlinear extensions of the Feynman-Kac formula (see Pardoux (1998) and Ap- pendix C), relating the nonlinear PDE in (77) to the stochas- tic process Xs deп¬Ғned by dXs = b(Xs,s) ds+ Пғ(Xs,s) dWs, X 0 = x0. (79) The key idea is then to deп¬Ғne the processes Ys = V(Xs,s), Z s = (ПғвҠӨвҲҮxV)(Xs,s), (80) as representations of the PDE solution and its gradient, re- spectively, and apply ItЛҶoвҖҷs lemma to obtain dYs = h(Xs,s,Y s,Zs) ds+ Zs В·dWs, (81) with terminal condition YT = g(XT). Noting that the processes Y and Z are adapted11 to the п¬Ғltration gener- ated by the Brownian motion W, they should indeed be understood as backward processes and not be confused with time-reversed processes. A convenient interpretation of the relations in (80) is that solving for the processes Y and Z under the constraint (81) corresponds to determining the so- lution of the PDE in (77) (and its gradient) along a random grid which is provided by the stochastic process X deп¬Ғned in (79). Let us note that under suitable assumptions on the coefп¬Ғ- cients b,Пғ,h, and gone can prove existence and uniqueness of a solution to the BSDE system as deп¬Ғned in (79) and (81), see for instance Theorem 4.3.1 in Zhang (2017). We further note that the standard BSDE system can be gen- eralized to dXv s =(b(Xv s,s) + v(Xv s,s)) ds+ Пғ(Xv s,s)dWs, dYv s =(h(Xv s,s,Y v s ,Zv s) + v(Xv s,s)В·Zv s)ds+ Zv sВ·dWs, with Xv 0 = x, Y v T = g(Xv T). (82) In the above, v вҲҲC(RdГ—[0,T],Rd) is a suitable control vector п¬Ғeld that can be understood as pushing the forward trajectories into desired regions of the state space, noting that the relations Yv s = V(Xv s,s), Z v s = (ПғвҠӨвҲҮxV)(Xv s,s), (83) 11Intuitively, this means that the processes Y and Z must not depend on future values of the Brownian motion W. Additional Material The following Lemma details the relation of the HJB equa- tion from Section 3.3 and a linear PDE of Kolmogorov type. Lemma G.1 (Linearization of HJB equation) . Let ЛңV вҲҲ C2,1(RdГ—[0,T],R) solve the HamiltonвҖ“JacobiвҖ“Bellman equation in (45), then V = exp(вҲ’ЛңV) fulп¬Ғlls the linear PDE as stated in Theorem C.1 with k= 0 and g= exp(вҲ’Лңg), i.e. (вҲӮt + LвҲ’f(x,t))V(x,t) = 0, (x,t) вҲҲRdГ—[0,T), V(x,T) = g(x), x вҲҲRd. Subsequently, we get the representation ЛңV(x,t) = вҲ’log E [ eвҲ’W(X) вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗXt = x ] (87) with W(X) = вҲ«T t f(Xs,s)ds+ Лңg(XT). Proof. We consider the transformation V = exp(вҲ’ЛңV) and for notational convenience omit the space and time depen- dencies of ЛңV,f,b , and Пғ. We can compute LeвҲ’ЛңV = вҲ’bВ·eвҲ’ЛңVвҲҮxЛңV вҲ’1 2 dвҲ‘ i,j=1 (ПғПғвҠӨ)ijвҲӮxi ( вҲӮxjeвҲ’ЛңV ЛңV ) = вҲ’eвҲ’ЛңV ( bВ·вҲҮxЛңV + 1 2 dвҲ‘ i,j=1 (ПғПғвҠӨ)ijвҲӮxiвҲӮxj ЛңV вҲ’1 2 dвҲ‘ i,j=1 (ПғПғвҠӨ)ijвҲӮxi ЛңVвҲӮxj ЛңV ) = вҲ’eвҲ’ЛңV ( bВ·вҲҮxЛңV + 1 2 dвҲ‘ i,j=1 (ПғПғвҠӨ)ijвҲӮxiвҲӮxj ЛңV вҲ’1 2 dвҲ‘ i,j,k=1 ПғikПғjkвҲӮxi ЛңVвҲӮxj ЛңV ) = вҲ’eвҲ’ЛңV ( bВ·вҲҮxЛңV + 1 2 dвҲ‘ i,j=1 (ПғПғвҠӨ)ijвҲӮxiвҲӮxj ЛңV вҲ’1 2 dвҲ‘ k=1 ( dвҲ‘ i=1 ПғikвҲӮxi ЛңV )2 ) = вҲ’eвҲ’ЛңV ( LЛңV вҲ’1 2вҲҘПғвҠӨвҲҮxЛңVвҲҘ2 ) . The PDE in (69) therefore becomes 0 = (вҲӮt + LвҲ’f)eвҲ’ЛңV (88a) = вҲ’eвҲ’ЛңV ( (вҲӮt + L)ЛңV + f вҲ’1 2вҲҘПғвҠӨвҲҮxЛңVвҲҘ2 ) , (88b) which is equivalent to the HJB equation in (45). llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper analyzes and improves deep-learning-based solvers for high-dimensional linear Kolmogorov PDEs by comparing FeynmanвҖ“Kac (FK) and backward SDE (BSDE) variational losses. It proves that BSDE-based objectives yield lower-variance estimators and gradient estimators (zero at the optimum), explains why FK losses can be numerically non-robust, and introduces practical, more robust and memory-efficient BSDE variants. Extensive experiments on 50D heat, 50D BlackвҖ“Scholes with correlated noise, and a 10D HJB (via HopfвҖ“Cole linearization) show substantial accuracy and robustness gains.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Formulate variational objectives from SDE representations: FK loss L_FK = E[(g(X_T) вҲ’ u(Оҫ,П„))^2] and BSDE loss L_BSDE = E[(О”_u вҲ’ S_u)^2], where S_u is an ItГҙ integral acting as a control variate.
- Theoretical results: (i) V minimizes both losses; L_BSDE attains zero minimum, L_FKвҖҷs minimum equals Var(S_V) > 0; (ii) at the solution, estimator and gradient variances vanish for BSDE but not for FK; (iii) near-solution variance bound for BSDE derivatives scales as O(Оө^2/K).
- Practical BSDE variants to reduce computational burden: (a) L_detach^BSDE (differentiate only w.r.t. one occurrence of u; removes second-order derivatives), (b) L_grad^BSDE (learns the gradient via a separate network r, replacing вҲҮ_x u in S_u), and (c) efficient on-the-fly gradient accumulation (L_BSDE,eff and L_grad^BSDE,eff) that keeps memory use independent of the SDE time-steps.
- Discretization: EulerвҖ“Maruyama for the forward SDE and discretized ItГҙ integrals; Gateaux derivative analysis; automatic differentiation for gradients; neural networks (multilevel architecture with SiLU) parameterize u (and r when used).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- PDEs:
  1) Heat equation (d=50, T=1) with Пғ = ОҪ I (e.g., ОҪ=0.5; also varied), b=0, g(x)=||x||^2; Оҫ ~ Uniform([-0.5,0.5]^d), П„ ~ Uniform([0,1]).
  2) BlackвҖ“Scholes rainbow put (d=50, T=1): Пғ(x,t)=diag(ОІ_i x_i)  �afПғ with correlated noise (Cholesky of Q with Q_{i,j}=0.5(1+Оҙ_{i,j})), b(x,t)=�afb x with �afb=-0.05, ОІ_i=0.1+i/(2d), payoff g(x)=max(0, ОәвҲ’min_i x_i), Оә=5.5; Оҫ ~ Uniform([4.5,5.5]^d), П„ ~ Uniform([0,1]). Reference values via MC using closed-form X dynamics.
  3) HJB (d=10) with double-well ОЁ(x)=Оә ОЈ_i (x_i^2вҲ’1)^2, Оә=0.1, Пғ=I, f=0, terminal g(x)=exp(вҲ’О· ОЈ_i (x_iвҲ’1)^2), О·=0.04; linearized via HopfвҖ“Cole V=exp(вҲ’V) to a linear PDE.
- Training: EulerвҖ“Maruyama discretization with О”tвҲҲ[1eвҲ’2,1eвҲ’3]; Adam optimizer; multilevel NN (L=3, amplification q=3 or 5, residual connections, SiLU); batch sizes KвҲҲ{2^4,...,2^19}; GPU memory cap 8 GiB; two schedules (fixed steps: 30k with LR decay at 90% steps; or time limit 24h with mid-run decay).
- Metrics: MSE of V on unseen points (N = 10В·2^17 samples of (Оҫ,П„)); MSE of gradients (when applicable); estimator and gradient estimator standard deviations computed over 30 independent batches; memory usage vs О”t; performance vs batch size and training time.
- Baselines/variants compared: L_FK, L_BSDE, L_grad^BSDE, L_detach^BSDE, and their efficient versions (eff).
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Core theory and experiments focus on linear Kolmogorov PDEs; the nonlinear HJB is handled via HopfвҖ“Cole linearization rather than a general nonlinear framework.
- Assumes strong regularity (V вҲҲ C^{2,1}), polynomial growth, Lipschitz coefficients, and uniform ellipticity; viscosity-solution settings are only discussed at a high level.
- L_BSDE requires SDE simulation (cannot exploit closed-form SDE solutions when unavailable); discretization (EulerвҖ“Maruyama) introduces bias not fully analyzed.
- Vanilla L_BSDE and L_grad^BSDE can be memory intensive due to second-order derivatives; robustness depends on batch size and can degrade without the efficient implementations.
- L_detach^BSDE may be unstable far from the solution (stochastic integral can increase variance early in training).
- Experiments focus on unbounded domains; boundary value problems with exit times are not empirically covered.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend robust BSDE-based estimators to bounded domains and elliptic/parabolic boundary value problems (with exit times), integrating walk-on-spheres or advanced boundary handling.
- Generalize to fully nonlinear PDEs and broader BSDE classes, with theoretical variance and convergence guarantees under weaker regularity (viscosity solutions).
- Improve discretization accuracy and efficiency (higher-order SDE schemes, multilevel Monte Carlo, adaptive time-stepping) and analyze biasвҖ“variance trade-offs rigorously.
- Develop adaptive/learned control variates and controlled diffusions (generalized BSDE with control v) to further reduce variance, especially far from the solution.
- Design better second-order-free training (e.g., coupling strategies between u and gradient networks r) and investigate curriculum/detachment schedules to stabilize early training.
- Combine with PINNs/residual minimization and explore hybrid objectives; apply to applications in RL and diffusion-based generative modeling.
- Automate batch-size/step-size selection under memory constraints and study memoryвҖ“timeвҖ“accuracy trade-offs formally.

research_study_list.title: An Efficient High-dimensional Gradient Estimator for Stochastic Differential Equations
research_study_list.abstract:
Overparameterized stochastic differential equation (SDE) models have achieved
remarkable success in various complex environments, such as PDE-constrained
optimization, stochastic control and reinforcement learning, financial
engineering, and neural SDEs. These models often feature system evolution
coefficients that are parameterized by a high-dimensional vector $\theta \in
\mathbb{R}^n$, aiming to optimize expectations of the SDE, such as a value
function, through stochastic gradient ascent. Consequently, designing efficient
gradient estimators for which the computational complexity scales well with $n$
is of significant interest. This paper introduces a novel unbiased stochastic
gradient estimator--the generator gradient estimator--for which the computation
time remains stable in $n$. In addition to establishing the validity of our
methodology for general SDEs with jumps, we also perform numerical experiments
that test our estimator in linear-quadratic control problems parameterized by
high-dimensional neural networks. The results show a significant improvement in
efficiency compared to the widely used pathwise differentiation method: Our
estimator achieves near-constant computation times, increasingly outperforms
its counterpart as $n$ increases, and does so without compromising estimation
variance. These empirical findings highlight the potential of our proposed
methodology for optimizing SDEs in contemporary applications.
research_study_list.full_text: An Efficient High-Dimensional Gradient Estimator for Stochastic Differential Equations Shengbo Wang, Jose Blanchet, and Peter Glynn MS&E, Stanford University September 2024 Abstract Overparameterized stochastic differential equation (SDE) models have achieved remarkable success in various complex environments, such as PDE-constrained optimization, stochastic control and rein- forcement learning, financial engineering, and neural SDEs. These models often feature system evolution coefficients that are parameterized by a high-dimensional vector Оё вҲҲ Rn, aiming to optimize expecta- tions of the SDE, such as a value function, through stochastic gradient ascent. Consequently, designing efficient gradient estimators for which the computational complexity scales well with n is of significant interest. This paper introduces a novel unbiased stochastic gradient estimatorвҖ“the generator gradient estimatorвҖ“for which the computation time remains stable in n. In addition to establishing the validity of our methodology for general SDEs with jumps, we also perform numerical experiments that test our estimator in linear-quadratic control problems parameterized by high-dimensional neural networks. The results show a significant improvement in efficiency compared to the widely used pathwise differentiation method: Our estimator achieves near-constant computation times, increasingly outperforms its counter- part as n increases, and does so without compromising estimation variance. These empirical findings highlight the potential of our proposed methodology for optimizing SDEs in contemporary applications. 1 Introduction We consider a family of jump diffusions   Xx Оё (t, s) вҲҲ Rd : s вҲҲ [t, T] 	 that are generated by stochastic differen- tial equations (SDEs) and indexed by the initial condition x вҲҲ Rd at time s and a parameter Оё вҲҲ Оҳ вҠӮ Rn. In modern applications, the parameter Оё, encoding characteristics of an engineering model, often represents the weights of a deep neural network. This paper focuses particularly on scenarios where the dimension n of Оё is significantly greater than the dimension d of the space. This setting naturally arises in the implementation of large AI architectures in modern applications. Concretely, for each 1 вүӨ i вүӨ d, the iвҖҷth entry of Xx Оё (t, В·), denoted by Xx Оё,i(t, В·), satisfies the ItЛҶ o SDE: Xx Оё,i(t, s) = xi + Z s t ВөОё,i(r, Xx Оё (t, r))dr + Z s t dвҖІ X k=1 ПғОё,i,k(r, Xx Оё (t, rвҲ’))dBk(r) + Z s t dJОё,i (1.1) Here, {ВөОё,i : 1 вүӨ i вүӨ d} and {ПғОё,i,k : 1 вүӨ i, kвүӨ d} are the drift and volatility, respectively, satisfying suitable regularity conditions (to be discussed). For simplicity in our introductory explanations, we will assume that the jump term JОё is zero. However, incorporating this jump feature is valuable in many applied settings, and arises in various fields such as financial engineering [17], stochastic control [6], and neural SDE models [7]. Accordingly, we will fully integrate and discuss the jump components in our main results in Section 3. 1 arXiv:2407.10065v2  [math.OC]  26 Sep 2024Table 1: Comparison of the dimensions of SDEs needed to be simulated. Estimator If the volatility depends on Оё Yes No Pathwise Differentiation d + d В· n d + d В· n Generator Gradient d + d2 + 1 2 d3 d + d2 The primary objective of this paper is to develop an efficient gradient estimator, with respect to Оё, for a large class of path-dependent expectations derived from an SDE. Concretely, we consider vОё(t, x) = E "Z T t ПҒОё(s, Xx Оё (t, s))ds + gОё(Xx Оё (t, T)) # . (1.2) The value vОё(t, x) represents the expected cumulative reward running Xx Оё from time t to T. Here, ПҒОё and gОё represents the reward rate and the terminal reward, respectively. This formulation encompasses a wide range of science and engineering problems including PDE-constrained optimization [22, 20], stochastic control and reinforcement learning [8], and neural SDE models [23]. The gradient вҲҮОёvОё(t, x) = ( вҲӮОё1 vОё(t, x), . . . , вҲӮОёnvОё(t, x)) вҲҲ Rn is of significant interest in the sensitivity analysis, learning, and optimization of these models. In particular, finding an efficient unbiased estimator for вҲҮОёvОё(t, x) with low variance is essential if one is to apply stochastic gradient descent to find near optimal policies or model parameters within the parametric class Оё вҲҲ Оҳ. Under reasonable smoothness and integrability conditions, it is natural to consider the pathwise differen- tiation estimator obtained by applying infinitesimal perturbation analysis (IPA) to the sample path of Xx Оё w.r.t. the ith coordinate of Оё. For instance, if ПҒОё(В·) = ПҒ(В·) independent of Оё and g = 0, then we have a representation вҲӮОёivОё(0, x) = E пЈ® пЈ° Z T 0 dX j=1 вҲӮxj ПҒОё(t, Xx Оё (t))вҲӮОёiXx Оё,j(t)dt пЈ№ пЈ». (1.3) where вҲӮОёiXx Оё (t) is the pathwise derivative of the process Xx Оё w.r.t. Оёi. The processes {Xx Оё,j, вҲӮОёiXx Оё,j : i = 1, . . . , n; j = 1, . . . , d} satisfy a system of d + d В· n SDEs [11, Equation (3.31)], which must be jointly simulated. Therefore, to estimate the gradient (or even one component) the pathwise differentiation method requires simulating this d+dВ·n dimensional SDE. Note that the dimension is linear inn, the dimension of the parameter space. Contemporary applications of SDEs in physics-informed and data-driven environments such as deep neural SDEs and deep RL where overparameterization excel, necessitate a model with exceptionally large n that is often many orders of magnitude larger thand. Hence, simulating the SDE of dimension d+dВ·n becomes extremely resource-intensive. Motivated by these applications, we ask the following question: Can we device an efficient, unbiased, and finite variance estimator for вҲҮОёv(t, x) with a computation time insensitive to n? The answer is affirmative. Precisely, our main contribution is designing the unbiased generator gradient estimator of вҲҮОёv(t, x) that requires only simulating O(d2) SDEs when the volatility parameters ПғОё do not depend on Оё and O(d3) SDEs in the general setting, as summarized in Table 1. We remark that in addition to pathwise differentiation, likelihood ratio-based estimators are also popular for sensitivity analysis in SDEs; see e.g. Yang and Kushner [26]. However, typically they are only applicable if ПғОё is independent of Оё and under more restrictive jump structures. When applicable, likelihood ratio- based estimators could be appealing alternatives as they introduce a change of measure that represents the 2derivatives as a functional of the d-dimensional processes Xx Оё . Nevertheless, these estimators typically have significantly higher variance. Finally, we apply our estimator to linear-quadratic control problems and test its performance in optimizing neural-network-parameterized controls. As we increase the number of network parameters n, the results in Figure 1a and Table 2 highlight a substantial improvement in computational efficiency, as compared to the pathwise differentiation method, while still maintaining competitive variance levels. Furthermore, Figure 1a confirms that the computation time of our estimator is robust to increases in n, even in extremely high-dimensional scenarios with n approaching 108. 1.1 Literature Review Gradient Estimation: Gradient estimation, particularly likelihood ratios and IPA methods, is crucial in sensitivity analysis. Foundational works in the late 20th century by Glynn [5, 4] and further adaptations to the SDE setting [26, 3] highlight these developments. IPA has evolved to apply stochastic flow techniques to SDEs, both with and without reflecting boundaries [23, 12, 16, 24, 14]. Applications of Gradient Estimators: Gradient estimators are widely used in stochastic control and reinforcement learning (RL) models. Policy gradient methods in discrete-time RL, including REINFORCE and deep policy gradient approaches, are notable applications [25, 13, 21]. Continuous-time RL have been explored using policy gradients in settings with continuous diffusion dynamics [8]. Jump diffusions are important models in financial engineering and stochastic control [17, 15, 9, 6]. Gradient estimators can also be used for optimizing these models. Neural SDE models are modern computational frameworks that model the dynamics of stochastic systems using a neural-network-parameterized SDE. Chen et al. [1], Tzen and Raginsky [23], Kidger [10] focus on the continuous case, while Jia and Benson [7] consider ODEs modulated by compound Poisson jumps. Efficient gradient estimators in high-dimensional settings are crucial for fitting these SDE models. Diffusion with Jumps and Stochastic Flow: The main technical tools for this paper are SDEs with jumps and stochastic flows. Our references are Protter [19], Kunita [11], Гҳksendal and Sulem [18]. 1.2 Remarks on Paper Organizations The paper is structured as follows: Section 2 outlines the core concepts of our estimator in a zero-jump setting, focusing on intuitive understanding over technical detail. In Section 3, we introduce the SDE model with jumps and provide a set of sufficient conditions that rigorously support the earlier insights. While more general and complex assumptions exist that lead to similar conclusions, these are presented in Appendix A to align with the concise format of the conference proceedings. The paper concludes with Section 4, where we conduct numerical experiments on neural-network-parameterized linear-quadratic control problems, demonstrating the effectiveness of our methodology. 2 Key Methodological Insights In this section, we motivate our proposed generator gradient estimator by first providing a non-rigorous derivation. We assume the SDE model (1.1) where the jumps JОё вүЎ 0 and Оҳ вҠӮ Rn is a bounded open neighbourhood of the origin. W.l.o.g, we are interested in estimating the gradient at Оё = 0 вҲҲ Оҳ and t = 0; i.e. вҲҮОёv0(0, x) = вҲҮОёvОё(0, x)|Оё=0. To simplify notation, we denote Xx Оё (t) := Xx Оё (0, t) and Xx Оё (tвҲ’) := Xx Оё (0, tвҲ’), and the function aОё,i,j(t, x) := 1 2 dвҖІ X k=1 ПғОё,i,k(t, x)ПғОё,j,k(t, x). (2.1) 3Also, for function vОё(t, x), we use вҲӮivОё(t, x) to denote the space derivative вҲӮiv вҲӮx    Оё,t,x and вҲҮ the space gradient. Similarly, вҲӮОёi and вҲҮОё denotes the Оё partials. Under sufficient regularity conditions, by the Feynman-Kac formula, vОё in (1.2) is the solution to the partial differential equation (PDE) вҲӮtvОё + LОёvОё + ПҒОё = 0, v Оё(T, В·) = gОё (2.2) for all Оё вҲҲ Оҳ, where LОё is the generator of Xx Оё given by LОёf(t, x) := dX i=1 ВөОё,i(t, x)вҲӮif(t, x) + dX i,j=1 aОё,i,j(t, x)вҲӮiвҲӮjf(t, x) for f that is twice differentiable in x. Assuming enough smoothness, we formally differentiate the PDE (2.2) w.r.t. Оёi and then set Оё = 0 to obtain вҲӮtвҲӮОёiv0 + L0вҲӮОёiv0 + (вҲӮОёiL0v0 + вҲӮОёiПҒ0) = 0, вҲӮ Оёiv0(T, В·) = вҲӮОёig0. (2.3) Here, the operator вҲӮОёiL0 is defined as вҲӮОёiL0f(t, x) := dX j=1 вҲӮОёiВө0,j(t, x)вҲӮjf(t, x) + dX j,l=1 вҲӮОёia0,j,l(t, x)вҲӮjвҲӮlf(t, x). (2.4) Interpreted as the derivative of LОё w.r.t. Оё at 0, this inspires the name вҖқgenerator gradientвҖқ method. Next, define u0 = вҲӮОёiv0. Treating вҲӮОёiL0v0 as fixed, we observe that u0 solves the PDE (2.3) which is of the form (2.2). Hence, applying the Feynman-Kac formula again to вҲӮОёiv0(0, x) = u0(0, x) yields the following expectation representation вҲӮОёiv0(0, x) = E "Z T 0 вҲӮОёiL0v0(t, Xx 0 (t)) + вҲӮОёiПҒ0(t, Xx 0 (t))dt + вҲӮОёig0(Xx 0 (T)) # . (2.5) Note that the expression inside the expectation contains only space derivatives (due to вҲӮОёiL0) of the value function v0 but not the Оё derivatives. In particular, if we can estimate the gradient вҲҮv0(t, x) and the Hessian matrix H[v0](t, x) := {вҲӮiвҲӮjv0(t, x) : 1 вүӨ i, jвүӨ d} efficiently, then the representation in (2.5) will lead to a natural estimator of вҲӮОёiv0(0, x). To estimateвҲҮv0(t, x) and H[v0](t, x), we employ the pathwise differentiation estimator from (1.3). Specif- ically, under enough regularity conditions, we can interchange the derivatives and integration вҲҮv0(t, x)вҠӨ = EZ(t, x)вҠӨ := E "Z T t вҲҮПҒвҠӨ 0 вҲҮXx 0 (t, r)dr + вҲҮgвҠӨ 0 вҲҮXx 0 (t, T) # , H[v0](t, x) = EH(t, x) := E  вҲҮXx 0 (t, T)вҠӨH[g0]вҲҮXx 0 (t, T) +  вҲҮg0, H[Xx 0,В·](t, T)  + E "Z T t вҲҮXx 0 (t, r)вҠӨH[ПҒ0]вҲҮXx 0 (t, r) +  вҲҮПҒ0, H[Xx 0,В·](t, r)  dr # . (2.6) Here, we write вҲҮXx 0 :=   вҲӮaXx 0,i : i, a= 1, . . . d 	 and H[Xx 0 ] :=   вҲӮbвҲӮaXx 0,i : i, a, b= 1, . . . d 	 . The notation вҲҮh, H[Xx 0,В·]  := Pd a=1 вҲӮahH[Xx 0,a] вҲҲ RdГ—d for h = ПҒ0, g0. The dependence of ПҒ0, g0 on time and the state process is hidden. 4We estimate these expectations by simulating the SDEs for {Xx 0 , вҲҮXx 0 , H[Xx 0 ]} given by (1.1) and вҲӮaXx 0,i = Оҙi,a + Z s t dX l=1 вҲӮlВө0,iвҲӮaXx 0,ldr + Z s t dX l=1 dвҖІ X k=1 вҲӮlПғ0,i,kвҲӮaXx 0,ldBk(r) вҲӮbвҲӮaXx 0,i = Z s t dX l=1 " вҲӮlВө0,iвҲӮbвҲӮaXx 0,l + dX m=1 вҲӮmвҲӮlВө0,iвҲӮaXx 0,lвҲӮbXx 0,m # dr + Z s t dвҖІ X k=1 dX l=1 " вҲӮlПғ0,i,kвҲӮbвҲӮaXx 0,l + dX m=1 вҲӮmвҲӮlПғ0,i,kвҲӮaXx 0,lвҲӮbXx 0,m # dBk(r) (2.7) where the dependence of the coefficients onr, Xx 0 (t, rвҲ’), and z, as well as the dependence ofXx 0 , вҲӮaXx 0 , вҲӮaвҲӮbXx 0 on (t, s), (t, rвҲ’) are suppressed. The dimension of these SDEs is d + d2 + 1 2 d3, where the 1 2 comes from the Hessian being symmetric. Moreover, when the volatility Пғ is independent of Оё, our method only necessitates estimating вҲҮv0. This reduction leads to simulating the SDEs for {Xx 0 , вҲҮXx 0 } of dimension only d + d2. Assuming sufficient integrability, the unbiasedness of Z implies E "Z T 0 вҲӮОёk ВөвҠӨ 0 Z(t, Xx 0 (0, t))dt # = E "Z T 0 вҲӮОёk ВөвҠӨ 0 вҲҮv0(t, Xx 0 (0, t))dt # (2.8) which we will elaborate upon in (A.1). The same holds for the H(t, x) process as well. Therefore, we can replace the derivatives вҲҮv0 with Z and H[v0] with H in (2.5) without changing the expectation. Also note that producing a sample of Z(t, x) requires simulating the solution to SDEs (1.1) and (2.7) within time [ t, T] starting from x, I,0. So, it is not very efficient to compute Z(t, Xx 0 (0, t)) for every t; a similar issue exists for H as well. This can be addressed by randomizing the integral. With these considerations, we proceed to define the generator gradient estimator. First, let вҲҮОёL0V0(t, x) be defined by replacing вҲӮiv(t, x) with Zi(t, x) and вҲӮjвҲӮiv with Hi,j(t, x) in the definition (2.4) of вҲӮОёiL0v0(t, x). Then, define the generator gradient estimator as D(x) := TвҲҮОёL0V0(П„, Xx 0 (0, П„)) + Z T 0 вҲҮОёПҒ0(t, Xx 0 (t))dt + вҲҮОёg0(Xx 0 (T)). (2.9) where П„ вҲј Unif[0, T] is sampled independently. We can also randomize the integral of вҲҮОёПҒ0(t, Xx Оё (t)) if the gradient is hard to compute. With the derivation in (2.8), it is easy to see that ED(x) = вҲҮОёv0(0, x) is unbiased. In summary, due to the observation in (2.5), we are able to вҖқmoveвҖқ the estimation of вҲҮОёv0 onto that of вҲҮv0 and H[v0]. This results in a significant reduction in the dimension of the SDEs we need to simulate, underlying the remarkable efficiency of our methodology, especially when the dimension n of Оё significantly exceeds d. 3 Jump Diffusions and the Generator Gradient Estimator In this section, we rigorously formulate a jump diffusion process driven by an SDE. We extend the gener- ator gradient estimator to this context by first rigorously establishing an expectation representation of the derivative as in (2.5). Then, we also validate the representation (2.6) using the jump version of (2.7). These lead to our generator gradient estimator in the jump diffusion context. To improve the clarity of the paper (at a cost of generalizability), we will state a set of sufficient assumptions that are easy to verify. However, we will state and prove our theorems using a set of more general assumptions in the Appendix A. We consider jump diffusions on the canonical probability space of c` adl` ag functions [0, T] вҶ’ Rd generated 5by SDEs of the form (1.1) where the jump term is given by Xx Оё,i(t, s) = xi + Z s t ВөОё,i(r, Xx Оё (t, r))dr + Z s t dвҖІ X k=1 ПғОё,i,k(r, Xx Оё (t, rвҲ’))dBk(r) + Z s t Z RdвҖІ 0 ПҮОё,i(t, Xx Оё (s, rвҲ’), z)d eN(dr, dz). (3.1) In this expression, B is a standard Brownian motion inRdвҖІ ; eN is a compensated Poisson random measure with intensity measure dtГ—ОҪ(dz) with ОҪ a LВҙ evy measure on (RdвҖІ 0 := RdвҖІ \{0}, B(RdвҖІ 0 )), i.e. R RdвҖІ 0 1вҲ§|z|2ОҪ(dz) < вҲһ; the вҲ’r notation in Xx Оё (t, rвҲ’) denotes the left limit; and the stochastic integrations are ItЛҶ o integrals. Here, for a vector/matrix/tensor v вҲҲ Rd1Г—d2Г—d3 , we denote |v|2 := P i,j,k |vi,j,k|2. We further define Оі(z) = |z| вҲ§1 and Вө(dz) = Оі(z)2ОҪ(dz). Then Вө is a finite measure on ( RdвҖІ 0 , B(RdвҖІ 0 )). Also, since we are interested in the gradient at Оё = 0, we can assume w.l.o.g. that Оҳ is a bounded open neighbourhood of 0. The generator of this system of SDEs is LОё := LC Оё + LJ Оё , where LC Оё f(t, x) = dX i=1 ВөОё,i(t, x)вҲӮif(t, x) + dX i,j=1 aОё,i,j(t, x)вҲӮiвҲӮjf(t, x) LJ Оё f(t, x) = Z RdвҖІ 0 " f(t, x+ ПҮОё(t, x, z)) вҲ’ f(t, x) вҲ’ dX i=1 ПҮОё,i(t, x, z)вҲӮif(t, x) # ОҪ(dz). (3.2) for f вҲҲ C1,2([0, T], Rd). We remark that for open subsets W, X, the space Ci,j,k([0, T], W, X) represents the set of functions f on [0, T]Г—WГ—X that has continuous mixed partial derivatives вҲӮa t вҲӮb wвҲӮc xf on (0, T)Г—WГ—X for every a вүӨ i, bвүӨ j, cвүӨ k. Moreover, these mixed partial derivatives have continuous extensions on [0, T] Г— W Г— X. 3.1 Probabilistic Representation of the Gradient In this section, we rigorously establish the probabilistic representation of the gradient вҲҮОёv0(0, x) as outlined in equation (2.5). Our approach leverages the continuous dependence of Оё вҶ’ Xx Оё of the solutions to (3.1) in a neighbourhood of 0, given sufficient regularity conditions. This behavior extends the properties associated with stochastic flows, as explained in the work by Kunita [11]. Recall that Оҳ is a bounded neighbourhood of 0 вҲҲ Rn. Let X вҲҲ Rd be the controlled process, given by the solution to the SDE Xx(t) = x + Z t 0 AXx(s) + BU (t)ds + Z t 0 CdB(s), where B(t) вҲҲ RdвҖІ is a standard Brownian motion, U(t) вҲҲ Rm is the control process that is adapted to the filtration generated by X, A вҲҲ RdГ—d, BвҲҲ RdГ—m, CвҲҲ RdГ—dвҖІ are non-random matrices. The objective is to choose an admissible control U(t) that minimizes the quadratic loss E "Z T 0 Xx(t)вҠӨQXx(t) + U(t)вҠӨRU(t)dt + Xx(T)вҠӨQT Xx(T) # where Q, QT вҲҲ RdГ—d and R вҲҲ RmГ—m are non-random matrices. In various applications of interests, the admissible control U(t) is a parameterized function of time and state U(t) = uОё(t, Xx Оё (t)) where the state process under control uОё is denoted by Xx Оё . The dimension of Оё could potentially be very highвҖ”e.g. when uОё is a neural network. To achieve an optimized loss in this over-parameterized setting, one common approach is to run gradient descent. Hence, an efficient gradient estimator that scales well with the dimension n of Оё is highly desirable. We compare the performance of the proposed generator gradient estimator and the pathwise differen- tiation estimator. In this context, these estimators take the following form. We deploy these estimators in an environment where the state variablex вҲҲ R4 represents the x-y positions and velocities of a point mass on a 2D plane. The controller applies a force to this mass. The cost function is designed to encourage the controller to swiftly move the point mass to the origin with minimal force. The force is state-time-dependent and parameterized through a 4-layer fully connected neural network with variable width. All computation times are recorded from a Tesla V100 GPU. Further details about the setup of our numerical experiments can be found in Appendix F.2. In Figure 1a, we present a comparison of the average runtime for computing a single sample of the generator gradient and the pathwise differentiation estimators D(x), eD(x) вҲҲ Rn, across increasing values of n the dimension of Оё. Our findings indicate that the generator gradient estimator not only outperforms the widely used pathwise differentiation method across all tested values of n but also surpasses it by more than an order of magnitude for larger values of n. Additionally, the computation time for our estimator shows remarkable stability with respect to increases in n, displaying only a slight uptrend when n вүі 107. Figure 1b confirms that, at n = 102, the estimated values by the two estimators are very similar with high confidence. This confirms that our estimator is consistently estimating the gradient вҲҮОёvОё(0, x). Further analysis of the SEs for each gradient coordinate is conducted and displayed in Figure 2 in Appendix F.2, highlighting similar histogram shapes and observable reduction in large values of SEs of our estimator. 5 Concluding Remarks The theoretical results in this paper have the limitation of requiring second-order continuous differentiability and uniform boundedness of the space derivatives of the parameters of the underlying jump diffusion. These strong conditions, which are standard in the literature of stochastic flows (cf. [19, 11]) to guarantee global existence and uniqueness of the derivative processes in (3.5), are necessary to achieve the generality of the results presented in this paper. However, our generator gradient estimator often works even when coefficients are not continuously dif- ferentiable. This is true if the generator and rewards gradients are defined almost everywhere, and the derivative processes in (3.5), with almost everywhere derivatives of the SDE parameters, exist for every t вҲҲ [0, T] and satisfy some integrability conditions. Examples include neural networks parameterized stochas- tic control with ReLU activation functions, heavy-traffic limits of controlled multi-server queues, and the CoxвҖ“IngersollвҖ“Ross (CIR) model. For these models, the existence and integrability of the derivative pro- cesses can be checked on a case-by-case basis, allowing the consistency and unbiasedness of the generator gradient estimator to be established. We confirm this by numerically investigating the CIR process and an SDE with ReLU drift in Appendix G. In particular, we allow discontinuities in time of the SDE coefficients. This flexibility is especially relevant in applications in data-driven decision-making environments where non-homogeneous SDE models with estimated drift, volatility, and jump parameters could be piece-wise constant. Moreover, we also relax the differentiability of the coefficients in the space variable to Lipschitz continuity. We will state the new set of assumptions, and establish a generalized version of Theorem 1 as in Theorem 1вҖҷ We proceed by presenting a critical theorem, along with the necessary assumptions, that forms the foundation of our probabilistic representation in Theorem 1вҖҷ. F Supplementary Materials for Section 4 F.1 Calculating the Estimators The Generator Gradient Estimator: We compute вҲӮiv(t, x) = E Z T t вҲӮxiПҒОё(s, Xx Оё (t, s))ds + вҲӮxig(Xx Оё (t, T)) = E Z T t uОё(s, Xx Оё (t, s))вҠӨ(R + RвҠӨ)вҲҮuОё(s, Xx Оё (t, s))вҲӮiXx Оё (t, s)ds + Z T t Xx Оё (t, s)вҠӨ(Q + QвҠӨ)вҲӮiXx Оё (t, s)ds + Xx Оё (t, T)вҠӨ(QT + QвҠӨ T )вҲӮiXx Оё (t, T). 38Here вҲӮiXx Оё (t, s) is a column vector. So, replacing it by the Jacobian will yield a row vector. Following the definition of Z in (2.6), we define Z(t, x)вҠӨ := Z T t uОё(s, Xx Оё (t, s))(R + RвҠӨ)вҲҮuОё(s, Xx Оё (t, s))вҲҮXx Оё (t, s)ds + Z T t Xx Оё (t, s)вҠӨ(Q + QвҠӨ)вҲҮXx Оё (t, s)ds + Xx Оё (t, T)вҠӨ(QT + QвҠӨ T )вҲҮXx Оё (t, T). (F.1) Here, the derivative process вҲҮXx Оё satisfies the following ODE with random coefficients: вҲӮiXx Оё (t, s) = ei + Z s t (A + BвҲҮuОё(r, Xx Оё (t, r)))вҲӮiXx Оё (t, r)dr; or in matrix form: вҲҮXx Оё (t, s) = I + Z s t (A + BвҲҮuОё(r, Xx Оё (t, r)))вҲҮXx Оё (t, r)dr. Therefore, in this setting, our generator gradient estimator in (2.9) is Di(x) = T вҲӮОёiuОё(П„, Xx Оё (П„))вҠӨBвҠӨZ(П„, Xx Оё (П„)) + T uОё(П„, Xx Оё (П„))вҠӨ(R + RвҠӨ)вҲӮОёiuОё(П„, Xx Оё (П„)) (F.2) where Z is given by (F.1). As explained in (2.9), we also randomize the integral corresponding to the gradient of the reward rate вҲҮОёПҒ0. The Pathwise Differentiation Estimator: From (1.3), we construct the following IPA estimator that randomizes the time integral eDi(x) = T uОё(П„, Xx Оё (П„))(R + RвҠӨ)вҲҮuОё(П„, Xx Оё (П„))вҲӮОёiXx Оё (П„) + T Xx Оё (П„)вҠӨ(Q + QвҠӨ)вҲӮОёiXx Оё (П„) + T uОё(П„, Xx Оё (П„))вҠӨ(R + RвҠӨ)вҲӮОёiuОё(П„, Xx Оё (П„)) + Xx Оё (T)вҠӨ(QT + QвҠӨ T )вҲӮОёiXx Оё (T). Here, the pathwise derivatives вҲӮОёiXx Оё (t) is the solution the following ODE with random coefficient: вҲӮОёiXx Оё (t) = Z t 0 (A + BвҲҮuОё(s, Xx Оё (s)))вҲӮОёiXx Оё (s) + BвҲӮОёiuОё(s, Xx Оё (s))ds. (F.3) F.2 Numerical Experimentation Details We conducted the computation time and variance comparison for both estimators using PyTorch. The computation time data was generated on a system equipped with a PCIE version of Nvidia Tesla V100 GPU, featuring 32GB of VRAM. Additionally, the system includes a 2-core CPU and 16GB of RAM, which are used to format and store data. The primary computational tasks are handled by the GPU. This aligns with the superior variance performance of our estimator demonstrated in Table 2. In particular, the generator gradient estimator has tighter confidence intervals in Figure 1b.
