llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces the generator gradient estimator, an unbiased stochastic gradient estimator for expectations of SDEs (including jump diffusions) whose computational cost is essentially independent of the parameter dimension n. It replaces pathwise differentiation w.r.t. Оё with a generator-based representation requiring only O(d^2) simulated SDE dimensions when Пғ does not depend on Оё and O(d^3) otherwise. Provides rigorous probabilistic representations for вҲҮОёv using the SDE generator and for spatial derivatives of v via stochastic flows; proves unbiasedness and polynomial variance bounds under general conditions. Demonstrates large empirical speedups over pathwise differentiation in neural-network-parameterized linearвҖ“quadratic control without increased variance, even for n up to ~1e7. Shows robustness on non-smooth models (CIR, ReLU drift) via comparisons to finite-difference baselines.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: 1) PDE/PIDE-based derivation: Use FeynmanвҖ“Kac to show vОё solves вҲӮtvОё+LОёvОё+ПҒОё=0, differentiate in Оё to obtain вҲӮtвҲӮОёiv0+L0вҲӮОёiv0+ (вҲӮОёiL0 v0+вҲӮОёiПҒОё)=0 and the expectation representation вҲӮОёiv0(0,x)=E[вҲ«0^T(вҲӮОёiL0 v0+вҲӮОёiПҒОё)dt+вҲӮОёig0(X0(T))]. Extend LОё to include jump operator. 2) Replace unknown spatial derivatives of v0 by Monte Carlo estimators: obtain probabilistic representations for вҲҮv0 and H[v0] using pathwise derivatives of the stochastic flow with respect to x, simulating SDEs for {X,вҲҮX,H[X]}. 3) Randomized-time estimator: define D(x)=TВ·вҲҮОёL0V0(П„,X0(П„)) + вҲ«0^TвҲҮОёПҒ0(t,X0(t))dt + вҲҮОёg0(X0(T)), with П„~Unif[0,T], where V0 substitutes Zi and Hi,j for вҲӮiv0 and вҲӮiвҲӮjv0. 4) Complexity: simulate d+d^2 SDE dimensions when Пғ independent of Оё; otherwise d+d^2+ВҪd^3 (due to Hessian). 5) Theory: under smoothness, Lipschitz, and growth assumptions on coefficients, rewards, and solutions, prove unbiasedness and Var(D(x)) вүӨ C(|x|+1)^{2m+4}. Provide generalized assumptions allowing piecewise time dependence and weaker regularity. 6) Extension to jumps: formulate generator parts вҲҮОёLC and вҲҮОёLJ, show corresponding expectation identities and derivative flow SDEs with compensated Poisson jumps.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: - Problem: Continuous-time linearвҖ“quadratic control with 4D state (2D position/velocity), Brownian noise, quadratic costs, and controls uОё(t,x) parameterized by a 4-layer fully connected neural network with varying width (n up to ~1.15e7). - Baselines: Compare generator gradient (GG) vs pathwise differentiation (PD). - Metrics: Per-sample runtime and estimation variance (standard errors). For variance, run 400 i.i.d. replications and report average SE per coordinate and SE ratios (GG/PD). - Implementation: PyTorch on Nvidia Tesla V100 GPU (32GB). Randomize time integrals via П„~Unif[0,T]. - Results: GG shows near-constant runtime as n grows and is >10Г— faster at large n; SEs are comparable or lower than PD across n. - Additional validation (appendix): CIR model and SDE with ReLU drift; compare GG to finite-difference (FD) with h=0.05 using 10^6 samples and Euler discretization; GG aligns with FD when differentiability holds effectively; variance increases for CIR when Оё<1/2 (possible hitting zero).
llm_extracted_info.limitations: - Requires strong regularity: classical C1,2 solutions to PIDE, bounded spatial derivatives of coefficients, Lipschitz and growth conditions for coefficients and rewards, and existence/uniqueness of derivative processes (including second-order). - When Пғ depends on Оё, the method needs Hessian estimation and simulating up to O(d^3) SDE dimensions, which may be costly for large d. - Assumptions may be violated in common non-smooth settings (e.g., ReLU networks, square-root diffusions); while numerically robust, theory does not cover all such cases. - Variance bounds are polynomial in state magnitude; can be large for certain dynamics (e.g., CIR near zero). - Requires access to gradients of coefficients and reward w.r.t. Оё and to simulate derivative flows in x; implementation complexity can be higher than standard PD in simple low-d problems. - No benchmarking on real-world datasets; experiments limited to synthetic control tasks.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Theoretical extensions to weaker regularity and non-smooth models (e.g., ReLU networks, square-root diffusions), including hitting-boundary behaviors and reflecting/absorbing boundaries. - Variance reduction: control variates, antithetics, importance sampling, multilevel Monte Carlo, or learned baselines tailored to generator terms. - Alternative spatial-derivative estimators (e.g., adjoint methods, PDE solvers, neural PDE surrogates) to reduce O(d^3) cost when Пғ depends on Оё. - Extensions to partial observation, path-dependent functionals, and constraints; integration with continuous-time RL algorithms (actorвҖ“critic, policy gradient with critics). - Scalability in high state dimension d: exploiting structure/sparsity, low-rank Hessians, Krylov or randomized second-order approximations, efficient parallel implementations. - Robustness under jump models with heavy tails or state-dependent LГ©vy measures; relaxing finite-measure conditions; adaptive time randomization. - Theoretical analysis of bias/variance under discretization (time stepping) and guidelines for step-size selection; adaptive or MLMC time-discretization schemes. - Applications to neural SDE training, financial engineering with jumps, and PDE-constrained optimization with large overparameterized models.

research_study_list.title: Kernel Quadrature with Randomly Pivoted Cholesky
research_study_list.abstract:
This paper presents new quadrature rules for functions in a reproducing
kernel Hilbert space using nodes drawn by a sampling algorithm known as
randomly pivoted Cholesky. The resulting computational procedure compares
favorably to previous kernel quadrature methods, which either achieve low
accuracy or require solving a computationally challenging sampling problem.
Theoretical and numerical results show that randomly pivoted Cholesky is fast
and achieves comparable quadrature error rates to more computationally
expensive quadrature schemes based on continuous volume sampling, thinning, and
recombination. Randomly pivoted Cholesky is easily adapted to complicated
geometries with arbitrary kernels, unlocking new potential for kernel
quadrature.
research_study_list.full_text: Kernel Quadrature with Randomly Pivoted Cholesky Ethan N. Epperly and Elvira MorenoвҲ— Department of Computing and Mathematical Sciences California Institute of Technology Pasadena, CA 91125 {eepperly,emoreno2}@caltech.edu Abstract This paper presents new quadrature rules for functions in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space using nodes drawn by a sampling algorithm known as randomly pivoted Cholesky. The resulting computational procedure compares favorably to previous kernel quadrature methods, which either achieve low accuracy or require solving a computationally challenging sampling problem. Theoretical and numerical results show that randomly pivoted Cholesky is fast and achieves comparable quadrature error rates to more computationally expensive quadrature schemes based on continuous volume sampling, thinning, and recombination. Randomly pivoted Cholesky is easily adapted to complicated geometries with arbitrary kernels, unlocking new potential for kernel quadrature. 1 Introduction Quadrature is one of the fundamental problems in computational mathematics, with applications in Bayesian statistics [35], probabilistic ODE solvers [27], reinforcement learning [32], and model- based machine learning [30]. The task is to approximate an integral of a function f by the weighted sum of fвҖҷs values at judiciously chosen quadrature points s1, . . . , sn: Z X f(x)g(x) dВө(x) вүҲ nX i=1 wif(si). (1) Here, and throughout, X denotes a topological space equipped with a Borel measure Вө, and g вҲҲ L2(Вө) denotes a square-integrable function. The goal of kernel quadrature is to select quadrature weights w1, . . . , wn вҲҲ R and nodes s1, . . . , sn вҲҲ X which minimize the error in the approximation (1) for all f drawn from a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) H of candidate functions. The ideal kernel quadrature scheme would satisfy three properties: 1. Spectral accuracy. The error of the approximation (1) decreases at a rate governed by the eigenvalues of the reproducing kernel k of H, with rapidly decaying eigenvalues guarantee- ing rapidly decaying quadrature error. 2. Efficiency. The nodes s1, . . . , sn and weights w1, . . . , wn can be computed by an algorithm which is efficient in both theory and practice. вҲ—ENE acknowledges support by NSF FRG 1952777, Carver Mead New Horizons Fund, and the U.S. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Advanced Scientific Computing Research, Department of Energy Computational Science Graduate Fellowship under Award Number DE-SC0021110. EM was supported in part by Air Force Office of Scientific Research grant FA9550-22-1-0225. 37th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2023). arXiv:2306.03955v3  [math.NA]  7 Dec 2023The first two goals may be more easily achieved if one has access to a Mercer decomposition k(x, y) = вҲһX i=1 О»iei(x)ei(y), (2) where e1, e2, . . .form an orthonormal basis of L2(Вө) and the eigenvalues О»1 вүҘ О»2 вүҘ В·В·В· are decreasing. Fortunately, the Mercer decomposition is known analytically for many RKHSвҖҷs H on simple sets X such as boxes [0, 1]d. However, such a decomposition is hard to compute for general kernels k and domains X, leading to the third of our desiderata: 3. Mercer-free. The quadrature scheme can be efficiently implemented without access to an explicit Mercer decomposition (2). Despite significant progress in the probabilistic and kernel quadrature literature (see, e.g., [3вҖ“8, 17, 18, 21вҖ“23, 26, 41]), the search for a kernel quadrature scheme meeting all three criteria remains ongoing. Contributions. We present a new kernel quadrature method based on therandomly pivoted Cholesky (RPC HOLESKY ) sampling algorithm that achieves all three of the goals. Our main contributions are 1. Generalizing RPC HOLESKY sampling (introduced in [10] for finite kernel matrices) to the continuum setting and demonstrating its effectiveness for kernel quadrature. 2. Establishing theoretical results (see theorem 1) which show that RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature achieves near-optimal quadrature error rates. 3. Developing efficient rejection sampling implementations (see algorithms 2 and 4) of RP- CHOLESKY in the continuous setting, allowing kernel quadrature to be applied to general spaces X, measures Вө, and kernels k with ease. The remainder of this introduction will sketch our proposal for RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature. A comparison with existing kernel quadrature approaches appears in section 2. Randomly pivoted Cholesky. Let H be an RKHS with a kernel k that is integrable on the diagonal Z X k(x, x) dВө(x) < +вҲһ. (3) RPC HOLESKY uses the kernel diagonal k(x, x) as a sampling distribution to pick quadrature nodes. The first node s1 is chosen to be a sample from the distribution k(x, x) dВө(x), properly normalized: s1 вҲј k(x, x) dВө(x)R X k(x, x) dВө(x). Having selected s1, we remove its influence on the kernel, updating the entire kernel function: kвҖІ(x, y) = k(x, y) вҲ’ k(x, s1)k(s1, y) k(s1, s1) . (4) Linear algebraically, the update (4) can be interpreted as Gaussian elimination, eliminating вҖңrowвҖқ and вҖңcolumnвҖқ s1 from the вҖңinfinite matrixвҖқ k. Probabilistically, if we interpret k as the covariance function of a Gaussian process, the update (4) represents conditioning on the value of the process at s1. We use the updated kernel kвҖІ to select the next quadrature node: s2 вҲј kвҖІ(x, x) dВө(x)R X kвҖІ(x, x) dВө(x), whose influence is then subtracted off kвҖІ as in (4). RPC HOLESKY continues along these lines until n nodes have been selected. The resulting algorithm is shown in algorithm 1. Having chosen the nodes s1, . . . , sn, our choice of weights w1, . . . , wn is standard and is discussed in section 3.2. RPC HOLESKY sampling is more flexible than many kernel quadrature methods, easily adapting to general spaces X, measures Вө, and kernels k. To demonstrate this flexibility, we apply RPC HOLESKY to the region X in fig. 1a equipped with the MatГ©rn 5/2-kernel with bandwidth 2 and the measure dВө(x, y) = (x2 + y2) dx dy. 2Algorithm 1 RPC HOLESKY : unoptimized implementation Input: Kernel k : X Г— X вҶ’ R and number of quadrature points n Output: Quadrature points s1, . . . , sn 1: for i = 1, 2, . . . , ndo 2: Sample si from the probability measure k(x, x) dВө(x)/ R X k(x, x) dВө(x) 3: Update kernel k вҶҗ k вҲ’ k(В·, si)k(si, si)вҲ’1k(si, В·) 4: end for A set of n = 20 quadrature nodes produced by RPC HOLESKY sampling (using algorithm 2) is shown in fig. 1a. The cyanвҖ“pink shading shows the diagonal of the kernel after updating for the selected nodes. We see that near the selected nodes, the updated kernel is very small, meaning that future steps of the algorithm will avoid choosing nodes in those regions. Nodes far from any currently selected nodes have a much larger kernel value, making them more likely to be chosen in future RPC HOLESKY iterations. (a)  (b) Figure 1: RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature on oddly shaped region. Left: Black dots show 20 points selected by RPC HOLESKY on a crescent-shaped region. Shading shows kernel val- ues k((x, y), (x, y)) after removing influence of selected points. Right: Mean relative error for RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature, iid kernel quadrature, and Monte Carlo quadrature for f(x, y) = sin(x) exp(y) with 100 trials. Shaded regions show 10%/90% quantiles. The quadrature error for f(x, y) = sin( x) exp(y), g вүЎ 1, and different numbers n of quadrature nodes for RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature, kernel quadrature with nodes drawn iid from Вө/Вө(X), and Monte Carlo quadrature are shown in fig. 1b. Other spectrally accurate kernel quadrature methods would be difficult to implement in this setting because they would require an explicit Mercer decomposition (projection DPPs and leverage scores) or an expensive sampling procedure (continuous volume sampling). A comparison of RPC HOLESKY with more kernel quadrature methods on a benchmark problem is provided in fig. 2. 2 Related work Here, we overview past work on kernel quadrature and discuss the history ofRPC HOLESKY sampling. 2.1 Kernel quadrature The goal of kernel quadrature is to provide a systematic means for designing quadrature rules on RKHSвҖҷs. Relevant points of comparison are Monte Carlo [36] and quasi-Monte Carlo [14] methods, which have O(1/вҲҡn) and O(polylog(n)/n) convergence rates, respectively. The literature on probabilistic and kernel approaches to quadrature is vast, so any short summary is necessarily incomplete. Here, we briefly summarize some of the most prominent kernel quadrature 3methods, highlighting limitations that we address with our RPC HOLESKY approach. We refer the reader to [22, Tab. 1] for a helpful comparison of many of the below-discussed methods. Herding. Kernel herding schemes [4, 11, 26, 41] iteratively select quadrature nodes using a greedy approach. These methods face two limitations. First, they require the solution of a (typically) nonconvex global optimization problem at every step, which may be computationally costly. Second, these methods exhibit slow O(1/n) quadrature error rates [ 11, Prop. 4] (or even slower [ 16]). Optimally weighted herding is known as sequential Bayesian quadrature [26]. Thinning. Thinning methods [17, 18, 38] try to select n good quadrature nodes from a larger set of N вү« n candidate nodes drawn from a simple distribution. While these methods have other desirable theoretical properties, they do not benefit from spectral accuracy. Leverage scores and projection DPPs. With optimal weights (section 3.2), quadrature with nodes sampled using a projection determintal point process (DPP) [7] (see also [5, 6, 21]) or iid from the (ridge) leverage score distribution [3] achieve spectral accuracy. However, known efficient sampling algorithms require access to the Mercer decomposition (2), limiting the applicability of these schemes to simple spaces X, measures Вө, and kernels k, where the decomposition is known analytically. Continuous volume sampling. Continuous volume sampling is the continuous analog of n-DPP sampling [28], providing quadrature nodes that achieve spectral accuracy [8, Prop. 5]. Unfortunately, continuous volume sampling is computationally challenging. In the finite setting, the best-known algorithm [9] for exact n-DPP sampling requires a costly O(|X| В·n6.5 + n9.5) operations. Inexact samplers based on Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) (e.g., [1, 2, 34]) may be more competitive, but the best-known samplers in the continuous setting still require an expensiveO(n5 log n) MCMC steps [34, Thms. 1.3вҖ“1.4]. RPC HOLESKY sampling achieves similar theoretical guarantees to continuous volume sampling (see theorem 1) and can be efficiently and exactly sampled (algorithm 2). Recombination and convex weights. The paper [22] (see also [23]) proposes two ideas for kernel quadrature when Вө is a probability measure and g вүЎ 1. First, they suggest using a recombination algorithm (e.g., [ 29]) to subselect good quadratures from N вү« n candidate nodes iid sampled from Вө. All of the variants of their method either fail to achieve spectral accuracy or require an explicit Mercer decomposition [22, Tab. 1]. Second, they propose choosing weights (w1, . . . , wn) that are convex combination coefficients. This choice makes the quadrature scheme robust against misspecification of the RKHS H МёвҲӢf, among other benefits. It may be worth investigating a combination of RPC HOLESKY quadrature nodes with convex weights in future work. 2.2 Randomly pivoted Cholesky RPC HOLESKY was proposed, implemented, and analyzed in [10] for the task of approximating an M Г— M positive-semidefinite matrix A. The algorithm is algebraically equivalent to applying an earlier algorithm known as adaptive sampling [12, 13] to A1/2. Despite their similarities, RPC- HOLESKY and adaptive sampling are different algorithms: To produce a rank-n approximation to an M Г— M matrix, RPC HOLESKY requires O(n2M) operations, while adaptive sampling requires a much larger O(nM2) operations. See [10, В§4.1] for further discussion on RPC HOLESKY вҖҷs history. In this paper, we introduce a continuous extension of RPC HOLESKY and analyze its effectiveness for kernel quadrature. 3 Theoretical results In this section, we prove our main theoretical result for RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature. We first establish the mathematical setting (section 3.1) and introduce kernel quadrature (section 3.2). Then, we present our main theorem (section 3.3) and discuss its proof (section 3.4). 3.1 Mathematical setting and notation Let Вө be a Borel measure supported on a topological space X and let H be a RKHS on X with continuous kernel k : X Г— X вҶ’ R. We assume that x 7вҶ’ k(x, x) is integrable (3) and that H 4is dense in L2(Вө). These assumptions imply that H is compactly embedded in L2(Вө), the Mercer decomposition (2) converges pointwise, and the Mercer eigenfunctions form an orthonormal basis of L2(Вө) and an orthogonal basis of H [39, Thm. 3.1]. Define the integral operator T f(x) = Z X k(x, y)f(y) dВө(y). (5) Viewed as an operator T : L2(Вө) вҶ’ L2(Вө), T is self-adjoint, positive semidefinite, and trace-class. One final piece of notation: For a functionОҙ : X вҶ’ R and an ordered (multi)set S = {s1, . . . , sn}, we let Оҙ(S) be the column vector with ith entry Оҙ(si). Similarly, for a bivariate function h : X Г— X вҶ’ R, h(В·, S) (resp. h(S, В·)) denotes the row (resp. column) vector-valued function with ith entry h(В·, si) (resp. h(si, В·)), and h(S, S) denotes the matrix with ij entry h(si, sj). 3.2 Kernel quadrature Following earlier works (e.g., [3, 7]), let us describe the kernel quadrature problem more precisely. Given a function g вҲҲ L2(Вө), we seek quadrature weights w = ( w1, . . . , wn) вҲҲ Rn and nodes S = {s1, . . . , sn} вҠҶX that minimize the maximum quadrature error over all f вҲҲ Hwith вҲҘfвҲҘH вүӨ 1: Err(S, w; g) := max вҲҘfвҲҘHвүӨ1       Z X f(x)g(x) dВө(x) вҲ’ nX i=1 wif(si)      . (6) A short derivation ([3, Eq. (7)] or appendix C) yields the simple formula Err(S, w; g) = T gвҲ’ nX i=1 wik(В·, si)  H . (7) This equation reveals that the quadrature rule minimizing Err(S, w; g) is the least-squares approxi- mation of T gвҲҲ Hby a linear combination of kernel function evaluations Pn i=1 wik(В·, si). If we fix the quadrature nodes S = {s1, . . . , sn}, the optimal weights wвӢҶ = (wвӢҶ1, . . . , wвӢҶn) вҲҲ Rn minimizing Err(S, w; g) are the solution of the linear system of equations k(S, S)wвӢҶ = T g(S). (8) We use (8) to select the weights throughout this paper, and denote the error with optimal weights by Err(S; g) := Err(S, wвӢҶ; g). (9) 3.3 Error bounds for randomly pivoted Cholesky kernel quadrature Our main result for RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature is as follows: Theorem 1. Let S = {s1, . . . , sn} be generated by the RPC HOLESKY sampling algorithm. For any function g вҲҲ L2(Вө), nonnegative integer r вүҘ 0, and real number Оҙ >0, we have EErr2(S; g) вүӨ Оҙ В· вҲҘgвҲҘ2 L2(Вө) В· вҲһX i=r+1 О»i provided n вүҘ r log  2О»1 Оҙ PвҲһ i=r+1 О»i  + 2 Оҙ . (10) To illustrate this result, consider an RKHS with eigenvalues О»i = O(iвҲ’2s) for s >1/2. An example is the periodic Sobolev space Hs per([0, 1]) (see section 5). By setting Оҙ = 1/r, we see that EErr2(S; g) вүӨ O(rвҲ’2s) вҲҘgвҲҘ2 L2(Вө) for RPC HOLESKY with n = в„Ұ(r log r). The optimal scheme requires n = Оҳ(r) nodes to achieve this bound [ 8, Prop. 5 & В§2.5]. Thus, to achieve an error of O(rвҲ’2s), RPC HOLESKY requires just logarithmically more nodes than 5the optimal quadrature scheme for such spaces. This compares favorably to continuous volume sampling, which achieves the slightly better optimal rate but is much more difficult to sample. Having established that RPC HOLESKY achieves nearly optimal error rates for interesting RKHSвҖҷs, we make some general comments on theorem 1. First, observe that the error depends on the sumPвҲһ i=r+1 О»i of the tail eigenvalues. This tail sum is characteristic of spectrally accurate kernel quadrature schemes [7, 8]. The more distinctive feature in (10) is the logarithmic factor. Fortunately, for double precision computation, the achieveable accuracy is bounded by the machine precision 2вҲ’52, so this logarithmic factor is effectively a modest constant: log  2О»1 Оҙ PвҲһ i=r+1  вӘ… log(252) < 37. 3.4 Connection to NystrГ¶m approximation and idea of proof We briefly outline the proof of theorem 1. Following previous works (e.g., [3, 23]), we first utilize the connection between kernel quadrature and the NystrГ¶m approximation [31, 42] of the kernel k. Definition 2. For nodes S = {s1, . . . , sn} вҠҶX, the NystrГ¶m approximation to the kernel k is kS(x, y) = k(x, S) k(S, S)вҖ  k(S, y). (11) Here, вҖ  denotes the MooreвҖ“Penrose pseudoinverse. The NystrГ¶m approximate integral operator is TSf := Z X kS(В·, x)f(x) dВө(x). This definition leads to a formula for the quadrature rule Pn i=1 wik(В·, si) with optimal weights (8). Proposition 3. Fix nodes S = {s1, . . . , sn}. With the optimal weights wвӢҶ вҲҲ Rn (8), we have nX i=1 wiвӢҶk(В·, si) = TSg. (12) Consequently, Err2(S; g) = вҲҘ(T вҲ’ TS)gвҲҘ2 H вүӨ вҹЁg, (T вҲ’ TS)gвҹ©L2(Вө). To finish the proof of theorem 1, we develop and solve a recurrence for an upper bound on the largest eigenvalue of E[T вҲ’ TS]. See appendix A for details and for the proof of proposition 3. 4 Efficient randomly pivoted Cholesky by rejection sampling To efficiently perform RPC HOLESKY sampling in the continuous setting, we can use rejection sampling. (A similar rejection sampling idea is used in the MCMC continuous volume sampler of [34].) Assume for simplicity that the measure Вө is normalized so that Z X k(x, x) dВө(x) = TrT = 1. We assume two forms of access to the kernel k: 1. Entry evaluations. For any x, yвҲҲ X, we can evaluate k(x, y). 2. Sampling the diagonal. We can produce samples from the measure k(x, x) dВө(x). To sample from the RPC HOLESKY distribution, we use k(x, x) dВө(x) as a proposal distribution and accept proposal s with probability 1 вҲ’ kS(s, s) k(s, s) . (Recall kS from (11).) The resulting algorithm for RPC HOLESKY sampling is shown in algorithm 2. 6Algorithm 2 RPC HOLESKY with rejection sampling Input: Kernel k : X Г— X вҶ’ R and number of quadrature points n Output: Quadrature points s1, . . . , sn 1: Initialize L вҶҗ 0nГ—n, i вҶҗ 1, S вҶҗ вҲ… в–· L stores a Cholesky decomposition of k(S, S) 2: while i вүӨ n do 3: Sample si from the probability measure k(x, x) dВө(x) 4: (d, c) вҶҗ RESIDUAL KERNEL (si, S, L, k) в–· Helper subroutine algorithm 3 5: Draw a uniform random variable U вҲј UNIF [0, 1] 6: if U < d/k(si, si) then в–· Accept with probability d/k(si, si) 7: Induct si into the selected set: S вҶҗ S вҲӘ {si}, i вҶҗ i + 1 8: L(i, 1 : i) вҶҗ  cвҠӨ вҲҡ d  9: end if 10: end while Algorithm 3 Helper subroutine to evaluate residual kernel Input: Point s вҲҲ X, set S вҠҶ X, Cholesky factor L of k(S, S), and kernel k Output: d = k(s, s) вҲ’ kS(s, s) and c = LвҲ’1k(S, s) 1: procedure RESIDUAL KERNEL (s,S,L,k) 2: c вҶҗ LвҲ’1k(S, s) 3: d вҶҗ k(s, s) вҲ’ вҲҘcвҲҘ2 в–· d= k(s, s) вҲ’ kS(s, s) 4: return (d, c) 5: end procedure Theorem 4. Algorithm 2 produces exact RPC HOLESKY samples. Let О·i denote the trace-error of the best rank-i approximation to T: О·i = вҲһX j=i+1 О»i. The expected runtime of algorithm 2 is at most O(Pn i=1 i2/О·i) вүӨ O(n3/О·nвҲ’1). This result demonstrates that algorithm 2 suffers from the curse of smoothness : The faster the eigenvalues О»1, О»2, . . .decrease, the smaller О·nвҲ’1 will be and, consequently, the slower algorithm 2 will be in expectation. While this curse is an unfortunate limitation, it is also a common one. The curse of smoothness affects all known Mercer-free, spectrally accurate kernel quadrature schemes. In fact, the situation is worse for other algorithms. The CVS sampler of [34], for example, requires as many as O(n5 log n) MCMC steps, each of which has cost O(n2/О·nвҲ’1). According to current analysis, algorithm 2 is O(n4 log n)-times faster than the CVS sampler of [34]. Notwithstanding the curse of smoothness, algorithm 2 is useful in practice. The algorithm works under minimal assumptions and can reach useful accuracies О· = 10вҲ’5 while sampling n = 1000 nodes on a laptop. Algorithm 2 may also be interesting for RPC HOLESKY sampling for a large finite set |X| вүҘ109, since its runtime has no explicit dependence on the size of the space X. To achieve higher accuracies and blunt the curse of smoothness, we can improve algorithm 2 with optimization. Indeed, the optimal acceptance probability for algorithm 2 would be p(si; Оұ) = 1 Оұ В· k(si, si) вҲ’ kS(si, si) k(si, si) where Оұ = max xвҲҲX k(x, x) вҲ’ kS(x, x) k(x, x) . This suggests the following scheme: Initialize with Оұ = 1 and run the algorithm with acceptance probability p(si; Оұ). If we perform many loop iterations without an acceptance, we then recompute the optimal Оұ by solving an optimization problem. The resulting procedure is shown in algorithm 4. If the optimization problem for Оұ is solved to global optimality, then algorithm 4 produces exact RPC HOLESKY samples. The downside of algorithm 4 is the need to solve a (typically) nonconvex global optimization problem to compute the optimal Оұ. Fortunately, in our experiments (section 5), only a small number of optimization problems (вүӨ 10) are needed to produce a sample of n = 1000 nodes. In the setting where the optimization problem is tractable, the speedup of algorithm 4 can 7Algorithm 4 RPC HOLESKY with optimized rejection sampling Input: Kernel k : X Г— X вҶ’ R and number of quadrature points n Output: Quadrature points s1, . . . , sn 1: Initialize L вҶҗ 0nГ—n, i вҶҗ 1, S вҶҗ вҲ…, trials вҶҗ 0, Оұ вҶҗ 1 2: while i вүӨ n do 3: trials вҶҗ trials + 1 4: Sample si from the probability measure k(x, x) dВө(x) 5: (d, c) вҶҗ RESIDUAL KERNEL (si, S, L, k) в–· Helper subroutine algorithm 3 6: Draw a uniform random variable U вҲј UNIF [0, 1] 7: if U <(1/Оұ) В· d/k(si, si) then в–· Accept with probability d/k(si, si) 8: S вҶҗ S вҲӘ {si}, i вҶҗ i + 1, trials вҶҗ 0 9: L(i, 1 : i) вҶҗ  cвҠӨ вҲҡ d  10: end if 11: if trials вүҘ TRIALS_MAX then в–· We use TRIALS_MAX вҲҲ [25, 1000] 12: Оұ вҶҗ maxxвҲҲX RESIDUAL KERNEL (x, S, L, k)/k(x, x) 13: trials вҶҗ 0 14: end if 15: end while be immense. To produce n = 200 RPC HOLESKY samples for the space [0, 1]3 with the kernel (13), algorithm 4 requires just 0.19 seconds compared to 7.47 seconds for algorithm 2. 5 Comparison of methods on a benchmark example Having demonstrated the versatility of RPC HOLESKY for general spaces X, measures Вө, and kernels k in fig. 1, we now present a benchmark example from the kernel quadrature literature to compare RPC HOLESKY with other methods. Particularly striking is the result that RPC HOLESKY samplingвҖҷs performance is either very close to or better than that of continuous volume sampling, despite the latterвҖҷs slightly stronger theoretical properties. To sample 128 nodes for s = 3 and d = 1, RPC HOLESKY was 17Г— faster than continuous volume sampling, 52Г— faster than thinning, and 170Г— faster than PWKQ. To summarize our numerical evaluation (comprising fig. 2 and fig. 1 from the introduction), we find that RPC HOLESKY is among the most accurate kernel quadrature methods tested. To us, the strongest benefits of RPC HOLESKY (supported by these experiments) are the methodвҖҷs speed and flexibility. These virtues make it possible to apply spectrally accurate kernel quadrature in scenarios where it would have been computationally intractable before. 6 Application: Analyzing large chemistry datasets While our focus thusfar has been on infinite domains X, the kernel quadrature formalism can also be applied to a finite set X of data points. Let Вө = UNIF X be the uniform distribution and set g вүЎ 1. Here, the task is to exhibit n nodes S = {s1, . . . , sn} вҠҶX and weights w вҲҲ Rn such that the average of every вҖңsmoothвҖқ function f : X вҶ’ R over the whole dataset is well-approximated by a sum: mean(f) := 1 |X| X xвҲҲX f(x) вүҲ X i=1 wif(si). (14) Here is an application to chemistry. Let X denote a large set of compounds of interest, and let f : X вҶ’ R denote a target chemical property such as specific heat capacity. We are interested 9in computing mean(f). Unfortunately, evaluating f on each x вҲҲ X requires an expensive density functional theory computation, so it can be prohibitively expensive to evaluate f on every x вҲҲ X. Fortunately, we can obtain fast approximations to mean(f) using (14), which only require evaluating f on a much smaller set of size |S| вүӘ |X|. Our experimental setup is as follows. For X, we use 2 Г— 104 randomly selected points from the QM9 dataset [33, 37, 40]. We represent each compound as a vector in R1500 using the many-body tensor representation [25] computed with the DScribe package [24]. Choose k to be a Gaussian kernel with bandwidth chosen by median heuristic [20] on a further subsample of 103 random points. We omit continuous volume sampling, thinning, and positively weighted kernel quadrature because of their computational cost. In their place, we add the greedy NystrГ¶m method [19] with optimal weights (8). (a)  (b) Figure 3: Application: chemistry. Worst-case quadrature error (left) and mean relative error for estimation of the average value of the isotropic polarizability function f(x) (right) for different methods (100 trials). Shaded regions show 10%/90% quantiles. Results are shown in fig. 3. Figure 3a shows the worst-case quadrature error (9). For this metric, IID and randomly pivoted Cholesky are the definitive winners, with randomly pivoted Cholesky being slightly better. Figure 3b shows the mean relative error for the kernel quadrature estimates of the mean isotropic polarizability of the compounds in X. For sufficiently large n, randomly pivoted Cholesky has the smallest error, beating IID and Monte Carlo by a factor of three at n = 512. 7 Conclusions, Limitations, and Possibilites for Future Work In this article, we developed continuous RPC HOLESKY sampling for kernel quadrature. Theorem 1 demonstrates RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature achieves near-optimal error rates. Numerical results (fig. 2) hint that its practical performance might be even better than that suggested by our theoretical analysis and fully comparable with kernel quadrature based on the much more computationally expensive continuous volume sampling distribution. RPC HOLESKY supports performant rejection sampling algorithms (algorithms 2 and 4), which facilitate implementation for general spaces X, measures Вө, and kernels k with ease. We highlight three limitations of RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature that would be worth addressing in future work. First, given the comparable performance of RPC HOLESKY and continuous volume sampling in practice, it would be desirable to prove stronger error bounds forRPC HOLESKY sampling or find counterexamples which demonstrate a separation between the methods. Second, it would be worth developing improved sampling algorithms for RPC HOLESKY which avoid the need for global optimization steps. Third, all known spectrally accurate kernel quadrature methods require integrals of the form R X k(x, y)g(y) dВө(y), which may not be available. RPC HOLESKY kernel quadrature requires them for the computation of the weights (8). Developing spectrally accurate kernel quadrature schemes that avoid such integrals remains a major open problem for the field. 10Acknowledgements We thank Lester Mackey, Eliza OвҖҷReilly, Joel Tropp, and Robert Webber for helpful discussions and feedback. The map ОҰ is positive, monotone, and concave with respect to the order вӘҜ on the set of trace-class psd operators on L2(Вө). Thus ОҰ(A) = A  Id вҲ’ A TrA  вӘ° 0, This establishes that ОҰ is positive: A вӘ° 0 implies ОҰ(A) вӘ° 0. Concavity. For Оё вҲҲ [0, 1], denote Оё := 1 вҲ’ Оё. Then ОҰ(ОёA + ОёB) вҲ’ ОёОҰ(A) вҲ’ ОёОҰ(B) = ОёОё Оё TrA + Оё TrB  r TrB TrA A вҲ’ r TrA TrB B ! вӘ° 0. This establishes the concavity claim (17). 14Monotonicity. Suppose 0 вӘҜ A вӘҜ B. Observe the map ОҰ is positive homogeneous: For Оё > 0, ОҰ(ОёA) = ОёОҰ(A). Thus, ОҰ(B) = ОҰ(A + (B вҲ’ A)) = 2ОҰ A + (B вҲ’ A) 2  . We can use the map ОҰ to bound the residual integral operator Ti: Proposition 6. For i вүҘ 0, we have the bound E[Ti] вӘҜ ОҰi(T), (18) where ОҰi denotes the i-fold composition of ОҰ. Proof. The ith step of RPC HOLESKY applies one step of RPC HOLESKY to kiвҲ’1. Thus, E[Ti] = E[E[Ti | s1, . . . , siвҲ’1]] = E[ОҰ(TiвҲ’1)]. Applying this result twice and using monotonicity (16) yields: E[Ti] вӘҜ ОҰ(E[TiвҲ’1]) and E[TiвҲ’1] вӘҜ ОҰ(E[TiвҲ’2]) =вҮ’ E[Ti] вӘҜ ОҰ(ОҰ(E[TiвҲ’2])). D Details for numerical experiments In this section, we provide additional details about our numerical experiments. Continuous volume sampling. We are unaware of a provably correctn-DPP/continuous volume distribution sampler that is sufficiently efficient to facillitate numerical testing and comparisons. The best-known sampler [34] for the continuous setting is inexact and requires O(n5 log n) MCMC steps, which would be prohibitive for us. To produce samples from the continuous volume sampling distribution for this paper, we use a continuous analog of the MCMC algorithm [ 2]. Given a set S = {s1, . . . , sn} and assuming Вө is a probability measure, one MCMC step proceeds as follows: 171. Sample snew вҲј Вө and sold вҲј UNIF S. 2. Define SвҖІ = S вҲӘ {snew} \ {sold}. 3. With probability 1 2 min  1, det k(SвҖІ, SвҖІ) det k(S, S)  , accept and set S вҶҗ SвҖІ. Otherwise, leave S unchanged. In our experiments, we initialize with s1, . . . , sn drawn iid from Вө and run for 10n MCMC steps. Running for just 10n MCMC steps is aggressive, even in the finite setting. Indeed, the theoretical analysis of [2, Thm. 2, Eq. (1.2)] for Вө = UNIF {1, 2, . . . ,|X|}, proves the sampler mixes to stationar- ity in at most ЛңO(n2|X|) MCMC steps when properly initialized. Even with just 10n MCMC steps, continuous volume sampling is dramatically more expensive than RPC HOLESKY in our experiments for n вүҘ 64. Finally, we notice that the performance of continuous volume sampling degrades to that of iid sampling for sufficiently large n. We believe that the cause for this are numerical issues in evaluating the determinants of the kernel matrices k(S, S). This suggests that, in addition to being faster, RPC HOLESKY may be more numerically robust than continuous volume sampling. Thinning and positively weighted kernel quadrature. Our kernel quadrature experiments with thinning and PWKQ is based on code from the authors of [22] available online at https://github. com/satoshi-hayakawa/kernel-quadrature, which uses the goodpoints package (https: //github.com/microsoft/goodpoints) by the authors of [17, 18, 38] to perform thinning. We use g = 4, Оҙ = 0.5, and four bins for the COMPRESS ++ algorithm. Miscellaneous. Our code is available online at https://github.com/eepperly/RPCholesky-Kernel-Quadrature. To evaluate exact integrals T g(x) = Z X k(x, y)g(y) dВө(y) for fig. 1, we use the 2D functionality in ChebFun [15]. To compute the optimal weights (8), we add a small multiple of the identity to regularize the system: wвӢҶ, reg = (k(S, S) + 10Оөmach trace(k(S, S)) В· I)вҲ’1T g(S). Here, Оөmach = 2вҲ’52 is the double precision machine epsilon. E Randomly pivoted Cholesky and NystrГ¶m approximation In this section, we describe the connection between the RPC HOLESKY algorithm and NystrГ¶m approximation. We hope that this explanation provides context that will be helpful in understanding the RPC HOLESKY algorithm and the proofs of theorems 1 and 4. To make the following discussion more clear, we begin by rewriting the basic RPC HOLESKY algorithm (see algorithm 1) in algorithm 5 to avoid overwriting the kernel k at each step: From this rewriting, we see that in addition to selecting quadrature nodes s1, . . . , sn, the RPC- HOLESKY algorithm builds an approximation ЛҶkn to the kernel k. Indeed, one can verify that the approximation ЛҶkn produced by this algorithm is precisely the NystrГ¶m approximation (definition 2). Proposition 8. The output ЛҶkn of algorithm 5 is equal to the NystrГ¶m approximation k{s1,...,sn}. Proof. Proceed by induction on n. The base case n = 0 is immediate. Fix n and let S := {s1, . . . , sn}, sвҖІ := sn+1, and SвҖІ := {s1, . . . , sn, sвҖІ}. Write the NystrГ¶m approximation kSвҖІ as kSвҖІ = [k(В·, S) k(В·, sвҖІ)]  k(S, S) k(S, sвҖІ) k(sвҖІ, S) k(sвҖІ, sвҖІ) вҲ’1  k(S, В·) k(sвҖІ, В·)  18Algorithm 5 RPC HOLESKY : unoptimized implementation without overwriting Input: Kernel k : X Г— X вҶ’ R and number of quadrature points n Output: Quadrature points s1, . . . , sn and NystrГ¶m approximation ЛҶkn = k{s1,...,sn} to k 1: Initialize k0 вҶҗ k, ЛҶk0 вҶҗ 0 2: for i = 1, 2, . . . , ndo 3: Sample si from the probability measure k(x, x) dВө(x)/ R X k(x, x) dВө(x) 4: Update NystrГ¶m approximation ЛҶki вҶҗ ЛҶkiвҲ’1 + kiвҲ’1(В·, si)kiвҲ’1(si, si)вҲ’1kiвҲ’1(si, В·) 5: Update kernel ki вҶҗ kiвҲ’1 вҲ’ kiвҲ’1(В·, si)kiвҲ’1(si, si)вҲ’1kiвҲ’1(si, В·) 6: end for Compute a block LDLвҠӨ factorization of the matrix in the middle of this expression:  k(S, S) k(S, sвҖІ) k(sвҖІ, S) k(sвҖІ, sвҖІ)  =  I 0 k(sвҖІ, S)k(S, S)вҲ’1 1 k(S, S) 0 0вҠӨ kn(sвҖІ, sвҖІ)  I k(S, S)вҲ’1k(S, sвҖІ) 0вҠӨ 1  . Here, weвҖҷve used the induction hypothesis kn(sвҖІ, sвҖІ) = k(sвҖІ, sвҖІ) вҲ’ kS(sвҖІ, sвҖІ) = k(sвҖІ, sвҖІ) вҲ’ k(sвҖІ, S)k(S, S)вҲ’1k(S, sвҖІ). (24) Thus, kSвҖІ = [k(В·, S) k(В·, sвҖІ)]  k(S, S) k(S, sвҖІ) k(sвҖІ, S) k(sвҖІ, sвҖІ) вҲ’1  k(S, В·) k(sвҖІ, В·)  = [k(В·, S) k(В·, sвҖІ)]  I вҲ’k(S, S)вҲ’1k(S, sвҖІ) 0вҠӨ 1 k(S, S)вҲ’1 0 0вҠӨ kn(sвҖІ, sвҖІ)вҲ’1  В·  I 0 вҲ’k(sвҖІ, S)k(S, S)вҲ’1 1  k(S, В·) k(sвҖІ, В·)  = [k(В·, S) kn(В·, sвҖІ)] k(S, S)вҲ’1 0 0вҠӨ kn(sвҖІ, sвҖІ)вҲ’1  k(S, В·) kn(В·, sвҖІ)  (25) = ЛҶkn + kn(В·, sвҖІ)kn(sвҖІ, sвҖІ)вҲ’1kn(sвҖІ, В·) = ЛҶkn+1. For (25), we used the induction hypothesis (24) again. Having established that kSвҖІ = kn+1, the proposition is proven. 19

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces a Mercer-free kernel quadrature method based on a continuous extension of randomly pivoted Cholesky (RPC) sampling. Proves near-optimal, spectrally governed error bounds (Theorem 1) with only a logarithmic oversampling gap to optimal schemes. Develops exact and efficient rejection-sampling implementations for continuous domains (Algorithms 2 and 4) with runtime guarantees (Theorem 4). Demonstrates broad applicability to arbitrary domains, measures, and kernels, and shows competitive or superior empirical accuracy and much faster sampling than continuous volume sampling (CVS), thinning, and positively weighted kernel quadrature (PWKQ). Establishes connections to NystrГ¶m approximation and gives optimal-weight computation via a linear system.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: - Node selection via RPC: sample s1 from the normalized kernel diagonal k(x,x)dОј, update kernel by Schur complement k вҶҗ k вҲ’ k(В·,s1)k(s1,s1)^{-1}k(s1,В·), and iterate. Probabilistic view: sequential GP conditioning on selected nodes. - Weights: optimal weights solve k(S,S) w* = T g(S), where T f(x) = вҲ« k(x,y) f(y) dОј(y). With these weights, the quadrature error equals ||(T вҲ’ T_S)g||_H. - NystrГ¶m connection: the constructed kernel approximation equals k_S; the quadrature rule equals T_S g, converting error control to bounding the residual operator T вҲ’ T_S. - Error analysis: define ОҰ(T) = T вҲ’ T^2/Tr T; use positivity/monotonicity/concavity of ОҰ to show E[T вҲ’ T_S] вӘҜ ОҰ^n(T) and derive Theorem 1: E Err^2 вүӨ Оҙ ||g||^2 ОЈ_{i>r} О»_i for n вүҘ r log(2 О»1/(Оҙ ОЈ_{i>r} О»_i)) + 2/Оҙ. - Continuous implementations: exact rejection sampling (Algorithm 2) draws from k(x,x)dОј and accepts with prob 1 вҲ’ k_S(x,x)/k(x,x) using a maintained Cholesky of k(S,S). Optimized variant (Algorithm 4) scales acceptance by Оұ = max_x (k вҲ’ k_S)(x,x)/k(x,x), updated via global optimization when needed. - Runtime: expected cost O(ОЈ_{i=1}^n i^2/О·_i) вүӨ O(n^3/О·_{nвҲ’1}), where О·_i = ОЈ_{j>i} О»_j (Theorem 4).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: - Illustrative geometry: nonconvex crescent domain in R^2, MatГ©rn 5/2 kernel (bandwidth 2), measure dОј(x,y) = (x^2 + y^2) dx dy. Function f(x,y) = sin(x) exp(y), g вүЎ 1. Compare RPC, iid kernel quadrature, and Monte Carlo over varying n with 100 trials. - Benchmark (Sobolev): periodic Sobolev H^s_per([0,1]) with kernel k(x,y) = 1 + 2 ОЈ_{mвүҘ1} m^{-2s} cos(2ПҖm(xвҲ’y)), and product kernel on [0,1]^3: kвҠ—3. Settings: (s=3,d=1) and (s=3,d=3). Ој = Uniform, g вүЎ 1. Metric: Err(S,w;g), computed in closed form. Methods compared: Monte Carlo (uniform weights), IID kernel quadrature (optimal weights), kernel thinning (COMPRESS++ with g=4, Оҙ=0.5, 4 bins), CVS via MCMC (10n steps), RPC (Alg. 4), PWKQ (NystrГ¶m+empirical+opt). 100 trials. Hardware: MacBook Pro, 2.4 GHz 8-Core Intel i9, 64 GB RAM. Code: https://github.com/eepperly/RPCholesky-Kernel-Quadrature. - Chemistry application (finite X): 20,000 QM9 molecules; MBTR features via DScribe; Gaussian kernel with median heuristic bandwidth (estimated on 1,000 subsample). Compare Monte Carlo, IID, RPC, and Greedy NystrГ¶m. Metrics: worst-case quadrature error Err(S;g) and mean relative error for estimating average isotropic polarizability. 100 trials. Implementation notes: Chebfun used for exact integrals in 2D example; weight system regularized with 10В·Оө_machВ·trace(k(S,S))В·I.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: - Curse of smoothness: rejection sampling acceptance depends on О·_{nвҲ’1} = ОЈ_{i>nвҲ’1} О»_i; for very smooth kernels (fast eigenvalue decay) Algorithm 2 slows (expected runtime O(n^3/О·_{nвҲ’1})). - Optimal-weight computation requires access to integrals T g(x) = вҲ« k(x,y) g(y) dОј(y), which may be unavailable or expensive in some settings. - Optimized rejection sampler (Algorithm 4) requires solving a generally nonconvex global optimization to update Оұ; although infrequent in practice, it can be costly. - Theory provides near-optimal rates with a logarithmic oversampling factor; whether RPC matches CVSвҖҷs optimal constants or rates without the log is open. - Assumptions: integrability of k(x,x) and ability to sample from k(x,x) dОј(x); continuity and RKHS density in L2(Ој). - CVS baseline used an inexact, short-run MCMC sampler (10n steps), potentially underestimating CVS performance; numerical determinant issues may affect CVS at large n.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Strengthen theoretical guarantees for RPC (e.g., remove or tighten the logarithmic factor) or prove separations vs. continuous volume sampling. - Design improved, exact RPC samplers that avoid global nonconvex optimization and mitigate the curse of smoothness (e.g., better proposal distributions, control variates, multi-scale or preconditioned variants). - Develop spectrally accurate kernel quadrature schemes that do not require access to T g integrals; or approximate-weight strategies with guarantees. - Combine RPC-selected nodes with convex/positive weights (Г  la PWKQ) to improve robustness under model misspecification. - Extend to large-scale and streaming settings (finite X with |X|вү«1e9), leveraging structure, fast kernel approximations, or distributed implementations. - Explore adaptive or data-driven choices of g and kernels, and applications to scientific computing (e.g., PDE solvers, probabilistic numerics) and domain-specific integrals over complex geometries.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import minimize
import warnings

# --- Core method: Randomized (rejection) pivoted Cholesky for node selection ---
# Implements exact rejection sampling with acceptance prob (k - k_S)(x,x) / k(x,x) / alpha
# and optional optimized variant that updates alpha via local optimization.

def rpcholesky(proposal, num_pts, kernel, algorithm="reject"):
    """
    Node selection via RPCholesky using rejection sampling.
    - proposal: callable returning a sample s ~ mu (domain measure)
    - num_pts: number of nodes to select
    - kernel: callable K(X, Y) returning Gram matrix
    - algorithm: "reject" (Algorithm 2) or any string containing "opt" to enable
      optimized acceptance scaling (Algorithm 4). When "opt" is used, a local
      optimizer updates alpha вүҲ max_x (k - k_S)(x,x)/k(x,x) to improve acceptance.

    Returns: pts (num_pts x d) array of selected nodes.
    """
    pts = np.zeros((num_pts, len(proposal())))
    L = np.zeros((num_pts, num_pts))  # maintained Cholesky factor of k(S,S)
    i = 0
    trials = 0
    alpha = 1.0  # acceptance scaling; alpha >= sup_x (k - k_S)(x,x)/k(x,x)

    if ("opt" in algorithm):
        warnings.warn(
            "Optimization should only be used when kernel is periodic or domain is all of space",
            UserWarning,
        )

    while i < num_pts:
        s = proposal()
        d, c, kss = reskernel(s, pts[0:i, :], L[0:i, 0:i], kernel)
        trials += 1

        # Exact rejection sampling acceptance probability
        if np.random.rand() < d / kss / alpha:
            pts[i, :] = s
            L[i, 0:i] = c
            L[i, i] = np.sqrt(d)
            i += 1
            trials = 0

        # Optional: update alpha by maximizing d/kss via local search
        if ("opt" in algorithm) and trials >= 25:
            def obj_helper(arr):
                # arr = (d, c, kss) from reskernel, we maximize d/kss => minimize -d/kss
                return -arr[0] / arr[2]

            def objective(x):
                return obj_helper(reskernel(x, pts[0:i, :], L[0:i, 0:i], kernel))

            result = minimize(
                objective,
                proposal(),
                method="nelder-mead",
                options={"xatol": 1e-3, "disp": False},
            )
            trials = 0
            if not result.success:
                warnings.warn("Optimization unsuccessful", RuntimeWarning)
            else:
                alpha = -result.fun  # updated acceptance scaling

    return pts


def reskernel(s, pts, L, kernel):
    """
    Residual kernel computations for RPCholesky.
    - s: candidate point (1D array of shape (d,))
    - pts: current point set S (i x d)
    - L: current Cholesky factor of k(S,S) (i x i)
    - kernel: callable K(X, Y)

    Returns: (d, c, kss)
    where
      kss = k(s,s),
      c = solve(L, k(S,s)),
      d = kss - ||c||^2 = (k - k_S)(s,s)
    """
    if len(pts) == 0:
        kss = float(kernel(s[np.newaxis, :], s[np.newaxis, :]))
        return kss, np.zeros((0,)), kss

    k = kernel(pts, s[np.newaxis, :]).reshape((len(pts),))
    # Solve L c = k for c (forward substitution); robust for L triangular
    c = np.linalg.solve(L, k)
    kss = float(kernel(s[np.newaxis, :], s[np.newaxis, :]))
    d = kss - np.dot(c, c)
    return d, c, kss


# --- Optimal weights: solve K(S,S) w* = z with z_j = вҲ« k(x, s_j) dmu(x) ---

def weights(pts, integrator=None, kernel=None, total_mass=1.0, method="optimal"):
    """
    Compute quadrature weights for selected nodes.
    - method="optimal": solve K w = z, where z_j = вҲ« k(x, s_j) dmu(x)
      using the provided 'integrator' callable.
    - method="uniform": uniform weights over pts with total mass 'total_mass'.

    Args:
      pts: (n x d) array of nodes S
      integrator: callable taking a function g(x) and returning вҲ« g(x) dmu(x)
      kernel: callable K(X, Y) returning Gram matrix
    """
    num_pts = pts.shape[0]
    if method == "optimal":
        if integrator is None or kernel is None:
            raise ValueError("integrator and kernel are required for method='optimal'")
        z = np.zeros(num_pts)
        for j in range(num_pts):
            # z_j = вҲ« k(x, s_j) dmu(x)
            z[j] = integrator(lambda x: float(kernel(x[:, np.newaxis], pts[j, np.newaxis])))
        K = kernel(pts, pts)
        # tiny Tikhonov for numerical stability
        reg = 10 * np.finfo(float).eps * np.trace(K)
        return np.linalg.solve(K + reg * np.eye(num_pts), z)
    elif method == "uniform":
        return total_mass * np.ones(num_pts) / num_pts
    else:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Method '{method}' unrecognized")


def integrate(f, pts, wts):
    """Compute sum_i w_i f(x_i)."""
    val = 0.0
    for i in range(len(wts)):
        val += wts[i] * float(f(pts[i, :]))
    return val


# --- Example kernel and usage (periodic Sobolev s=1 on [0,1]) ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    s = 1
    bern = lambda x: x**2 - x + 1.0 / 6.0
    pref = (-1) ** (s - 1) * (2 * np.pi) ** (2 * s) / np.math.factorial(2 * s)
    sobolev_kernel = lambda X, Y: 1.0 + pref * bern((X - Y.T) % 1.0)

    # Proposal mu: uniform on [0,1]
    proposal = lambda: np.random.rand(1)

    # For this kernel on [0,1], вҲ« k(x, y) dx = 1 for all y
    integrator = lambda g: 1.0

    f = lambda x: np.sin(2 * np.pi * x)

    n = 100
    S = rpcholesky(proposal, n, sobolev_kernel, algorithm="reject")
    w = weights(S, integrator, sobolev_kernel, method="optimal")

    err = abs(integrate(f, S, w))
    print(f"RPCholesky (reject) error with n={n}: {err}")

    # Optimized acceptance variant (Algorithm 4)
    Sopt = rpcholesky(proposal, n, sobolev_kernel, algorithm="reject+opt")
    wopt = weights(Sopt, integrator, sobolev_kernel, method="optimal")
    eropt = abs(integrate(f, Sopt, wopt))
    print(f"RPCholesky (optimized) error with n={n}: {eropt}")
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
Experimental settings (as implemented in the extracted code):
- Domain and base measure mu: unit interval [0,1] with uniform measure (proposal() -> np.random.rand(1)). For d>1 use np.random.rand(d).
- Kernel (periodic Sobolev, s=1): k(x,y) = 1 + C * B_2((x-y) mod 1), where B_2 is the Bernoulli polynomial x^2 - x + 1/6 and C = (2*pi)^(2s) * (-1)^(s-1) / (2s)!. For this kernel on [0,1], вҲ«_0^1 k(x,y) dx = 1 for all y.
- Node selection (Algorithm 2: exact rejection sampling):
  - Maintain Cholesky factor L of k(S,S).
  - For candidate s ~ mu: compute d = (k - k_S)(s,s) = k(s,s) - ||c||^2 with c = solve(L, k(S,s)).
  - Accept with probability d / k(s,s) / alpha. With alpha = 1 for kernels with constant diagonal 1 (e.g., this Sobolev kernel), this is exact.
- Optimized acceptance (Algorithm 4, optional):
  - After 25 rejected trials, update alpha by local optimization of max_x (k - k_S)(x,x)/k(x,x) via NelderвҖ“Mead (xatol=1e-3). This improves acceptance rate; only recommended for periodic or whole-space domains.
- Weights (optimal): solve K(S,S) w = z with z_j = вҲ« k(x, s_j) dmu(x). For the example Sobolev kernel on [0,1], z_j = 1, so integrator returns 1. A tiny Tikhonov regularization 10*eps*trace(K) is added for numerical stability.
- Test function: f(x) = sin(2*pi*x). Report absolute error |sum_i w_i f(x_i)|.
- Default parameters in the example script: n = 100 nodes; algorithm either "reject" (Algorithm 2) or "reject+opt" (Algorithm 4). No random seed is fixed (results vary per run).
- Extensibility:
  - Higher dimensions: set proposal = lambda: np.random.rand(d) and provide a kernel K on [0,1]^d; integrator must compute z_j = вҲ« k(x, s_j) dx.
  - Other measures mu: change proposal accordingly and use the corresponding integrator for z.

research_study_list.title: An analysis of Ermakov-Zolotukhin quadrature using kernels
research_study_list.abstract:
We study a quadrature, proposed by Ermakov and Zolotukhin in the sixties,
through the lens of kernel methods. The nodes of this quadrature rule follow
the distribution of a determinantal point process, while the weights are
defined through a linear system, similarly to the optimal kernel quadrature. In
this work, we show how these two classes of quadrature are related, and we
prove a tractable formula of the expected value of the squared worst-case
integration error on the unit ball of an RKHS of the former quadrature. In
particular, this formula involves the eigenvalues of the corresponding kernel
and leads to improving on the existing theoretical guarantees of the optimal
kernel quadrature with determinantal point processes.
research_study_list.full_text: An analysis of Ermakov-Zolotukhin quadrature using kernels Ayoub Belhadji Univ Lyon, ENS de Lyon Inria, CNRS, UCBL LIP UMR 5668, Lyon, France ayoub.belhadji@ens-lyon.fr Abstract We study a quadrature, proposed by Ermakov and Zolotukhin in the sixties, through the lens of kernel methods. The nodes of this quadrature rule follow the distribution of a determinantal point process, while the weights are defined through a linear system, similarly to the optimal kernel quadrature. In this work, we show how these two classes of quadrature are related, and we prove a tractable formula of the expected value of the squared worst-case integration error on the unit ball of an RKHS of the former quadrature. In particular, this formula involves the eigenvalues of the corresponding kernel and leads to improving on the existing theoretical guarantees of the optimal kernel quadrature with determinantal point processes. 1 Introduction Integrals appear in many scientific fields as quantities of interest per se. For example, in statistics, they represent expectations [27], while in mathematical finance, they represent the prices of financial products [17]. Unfortunately, integrals that can be written in closed form are exceptional. In general, their values are only known through approximations. For this reason, numerical integration is at the heart of many tasks in applied mathematics and statistics. Among all the possible approximation schemes, quadratures are the most practical since they approximate the integral of a function by a finite mixture of its evaluations. In this work, we focus on quadrature rules that take the form Z X f(x)g(x)dПү(x) вүҲ X iвҲҲ[N] wif(xi), (1) where the nodes xi are independent of f and g, while the weights wi depend only on g. The nodes and the weights of a quadrature may be seen as degrees of freedom that the practitioner may tune in order to achieve a given level of approximation error. The design of quadratures gave birth to a rich literature from Gaussian quadrature [15] to Monte Carlo methods [25] to quadratures based on determinantal point processes (DPPs) [1]. These latter form a large class of probabilistic models of repulsive random subsets that make numerical integration possible in a variety of domains with strong theoretical guarantees. In particular, central limit theorems with asymptotic convergence rates that scale better than the typical Monte Carlo rate O(NвҲ’1/2) were proven for several DPP based quadratures: when the integrand is a C1 function [1] or even when the integrand is non-differentiable [10]. Moreover, it is possible to design quadrature rules based on DPPs with non-asymptotic guarantees and with rates of convergence that adapt to the smoothness of the integrand. This is the case of the quadrature proposed by Ermakov and Zolotukhin in [14] and recently revisited in [16], and the optimal kernel quadrature [3]. In this work, we study the quadrature rule proposed by Ermakov and Zolotukhin (EZQ) through the lens of kernel methods. We start by comparing the weights of EZQ to the weights of the optimal 35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021). arXiv:2309.01200v1  [math.NA]  3 Sep 2023kernel quadrature (OKQ), and we prove that they both belong to a broader class of quadrature rules that we call kernel based interpolation quadrature. Then, we study the approximation quality of EZQ in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHSs). This is done by proving a general tractable formula of the expected value of the squared worst-case integration error for functions that belong to the unit ball of an RKHS when the nodes follow the distribution of a determinantal point process. This formula involves principally the eigenvalues of the integral operator, and converges to0 at a slightly slower rate than the optimal rate. Interestingly, this analysis yields a better upper bound for the optimal kernel quadrature with DPPs proposed initially in [ 3]. Comparably to the theoretical guarantees given in [14, 16], our theoretical guarantees are independent of the choice of the test function. This facilitates the comparison of EZQ with other quadratures such as OKQ. The rest of the article is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews the work of [ 14] and recall key concepts on kernel based quadrature. In Section 3, we present the main results of this work and their consequences. A sketch of the proof of the main theorem is given in Section 4. We illustrate the theoretical results by numerical experiments in Section 5. Finally, we give a conclusion in Section 6. Notation and assumptions. We use the notation NвҲ— = N вҲ–{0}. We denote by Пү a Borel measure supported on X, and we denote by L2(Пү) the Hilbert space of square integrable real-valued functions on X with respect to Пү, equipped with the inner product вҹЁВ·, В·вҹ©Пү, and the associated norm вҲҘ.вҲҘПү. For N вҲҲ NвҲ—, we denote by ПүвҠ—N the tensor product of Пү defined on XN . Moreover, we denote by F the RKHS associated to the kernel k : X Г— X вҶ’R that we assume to be continuous and satisfying the condition R X k(x, x)dПү(x) < +вҲһ. In particular, we assume the Mercer decomposition k(x, y) = X mвҲҲNвҲ— ПғmП•m(x)П•m(y), (2) to hold, where the convergence is pointwise, and Пғm and П•m are the corresponding eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of the integral operator ОЈ defined for f вҲҲ L2(Пү) by ОЈf(В·) = Z X k(В·, y)f(y)dПү(y). (3) We assume that the sequence Пғ = (Пғm)mвҲҲNвҲ— is non-increasing and its elements are non-vanishing, and we assume that the corresponding eigenfunctions П•m are continuous. Note that the П•m are normalized: вҲҘП•mвҲҘПү = 1for m вҲҲ NвҲ—. In particular, (П•m)mвҲҲNвҲ— is an o.n.b. of L2(Пү), and every element f вҲҲ Fsatisfies X mвҲҲNвҲ— вҹЁf, П•mвҹ©2 Пү Пғm < +вҲһ. (4) Moreover, for every N вҲҲ NвҲ—, we denote by EN the eigen-subspace of L2(Пү) spanned by П•1, . . . , П•N . For any kernel Оә : X Г— X вҶ’R, and for x вҲҲ XN , we define the kernel matrix Оә(x) := (Оә(xi, xj))i,jвҲҲ[N] вҲҲ RNГ—N . Finally, we denote in bold fonts the corresponding kernel matrices: K(x) for the kernel k, KN (x) for the kernel kN , KвҠҘ N (x) for the kernel kвҠҘ N , Оә(x) for the kernel Оә... Similarly, for any function Вө : X вҶ’R and for x вҲҲ XN , we define the vector of evaluations Вө(x) := (Вө(xi))iвҲҲ[N] вҲҲ RN . 2 Related work In the section, we review some results that are relevant to the contribution. 2.1 Ermakov-Zolotukhin quadrature The quadrature rule proposed by Ermakov and Zolotukhin in [14] deals with integrals that write Z X f(x)П•m(x)dПү(x), (5) where f вҲҲ L2(Пү), and (П•m)mвҲҲNвҲ— is an orthonormal family with respect to the measure Пү. Its construction goes as follows. Let N вҲҲ NвҲ— and let x вҲҲ XN such that the matrix ОҰN (x) := (П•n(xi))(n,i)вҲҲ[N]Г—[N] 2is non-singular. For n вҲҲ [N], define IEZ,n(f) = X iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwEZ,n i f(xi), (6) where ЛҶwEZ,n := ( ЛҶwEZ,n i )iвҲҲ[N] вҲҲ RN is given by ЛҶwEZ,n = ОҰN (x)вҲ’1en, with en is the vector of RN with the n-th coordinate is 1 and the rest are 0 1. We can prove easily that this quadrature is exact вҲҖf вҲҲ Span(П•n)nвҲҲ[N], X iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwEZ,n i f(xi) = Z X f(x)П•n(x) dПү(x). (7) A quadrature rule that satisfies (7) is typically called an interpolatory quadrature rule. Now, if f /вҲҲ Span(П•n)nвҲҲ[N], the authors studied the expected value and the variance of IEZ,n(f) when x = (x1, . . . , xN ) is taken to be a random variable in XN that follows the distribution of density pDPP(x1, . . . , xN ) := 1 N! Det2 ОҰN (x), (8) with respect to the product measure ПүвҠ—N defined on XN . As it was observed in [16], the nodes of the quadrature follow the distribution of the determinantal point process of reference measure Пү and marginal kernel ОәN defined by ОәN (x, y) =P nвҲҲ[N] П•n(x)П•n(y). We refer to [20] for further details on determinantal point processes. Now, we recall the main result of [14]. Theorem 1. Let x be a random subset of X that follows the distribution of DPP of kernel ОәN and reference measure Пү. Let f вҲҲ L2(Пү), and n вҲҲ [N]. Then EDPPIEZ,n(f) = Z X f(x)П•n(x)dПү(x), (9) and VDPPIEZ,n(f) = X mвүҘN+1 вҹЁf, П•mвҹ©2 Пү. (10) Theorem 1 shows that the IEZ,n(f) is an unbiased estimator of R X f(x)П•n(x)dПү(x), and its variance depends on the coefficients вҹЁf, П•mвҹ©Пү for m вүҘ N + 1. Consequently, the expected squared error of the quadrature is equal to the variance of IEZ,n(f) and it is given by EDPP      Z X f(x)П•n(x)dПү(x) вҲ’ X iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwEZ,n i f(xi)      2 = X mвүҘN+1 вҹЁf, П•mвҹ©2 Пү. (11) The identity (11) gives a theoretical guarantee for an interpolatory quadrature rule when the nodes follow the distribution of the DPP defined by (8). Compared to existing work on the literature [11, 22вҖ“24], (11) is generic and applies to any orthonormal family. Now, we may observe that the expected squared error in(11) depends strongly on the function f. This makes the comparison between EZQ and other quadratures, based on some test functionf, tricky: the choice of f may favor (or disfavor) EZQ. In order to circumvent this difficulty, we suggest to study a figure of merit that is independent of the choice of the function f. This is possible using kernels through the study of the worst-case integration error on the unit ball of an RKHS. The definition of this quantity will be recalled in the following section. 2.2 The worst integration error in kernel quadrature The use of the kernel framework in the context of numerical integration can be tracked back to the work of Hickernell [18, 19], who introduced the use of kernels to the quasi Monte Carlo community. Their use was popularized in the machine learning community by [ 28, 9]. In this framework, the quality of a quadrature is assessed by the worst-case integration error on the unit ball of an RKHS F associated to some kernel k : X Г— X вҶ’R+. This quantity is defined as follows sup fвҲҲF, вҲҘfвҲҘFвүӨ1     Z X f(x)g(x)dПү(x) вҲ’ X iвҲҲ[N] wif(xi)    . (12) 1The dependency of the vector ЛҶwEZ,n on x was dropped for simplicity. 3This quantity reflects how good is the quadrature uniformly on the unit ball of F. Interestingly, this quantity has a closed formula Вөg вҲ’ X iвҲҲ[N] wik(xi, .)  F , (13) where Вөg = ОЈg is the so-called embedding of g in the RKHS F. We shall use in Section 3.2 the equivalent expression (13) of the worst-case integration error, to derive a closed formula of EDPP sup fвҲҲF, вҲҘfвҲҘFвүӨ1     Z X f(x)g(x)dПү(x) вҲ’ X iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwEZ,n i f(xi)     2 . (14) By now, we observe that the weights ЛҶwEZ,n i of EZQ are non-optimal in the sense that they do not minimize (13). By definition, the optimal kernel quadrature for a given configuration x, such that the kernel matrix K(x) is non-singular, is the quadrature with nodes the xi and weights the ЛҶwi that minimize (13). We specify in Section 3.1 the subtle difference between the quadrature of Ermakov and Zolotukhin and the optimal kernel quadrature. Before that, we review the existing constructions of the optimal kernel quadrature in the following section. 2.3 The design of the optimal kernel quadrature The optimal kernel quadrature may be calculated numerically under the assumption that the matrix K(x) is non-singular. Indeed, for a given configuration of nodes x вҲҲ XN , the square of (13) is quadratic on w and have a unique solution given by ЛҶwOKQ,g = K(x)вҲ’1Вөg(x) 2. In particular, the optimal mixture P iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwOKQ,g i k(xi, .) takes the same values as Вөg on the nodes xi: the optimal mixture interpolates the function Вөg on the configuration of nodes x. At this level, x is still a degree of freedom and need to be designed. This task was tackled by different approaches. One approach consists on using adhoc designs for which a theoretical analysis of the convergence rate is possible. This is the case of, inter alia, the uniform grid in the periodic Sobolev space [ 6, 26], higher-order digital nets sequences in tensor products of Sobolev spaces [ 8], or tensor product of scaled Hermite roots in the RKHS defined by the Gaussian kernel [22]. Another approach consists on using a sequential algorithm to build up the configuration x [12, 13, 21, 7]. In general, each step of these greedy algorithms requires to solve a non-convex problem and costly approximations must be employed. Alternatively, random designs, based on determinantal point processes and their mixtures [3, 4], were shown to have strong theoretical guarantees and competitive empirical performances. More precisely, it was shown that ifx follows the distribution of DPP of reference measure Пү and marginal kernel ОәN , and if g вҲҲ L2(Пү) such that вҲҘgвҲҘПү вүӨ 1, then EDPP Вөg вҲ’ X iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwOKQ,g i k(xi, .)  2 F вүӨ 2ПғN+1 + 2  X nвҲҲ[N] |вҹЁg, П•nвҹ©Пү| 2 NrN , (15) where rN = P mвүҘN+1 Пғm [2] (Theorem 4.8). However, numerical simulations suggest that the l.h.s. of (15) converges to 0 at the faster rate O(ПғN+1), which corresponds to the best achievable rate according to [4]. This optimal rate was proved to be achieved, under some mild conditions on the eigenvalues Пғn, using the distribution of continuous volume sampling (CVS) [4]. This distribution is a mixture of determinantal point processes and is closely tied to the projection DPP used in [3] and comes with the following guarantee вҲҖg вҲҲ L2(Пү), ECVS Вөg вҲ’ X iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwOKQ,g i k(xi, .)  2 F = X mвҲҲNвҲ— вҹЁg, П•nвҹ©2 ПүПөm(N), (16) where Пөm(N) =O(ПғN+1) for every m вҲҲ NвҲ—, so that the expected squared worst-integration error of OKQ under the continuous volume sampling distribution scales as O(ПғN+1) for every g вҲҲ L2(Пү). 2The dependency of the vector ЛҶwOKQ,g on x was dropped for simplicity. 43 Main results This section gathers the main contributions of this article. In Section 3.1, we prove that both EZQ and OKQ belong to a larger class of quadrature rules called kernel-based interpolation quadrature (KBIQ). In Section 3.2, we prove a close formula of the expected squared worst-case integration error of EZQ. In Section 3.3, we use Theorem 3 to improve on the existing theoretical guarantees of OKQ with DPPs. 3.1 Kernel-based interpolation quadrature In this section, we define a new class of quadrature rules that extends both Ermakov-Zolotukhin quadrature and the optimal kernel quadrature. We start by the following observation: the weights ЛҶwEZ,n i (x) of EZQ, defined in (6), writes as ЛҶwEZ,n(x) =ОҰN (x)вҲ’1en. (17) By observing that П•n(x) =ОҰN (x)вҠәen, and ОәN (x) =ОҰN (x)вҠәОҰN (x), we prove that ЛҶwEZ,n(x) =ОәN (x)вҲ’1П•n(x), (18) Equivalently, we haveП•n(x) =ОәN (x) ЛҶwEZ,n(x). In other words, P iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwEZ,n i (x)ОәN (xi, .) takes the same values as П•n on the nodes xi: ЛҶwEZ,n(x) is the vector resulting of the interpolation of П•n by the kernel ОәN . From this observation, we define kernel-based interpolation quadrature as an extension of EZQ as follows: let Оі := (Оіm)mвҲҲNвҲ— be a sequence of positive real numbers, and let M вҲҲ NвҲ— вҲӘ {+вҲһ}. Define the kernel ОәОі,M on X Г— Xby вҲҖx, yвҲҲ X, ОәОі,M (x, y) = MX m=1 ОіmП•m(x)П•m(y). (19) Now, starting from a configuration x вҲҲ XN such that Det ОәN (x) > 0, we have Det ОәОі,M (x) > 0 3, and for a given g вҲҲ L2(Пү), we define the vector of weights ЛҶwОі,M,g(x) вҲҲ RN by ЛҶwОі,M,g(x) =ОәОі,M (x)вҲ’1ВөОі,M g (x), (20) where ВөОі,M g (x) = MX m=1 ОіmвҹЁg, П•mвҹ©ПүП•m(x). (21) We check again that P iвҲҲ[N] ЛҶwОі,M,g i ОәОі,M (xi, .) takes the same values as ВөОі,M g on the nodes xi: the mixture interpolates ВөОі,M g on the nodes xi. Now, for a given g вҲҲ L2(Пү), the vector of weights ЛҶwОі,M,g(x) have two degrees of freedom: the sequence Оі and the rank of the kernel M. These degrees of freedom may be mixed in a variety of ways to cover a large class of quadrature rules. In particular, we show in Section 3.1.1 that, for any sequence Оі, KBIQ is equivalent to EZQ when M = N, and we show in Section 3.1.2 that KBIQ is equivalent to OKQ whenM = +вҲһ and Оі = Пғ; as summarized in Table 1. We may also considerM to be finite but strictly larger than N. Yet, the theoretical analysis of these intermediate quadrature rules is beyond the scope of this work. In particular, for any sequence of positive numbers Оі we have вҲҖn вҲҲ [N], ЛҶwОі,N,П•n(x) = ЛҶwEZ,n(x). (22) Proposition 2. Let g вҲҲ EN , and let x вҲҲ XN such that Det ОәN (x) > 0. (23) Thanks to the invariance of ЛҶwОі,N,g with respect to Оі, we simplify the notation and we write ЛҶwEZ,g instead 4. Moreover, using this invariance, EZQ may be seen as an approximation of OKQ when g вҲҲ EN . Indeed, by approximating the kernel matrix K(x) вүҲ KN (x) where kN (x, y) =P nвҲҲ[N] ПғnП•n(x)П•n(y), we have K(x)вҲ’1Вөg(x) вүҲ ЛҶwEZ,g, (24) since KN (x)вҲ’1Вөg(x) = ЛҶwEZ,g by Proposition 2. Interestingly, this approximation is reminiscent to the one used in [23] in the case of the Gaussian kernel. 3.1.2 OKQ is a special case of KBIQ The optimal kernel quadrature is a special case of KBIQ when M = +вҲһ and Оі = Пғ. Indeed, in this case, we have ОәОі,M = k, and ВөОі,M g = Вөg, so that ЛҶwПғ,M,g(x) =K(x)вҲ’1Вөg(x) = ЛҶwOKQ,g. (25) In other words, Ermakov-Zolotukhin quadrature and the optimal kernel quadrature are extreme instances of interpolation based kernel quadrature that correspond to the regimes M = N and M = +вҲһ. As it was shown in Proposition 2, the weights of EZQ depend only on the eigenfunctions П•m and do not depend on the eigenvalues Пғm. This is to be compared to the weights of OKQ that depend simultaneously on the eigenvalues and the eigenfunctions. 3.2 Main theorem We give in this section the theoretical analysis of the worst case integration error of EZQ under the distribution of the projection DPP. We conclude that the convergence of EZQ under DPP is slower than the optimal rate O(ПғN+1), that was observed empirically for OKQ under DPP in [3] and proved theoretically for OKQ under CVS in [4], if we consider the worst-case integration error as a figure of merit. This is to be compared with the theoretical result of [14] that can not predict the difference in the rate of convergence between EZQ and OKQ: our analysis highlights the interest of using kernels when comparing quadratures. 3.3 Improved theoretical guarantees for the optimal kernel quadrature with DPPs Theorem 3 improves on the existing theoretical guarantees of the optimal kernel quadrature with determinantal point processes initially proposed in [3]. In the following section, we give numerical experiments illustrating this result. 5 Numerical experiments In this section, we illustrate the theoretical results presented in Section 3 in the case of the RKHS associated to the kernel ks(x, y) = 1 + X mвҲҲNвҲ— 1 m2s cos(2ПҖm(x вҲ’ y)), (47) that corresponds to the periodic Sobolev space of orders on [0, 1] [5], and we take Пү to be the uniform measure on X = [0, 1]. We compare the squared worst-case integration error of EZQ and OKQ and KBIQ, with M = 2N and Оі = Пғ, for x that follows the distribution of the projection DPP and for g вҲҲ {e1, e10, e20}. We take N вҲҲ [5, 100]. Figure 1 shows log-log plots of the squared error w.r.t. N, averaged over 1000 samples for each point, for s вҲҲ {2, 3}. We observe that the squared error of EZQ converges to 0 at the exact rate O(rN+1) predicted by Theorem 3, while the squared error of OKQ converges to 0 at the rate O(ПғN+1) as it was already observed in [3], which is still better than the rate O(rN+1) proved in Theorem 4. Finally, KBIQ (M = 2N and Оі = Пғ) converges to 0 at the rate O(ПғN+1). We conclude that, by taking M = ОұN with Оұ >1, KBIQ have practically the same averaged error as OKQ (M = +вҲһ). As we have mentioned before, the theoretical analysis of KBIQ in the regime when M is finite and strictly larger than N is beyond the scope of this work, and we defer it for future work. 96 Conclusion We studied the quadrature rule proposed by Ermakov and Zolotukhin through the lens of kernel methods. We proved that EZQ and OKQ belong to a larger class of quadrature rules that may be defined through kernel based interpolation. From this new perspective, EZQ may be seen as an approximation of OKQ. Moreover, we studied the expected value of the squared worst-case integration error of EZQ when the nodes follow the distribution of a DPP. In particular, we proved that EZQ converges to 0 at the rate O(rN+1) which is slower than the optimal rate O(ПғN+1) typically observed for OKQ with DPPs. This work shows the importance of the worst-case integration error as a figure of merit when comparing quadrature rules. Interestingly, we use our analysis of EZQ to improve upon the existing theoretical guarantees of OKQ under DPPs. Finally, we illustrated the theoretical results by some numerical experiments that hint that KBIQ in the regime M > Nmay have similar performances as OKQ. It would be interesting to study this broader class of quadratures in the future. Broader impact This article makes contributions to the fundamentals of numerical integration, and due to its theoretical nature, the author sees no ethical or immediate societal consequence of this work.
