llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses the computational bottleneck of integration in Riemannian statistics by introducing Bayesian quadrature (BQ) tailored to data-driven Riemannian manifolds. Proposes a directional cumulative variance (DCV) acquisition function that selects informative directions (rays) in the tangent space to minimize expensive exponential-map evaluations. Adopts warped BQ (WSABI-L/M) to encode integrand positivity and improve modeling of large dynamic ranges. Adds a transfer-learning scheme reusing nodes when only the covariance of the integration measure changes during LAND optimization. Demonstrates consistent accuracy gains and speedups (up to ~20x vs. Monte Carlo) on synthetic and real manifolds, including MNIST (with a VAE-induced metric) and a molecular dynamics application (adenylate kinase).
llm_extracted_info.methodology: 1) Manifold construction: Learn data-driven Riemannian metrics M(x). Two options: (a) nonparametric kernel metric (inverse local diagonal covariance) with hyperparameters Пғ (curvature scale) and ПҒ (far-field volume), and (b) a surrogate metric in a VAE latent space using the aggregated posterior qПҶ(x) with M(x) = (qПҶ(x)+ПҒ)^(-2/D) I for efficiency vs. pullback metrics. 2) Riemannian normal (LAND): Density p(x) вҲқ exp(-1/2 вҹЁLog_Ој(x), ОЈ^{-1} Log_Ој(x)вҹ©). The normalizing constant C(Ој,ОЈ) is intractable; move integration to tangent space via x = Exp_Ој(v) to integrate g_Ој(v) under ПҖ(v)=N(0,ОЈ), where g_Ој(v) is the Riemannian volume element at Exp_Ој(v). 3) Bayesian quadrature: Place a GP prior on the square-root warped integrand f = вҲҡ(2(g-Оҙ)) (WSABI-L/M) to enforce positivity and reduce dynamic range; derive approximate posterior mean/variance for g and compute the integral under Gaussian ПҖ. Use RBF or MatГ©rn-5/2 kernels; set prior mean to far-field volume when known. 4) Directional Cumulative Variance (DCV): Optimize over unit directions r on the sphere S^{D-1} to maximize вҲ«_0^вҲһ Var[g(ОІr)] ПҖ(ОІr)^2 dОІ; compute via numerical quadrature (Simpson rule), manifold optimization with line-search; after one expensive Exp_Ој(Оұ r), cheaply evaluate multiple ОІr along the ray using WSABI uncertainty sampling. 5) Transfer learning across LAND iterations: Reuse observations when ОЈ changes but Ој stays fixed, since g_Ој is unchanged; collect few new nodes to adapt to new ПҖ. 6) LAND optimization: Alternate updates of Ој and ОЈ. Ој via steepest-descent direction requiring vector-valued integrals from BQ; ОЈ via gradient descent on the SPD manifold with the bi-invariant metric using Pymanopt; compute geodesics using a fast fixed-point (FP) solver to initialize a precise SciPy BVP solver; exponential maps via IVP. GP integrals for gradients/emulation estimated with MC over the GP posterior mean (e.g., 30k samples).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: - Datasets/manifolds: Synthetic 2D datasets (CIRCLE, CURLY, 2-CIRCLES) and embedded higher-dimensional variants (3D, 4D, 5D) via random orthonormal projections plus noise; MNIST (first three digits) with a 2D VAE latent space (5,504 points; VAE architecture specified; aggregated-posterior metric with ПҒ=0.001); Molecular dynamics (adenylate kinase, DIMS subset, 2,038 conformations; original 10,023D coordinates reduced by PCA; kernel metric Пғ=0.035, ПҒ=1e-5). - Integrals: Normalizing constants C(Ој,ОЈ) arising throughout LAND optimization; g_Ој evaluated via Exp_Ој(v); Gaussian measure ПҖ(v)=N(0,ОЈ). - Baselines/methods: MC vs. BQ variants WSABI-L, WSABI-M, and DCV (WSABI-L with directional acquisition). - Protocol 1 (full LAND fit): Record every integral across optimization; 16 independent runs per method; ground truth integrals estimated with MC S=40,000; fixed BQ sample budgets (WSABI-L/M: 80 new nodes when Ој updates, 10 when ОЈ updates; DCV: 18 and 2 exponential maps respectively, selecting 6 points along each ray). MC receives the runtime budget of the slowest BQ method per problem. - Protocol 2 (first integral only): Error vs. wall-clock time; 30 runtime limits from 5вҖ“65s; 30 runs per limit; report mean error and 95% CIs. - Scale: 43,920 BQ integrations total. Mean exponential-map times reported (e.g., MNIST вүҲ238 ms). Results show BQ consistently outperforming MC; DCV best when exponential maps are expensive (e.g., MNIST вүҲ20x speedup over MC for similar accuracy).
llm_extracted_info.limitations: - Geodesic computations remain expensive and can fail on high-curvature manifolds; BVP solvers are sensitive and may be slow or unstable for long curves. The method chains an approximate FP solver with a precise BVP solver, but robustness still depends on problem geometry. - Assumptions/simplifications: Treat RD as a global chart (geodesic completeness assumed); Log and Exp not globally inverse in high curvature; LAND optimization favors regions where this is less problematic but it remains an approximation. - Metric learning issues: Kernel metric suffers in higher dimensions (curse of dimensionality); VAE aggregated-posterior metric can induce high curvature; diagonal/isotropic surrogate metrics may miss anisotropy. - DCV drawbacks: Optimizing DCV is costly (requires posterior gradients and numerical integration) and selecting along rays may reduce spatial coverage; benefits depend on expensive exponential-map regimes. - GP modeling/integration: Uses GP emulation with numerical quadrature (MC over GP posterior mean), not fully closed-form; performance depends on kernel choices and hyperparameter tuning. - Empirical scope: Evaluations mostly in low (2D) latent spaces or PCA subspaces; limited quantitative domain metrics for molecular dynamics; small set of datasets; occasional outlier failures reported for DCV. - Transfer learning limited to ОЈ updates; changes in Ој require new expensive evaluations.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Develop more robust and faster geodesic solvers with probabilistic ODE methods; integrate PN solvers for BVP/IVP to quantify and propagate solver uncertainty into LAND learning. - Theoretical analysis and convergence guarantees for DCV; derive cheaper-to-evaluate directional objectives and batch/parallel variants. - Richer manifold metrics: scalable high-dimensional metrics with curvature control; anisotropic surrogate metrics in VAEs; learned metrics that balance curvature and computational tractability. - Broader Riemannian PN pipeline: combine PN methods for geodesics, integration, optimization, and sampling; propagate uncertainty across stages for end-to-end robust Riemannian inference. - Transfer and multi-task BQ: Jointly estimate families of related integrals across LAND iterations/components; exploit correlations between integrals; batch acquisition strategies. - Alternative warps/priors and kernels tailored to high curvature and sharp volume growth; adaptive/heteroscedastic GP models. - Applications: quantitative validation in molecular dynamics (e.g., kinetics, free-energy landscapes), more complex datasets (e.g., high-D latent manifolds), and other Riemannian tasks (FrГ©chet means, regression, classification on manifolds). - Address global noninvertibility/topological challenges (manifolds with boundaries or multiple charts) and extend to known-prior manifolds (spheres, tori) with specialized kernels and priors.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
Scope mapping between Method and Repository:
- Implemented here (corresponds to Method item 3): Bayesian quadrature with a square-root warped integrand (WSABI-L). The code provides:
  - WsabiLGP: a warped GP using the WSABI-L approximation with alpha = 0.8 * min(observed_Y).
  - IntegrandModel: multiplies the warped GP by a prior (Gaussian) and exposes posterior mean/variance, their Jacobian/Hessian.
  - Closed-form mean of the integral for Gaussian prior Г— WSABI-L with RBF kernel (quadrature._compute_mean for (Gaussian, WsabiLGP, RBF)).
  - Acquisition utilities based on model variance and its gradient/Hessian, plus batch selection (local penalisation, kriging believer/optimist) to select evaluation nodes.
  - Kernel derivatives (RBF) and calculus helpers to compute gradients/Hessians required by acquisition/optimisation.

- Not implemented in this repository (from Method):
  - Manifold construction with data-driven Riemannian metrics; LAND density and geodesic Log/Exp maps; DCV objective; transfer across LAND iterations; SPD-metric optimisation for ОЈ. These are out of scope here.

Experimental settings (from examples in the repository) to reproduce WSABI-L BQ experiments:
1) 1D example (examples/example_1d.py):
- True function: f(x) = ((sin(x) + 0.5*cos(3x))^2) / ((x/2)^2 + 0.3)
- Initial design: X0 = [[-3]] (shape (1,1)); initial GP targets: Y0 = sqrt(2 * f(X0))
- GP kernel: RBF with variance = 2.0, lengthscale = 2.0
- Likelihood (noise): Gaussian with variance = 1e-10 (essentially noiseless)
- Warping: WSABI-L (square-root warping with alpha = 0.8 * min(Y_observed) updated when data changes)
- Prior over x (for integration): Gaussian(mean = [0], covariance = [[2]])
- Acquisition/batching: Local Penalisation
  - Soft-min p-norm parameter p = 6
  - Local gradient maximisation uses num_local_initial_points = 10 * D per selected point
  - Multi-start global optimiser uses num_initial_points = 10 * D sampled from the prior
  - Optimiser: scipy.optimize.minimize with BFGS, jac=True, gtol = 1e-2 (see DEFAULT_MINIMIZER_KWARGS)
- Batch configuration: BATCHES = 10, BATCH_SIZE = 3
- Domain for plotting only: [-4, 4], resolution = 2000
- Hyperparameter optimisation: gpy_gp.optimize() called after each batch
- Integral estimate: model.integral_mean() using the closed-form for WSABI-L + RBF + Gaussian prior
- Notes:
  - When updating the model with newly selected points X, targets used are the unwarped true function values Y = f(X); WsabiLGP applies the warp internally to feed the GP.

2) 2D example (examples/example_2d.py):
- True function: separable product across dimensions of the same 1D form: prod_j (((sin(x_j) + 0.5*cos(3x_j))^2)/((x_j/2)^2+0.3))
- Initial design: X0 = [[0, 0]]; Y0 = sqrt(2 * f(X0))
- GP kernel: RBF with variance = 2.0, lengthscale = 2.0
- Likelihood (noise): Gaussian with variance = 1e-10
- Warping: WSABI-L
- Prior over x: Gaussian(mean = [0, 0], covariance = 2 * I_2)
- Acquisition/batching: Local Penalisation (same defaults as 1D)
- Batch configuration: BATCHES = 25, BATCH_SIZE = 4
- Domain for plotting: square [-4, 4]^2, resolution = 200 per axis
- Hyperparameter optimisation: gpy_gp.optimize() after each batch
- Reporting: prints model.integral_mean() each iteration

Key algorithmic details/settings embedded in the code:
- Warping constant alpha for WSABI-L: alpha = 0.8 * min(observed_Y) (gps.WsabiLGP)
- Posterior variance of WSABI-L model at x: V(x) = C_GP(x) * m_GP(x)^2
- Product model with prior ПҖ(x):
  - Mean: E[g(x)] = E_warped(x) * ПҖ(x)
  - Variance: Var[g(x)] = Var_warped(x) * ПҖ(x)^2
  - Jacobian/Hessian computed via helpers for f^2 * g (maths_helpers)
- Closed-form integral mean (quadrature._compute_mean for (Gaussian, WsabiLGP, RBF)) uses:
  - A = K_D^{-1} Y_D from the GP posterior
  - ОҪ = (x_i + x_j)/2 for data pairs
  - C = ОЈ^{-1} + 2 I / в„“^2, Оі = C^{-1}(2ОҪ/в„“^2 + ОЈ^{-1}Ој)
  - K, L matrices and determinant terms as in the provided code
- Acquisition: uses model variance (with optional gradient and Hessian) as the objective; local penalisation constructs cones around selected points with slope = 0.5 * max local gradient magnitude; soft-min aggregation in log space guards against zeros/overflow.

Dependencies and environment:
- Python >= 3.5
- pip packages: numpy, scipy, GPy, multimethod, matplotlib (for examples/plots)

Limitations and notes:
- Kernel derivatives and closed-form integral currently specialised for RBF kernels. Other kernels like MatГ©rn-5/2 are not implemented here.
- Only WSABI-L (linearisation) is implemented; WSABI-M is not included.
- This repository focuses on Bayesian quadrature with warped GP; it does not include manifold/LAND/DCV components from the broader Method description.

research_study_list.title: Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations: The Lie Brackets Make A Difference
research_study_list.abstract:
The vector field of a controlled differential equation (CDE) describes the
relationship between a control path and the evolution of a solution path.
Neural CDEs (NCDEs) treat time series data as observations from a control path,
parameterise a CDE's vector field using a neural network, and use the solution
path as a continuously evolving hidden state. As their formulation makes them
robust to irregular sampling rates, NCDEs are a powerful approach for modelling
real-world data. Building on neural rough differential equations (NRDEs), we
introduce Log-NCDEs, a novel, effective, and efficient method for training
NCDEs. The core component of Log-NCDEs is the Log-ODE method, a tool from the
study of rough paths for approximating a CDE's solution. Log-NCDEs are shown to
outperform NCDEs, NRDEs, the linear recurrent unit, S5, and MAMBA on a range of
multivariate time series datasets with up to $50{,}000$ observations.
research_study_list.full_text: Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations: The Lie Brackets Make a Difference Benjamin Walker1 Andrew D. McLeod 1 Tiexin Qin 2 Yichuan Cheng 2 Haoliang Li 2 Terry Lyons1 Abstract The vector field of a controlled differential equa- tion (CDE) describes the relationship between a control path and the evolution of a solution path. Neural CDEs (NCDEs) treat time series data as observations from a control path, parameterise a CDEвҖҷs vector field using a neural network, and use the solution path as a continuously evolving hidden state. As their formulation makes them robust to irregular sampling rates, NCDEs are a powerful approach for modelling real-world data. Building on neural rough differential equations (NRDEs), we introduce Log-NCDEs, a novel, ef- fective, and efficient method for training NCDEs. The core component of Log-NCDEs is the Log- ODE method, a tool from the study of rough paths for approximating a CDEвҖҷs solution. Log-NCDEs are shown to outperform NCDEs, NRDEs, the lin- ear recurrent unit, S5, and MAMBA on a range of multivariate time series datasets with up to50,000 observations. 1. Introduction 1.1. Multivariate Time Series Modelling Neural controlled differential equations (NCDEs) offer a number of advantages for modelling real-world multivariate time series. These include being robust to irregular sampling rates and decoupling the number of forward passes through their neural network from the number of observations in the time series. However, there exists a gap in performance between NCDEs and current state-of-the-art approaches for time series modelling, such as S5, the linear recurrent unit (LRU), and MAMBA (Smith et al., 2023; Orvieto et al., 2023; Gu & Dao, 2023). 1Mathematical Institute, University of Oxford, UK 2Department of Electrical Engineering, City University of Hong Kong. Correspondence to: Benjamin Walker <mlbenjamin walker@gmail.com>. Proceedings of the 41 st International Conference on Machine Learning, Vienna, Austria. PMLR 235, 2024. Copyright 2024 by the author(s). This paper demonstrates that, on a range of multivariate time series benchmarks, NCDEs can outperform current state-of-the-art approaches by utilising a tool from the study of rough paths, the Log-ODE method. We refer to this new approach as Log-NCDEs1. 1.2. Neural Controlled Differential Equations Let {(ti, xi)}n i=0 denote a set of observations from a mul- tivariate time series, where xi вҲҲ RvвҲ’1. Let X : [t0, tn] вҶ’ Rv be a continuous interpolation, such that Xti = (ti, xi), where subscript on time-dependent variables denotes evalua- tion. Let ht вҲҲ Ru and zt вҲҲ Rw be the NCDEвҖҷs hidden state and output at time t respectively. Let ОҫП• : Rv вҶ’ Ru and fОё : Ru вҶ’ RuГ—v be neural networks, and lПҲ : Ru вҶ’ Rw be a linear map, where П•, Оё, and ПҲ represent the learnable parameters. A NCDE is defined by ht0 = ОҫП•(t0, x0), ht = ht0 + Z t t0 fОё(hs)dXs, zt = lПҲ(ht), (1) for t вҲҲ [t0, tn], where fОё(hs)dXs is matrix-vector multi- plication (Kidger et al., 2020). Details on the regularity required for existence and uniqueness of the solution to (1) can be found in Appendix A.1. By an extension of the Picard-LindelВЁof Theorem, a sufficient condition is X be- ing of bounded variation and fОё being Lipschitz continuous (Lyons et al., 2007, Theorem 1.3). NCDEs are an attractive option for modelling multivariate time series. They are universal approximators of continu- ous real-valued functions on time series data (Kidger, 2022, Theorem 3.9). Additionally, since they interact with time series data through X, NCDEs are unaware of when the time series was observed. This makes them robust to irreg- ular sampling rates. Furthermore, the number of forward passes through fОё when evaluating (1) is controlled by the differential equation solver. This is opposed to recurrent models, where it is controlled by the number of observations n. By decoupling the number of forward passes through 1https://github.com/Benjamin-Walker/ Log-Neural-CDEs 1 arXiv:2402.18512v3  [cs.LG]  28 Oct 2024Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations their neural network from the number of observations in the time series, NCDEs can mitigate exploding or vanishing gradients on highly-sampled time series. To make use of the techniques developed for training neural ordinary differential equations (ODEs) (Chen et al., 2018), NCDEs are typically rewritten as an ODE, Лңht = Лңht0 + Z t t0 gОё,X (Лңhs)ds, (2) where Лңht0 = ht0 . Kidger et al. (2020) proposed taking X to be a differentiable interpolation and gОё,X (Лңhs) = fОё(Лңhs)dX ds . (3) 1.3. Neural Rough Differential Equations Neural rough differential equations (NRDEs) are based on the Log-ODE method, which is discussed briefly here, and in full detail in Section 2. Given a set of intervals {[ri, ri+1]}mвҲ’1 i=0 satisfying t0 = r0 < . . . < rm = tn, the Log-ODE method replaces a CDE with an ODE on each interval. For a depthвҲ’N method, the ODE on [ri, ri+1] is defined by gОё,X (Лңhs) = ВҜfОё  Лңhs  log(SN (X)[ri,ri+1]) ri+1 вҲ’ ri , (4) where ВҜfОё is constructed using the iterated Lie brackets of fОё and log(SN (X)[ri,ri+1]) is the depth-N truncated log- signature of X over [ri, ri+1] (Boutaib et al., 2013). Infor- mally, ВҜfОё is a high order description of the vector field fОё and log(SN (X)[ri,ri+1]) is a high order description of the control path X over [ri, ri+1]. Note that when using (3), Лңht is exactly ht for all t вҲҲ [t0, tn], whereas when using (4), Лңht is an approximation of ht when t вҲҲ {ri}m i=1. NRDEs replace (3) with (4), but instead of calculating ВҜfОё using the iterated Lie brackets of fОё, it is treated as a neu- ral network ВҜfОё : Ru вҶ’ RuГ—ОІ(v,N), where ОІ(v, N) is the dimension of a depthвҲ’N truncated log-signature of a v di- mensional path. By neglecting the structure of ВҜfОё, NRDEs are able to reduce the computational burden of evaluating the vector field, at the cost of increasing the output dimen- sion of the neural network. Compared to NCDEs, NRDEs can reduce the number of forward passes through the network while evaluating the model, as the vector field is autonomous on each interval [ri, ri+1]. This has been shown to lead to improved classifi- cation accuracy, alongside reduced time and memory-usage, on time series with up to 17,000 observations (Morrill et al., 2021). Furthermore, as it is no longer necessary to apply a differentiable interpolation to the time series data, NRDEs are applicable to a wider range of input signals. 1.4. Contributions This paper introduces Log-NCDEs, which build on NRDEs by constructing ВҜfОё using the iterated Lie brackets of a NCDEвҖҷs vector field, fОё. For depthвҖҷs N вүҘ 2, this change drastically reduces the output dimension of the modelвҖҷs neural network, without impacting the modelвҖҷs expressiv- ity. Furthermore, it improves model performance on every dataset considered in this paper. Calculating the Lie brackets requires that fОё satisfies a regularity constraint, specifically being Lip(Оі) for Оі вҲҲ (N вҲ’ 1, N]. Section 3.2 presents a novel theoretical result bounding the Lip(Оі)вҲ’norm for a class of fully connected neural networks when 1 < ОівүӨ 2. Following this, Section 3.3 details how to efficiently cal- culate the Lie brackets of a Lip(Оі) neural network using standard machine learning tools. The paper concludes by showing that, over a range of multivariate time series bench- marks, Log-NCDEs outperform NCDEs, NRDEs, S5, LRU, and MAMBA. 2. Mathematical Background The following section provides a thorough mathematical de- scription of the Log-ODE method. It will introduce Lip(Оі) regularity, the Lie bracket of two vector fields, and the sig- nature and log-signature of a path, alongside their respective spaces, the tensor algebra and the free Lie algebra. 2.1. The Tensor Algebra Definition 2.1. Let U, V , and W be vector spaces. The ten- sor product space U вҠ— V is the unique (up to isomorphism) space such that for all bilinear functions Оә : U Г— V вҶ’ W there exists a unique linear map П„ : U вҠ— V вҶ’ W, such that Оә = П„ в—Ұ вҠ—(Roman, 2007, Chapter 14). As an example, let V = R2 and W = R3. In this case, the tensor product is the outer product of the two vectors, and the resulting tensor product space is the space of 2 Г— 3 matrices, R2 вҠ— R3 = R2Г—3. The tensor product of v вҲҲ R2 and w вҲҲ R3 is defined by v вҠ— w =  a b  вҠ— пЈ® пЈ° c d e пЈ№ пЈ» =  ac ad ae bc bd be  , (5) where any bilinear function Оә(v, w) can be written as a linear function П„(v вҠ— w). Definition 2.2. The tensor algebra space is the space T((V )) = {x = (x0, x1, . . .)|xi вҲҲ V вҠ—i}, (6) where V вҠ—0 = R, V вҠ—1 = V , and V вҠ—j = V вҠ— V вҠ—jвҲ’1 (Roman, 2007, Chapter 14). Details on the choice of norm forV вҠ—i when V is a complete 2Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations normed vector space, i.e. a Banach space, can be found in Appendix A.2. 2.2. Lip(Оі) Functions Let V and W be Banach spaces and L(V, W) denote the space of linear mappings from V to W equipped with the operator norm. Definition 2.3. A linear map l вҲҲ L(V вҠ—j, W) is j symmet- ric if for all v1 вҠ— В·В·В· вҠ—vj вҲҲ V вҠ—j and all bijective functions p : {1, . . . , j} вҶ’ {1, . . . , j} (Roman, 2007, Chapter 14), l(v1 вҠ— . . .вҠ— vj) = l(vp(1) вҠ— В·В·В· вҠ—vp(j)). (7) Let Ls(V вҠ—j, W) denote the set of all j symmetric linear maps. Definition 2.4. Let Оі >0, k вҲҲ Z such that Оі вҲҲ (k, k+ 1], F be a closed subset of V , and f0 : F вҶ’ W. For j вҲҲ {1, . . . , k}, let fj : F вҶ’ Ls(V вҠ—j, W). The collection (f0, f1, . . . , fk) is an element ofLip(Оі) if there existsM вүҘ 0 such that for j вҲҲ {0, . . . , k}, sup xвҲҲF ||fj(x)||L(V вҠ—j,W) вүӨ M, (8) and for j вҲҲ {0, . . . , k}, all x, yвҲҲ F, and each v вҲҲ V вҠ—j (Stein, 1970),        fj(y)(v) вҲ’ PkвҲ’j l=0 fj+l(x)(vвҠ—(yвҲ’x)вҠ—l) l!         W |x вҲ’ y|ОівҲ’j V вүӨ M. (9) If f = (f0, f1, . . . , fk) вҲҲ Lip(Оі), then the Lip(Оі)вҲ’norm, denoted ||f||Lip(Оі), is the smallest M for which (8) and (9) hold. When 0 < Оі вүӨ 1, then k = 0 and f0 вҲҲ Lip(Оі) implies f0 is bounded and ОівҲ’HВЁolder continuous. When Оі = 1, then f0 is bounded and Lipschitz. In this paper, we are concerned with the regularity of vector fields on Ru. In this case, f вҲҲ Lip(Оі) for Оі вҲҲ (k, k+ 1] implies that f is bounded, has k bounded derivatives, and the kth derivative satisfies ||Dkf(y) вҲ’ Dkf(x)|| вүӨM|x вҲ’ y|ОівҲ’k, (10) for all x, yвҲҲ Ru. 2.3. The Free Lie Algebra Definition 2.5. A Lie algebra is a vector space V with a bilinear map [В·, В·] : V Г— V вҶ’ V satisfying [w, w] = 0 and the Jacobi identity, [[x, y], z] + [[y, z], x] + [[z, x], y] = 0, (11) for all w, x, y, zвҲҲ V . The map [В·, В·] is called the Lie bracket (Reutenauer, 1993). Any associative algebra, (V, Г—), has a Lie bracket structure with Lie bracket defined by [x, y] = x Г— y вҲ’ y Г— x, (12) for all x, yвҲҲ V . For example, V = RnГ—n with the matrix product. For another example, consider the vector space of infinitely differentiable functions from Ru to Ru with pointwise addition, denoted CвҲһ(Ru, Ru). This space is a Lie algebra when equipped with the Lie bracket [a, b](x) = Ja(x)b(x) вҲ’ Jb(x)a(x), (13) for a, bвҲҲ CвҲһ(Ru, Ru) and x вҲҲ Ru, where Ja(x) вҲҲ RuГ—u is the Jacobian of a with entries given by Jij a (x) = вҲӮjai(x) for i, jвҲҲ {1, . . . , u} (Kirillov, 2008). Definition 2.6. Let A be a non-empty set, L0 be a Lie algebra, and П• : A вҶ’ L0 be a map. The Lie algebra L0 is said to be the free Lie algebra generated by A if for any Lie algebra L and any map f : A вҶ’ L, there exists a unique Lie algebra homomorphism g : L0 вҶ’ L such that g в—ҰП• = f (Reutenauer, 1993). The free Lie algebra generated by V is the space L((V )) = {(l0, l1, . . .) : li вҲҲ Li}, (14) where L0 = 0, L1 = V , and Li+1 is the span of [v, l] for v вҲҲ V and l вҲҲ Li. 2.4. The Log-Signature Let X : [t0, tn] вҶ’ V have bounded variation and define Xn [t0,tn] = Z В·В·В· Z | {z } u1<В·В·В·<un uiвҲҲ[t0,tn] dXu1 вҠ— В·В·В· вҠ—dXun вҲҲ V вҠ—n. (15) The signature of the path X is S(X)[t0,tn] = (1, X1 [t0,tn], . . . , Xn [t0,tn], . . .) вҲҲ ЛңT((V )), (16) where ЛңT((V )) = {x вҲҲ T((V ))|x = (1 , . . .)} (Lyons et al., 2007). The depth-N truncated signature is defined as SN (X)[t0,tn] = (1, X1 [t0,tn], . . . , XN [t0,tn]) вҲҲ ЛңTN (V ). The signature is an infinite dimensional vector which describes the path X over the interval [t0, tn]. In fact, assuming X contains time as a channel, linear maps on S(X)[t0,tn] are universal approximators for continuous, real-valued func- tions of X (Lyons, 2014; Arribas, 2018). This property of the signature relies on the shuffle-product identity, which states that polynomials of the elements in a truncated signa- ture can be rewritten as linear maps on the signature trun- cated at greater depth (Ree, 1958; Chevyrev & Kormilitzin, 2016). A consequence of the shuffle-product identity is that not every term in the signature provides new information about the path X. The transformation which removes the information redundancy is the logarithm. 3Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations Figure 1.A schematic diagram of the Log-ODE method. Definition 2.7. For x вҲҲ ЛңT((V )), the logarithm is defined by log(x) = log(1 + t) = вҲһX n=1 (вҲ’1)n n tвҠ—n, (17) where t = (0, x1, x2, . . .) (Reutenauer, 1993). It was shown by Chen (1957) that log(S(X)[t0,tn]) вҲҲ L((V )). (18) This result plays a crucial role in the Log-ODE method. 2.5. The Log-ODE Method The vector field of a CDE is typically thought of as a matrix- valued function f : Ru вҶ’ RuГ—v. An equivalent formu- lation is f being a linear map acting on dX вҲҲ Rv and returning a vector field on Ru. In other words, f(В·)dX : Ru вҶ’ Ru. This formulation will prove more useful in the following section. Let X : [t0, tn] вҶ’ Rv be a continuous path. The (truncated) log-signature is a map which takes X to the (truncated) free Lie algebra LN (Rv). If f(В·)dX is restricted to smooth vector fields on Ru, then f(В·)dX is a linear map from Rv to the Lie algebra CвҲһ(Ru, Ru). By definition 2.6, there exists a unique linear map ВҜf from LN (Rv) to the smooth vector fields on Ru. Figure 1 is a schematic diagram of this relationship. Since ВҜf is a Lie algebra homomorphism, it can be defined recursively by ВҜf(В·)a = f(В·)a, a вҲҲ Rv (19) and ВҜf(В·)[a, b] = [ ВҜf(В·)a, ВҜf(В·)b] (20) for [a, b] вҲҲ LN (Rv). Over an interval, the Log-ODE method approximates a CDE using an autonomous ODE constructed by applying the lin- ear map ВҜf to the truncated log-signature of the control, as seen in (21) (Lyons, 2014). There exist theoretical results bounding the error in the Log-ODE methodвҖҷs approxima- tion, including when the control and solution paths live in infinite dimensional Banach spaces (Boutaib et al., 2013). However, for a given set of intervals, the series of vector fields {gX(В·)}вҲһ N=1 is not guaranteed to converge. In prac- tice, N is typically chosen as the smallest N such that a reasonably sized set of intervals {ri}m i=0 gives an approx- imation error of the desired level. A recent development has been the introduction of an algorithm which adaptively updates N and {ri}m i=0 (Bayer et al., 2023). 3. Method 3.1. Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations Log-NCDEs use the same underlying model as NRDEs ht = ht0 + Z t t0 gОё,X (hs)ds, gОё,X (hs) = ВҜfОё (hs) log(SN (X)[ri,ri+1]) ri+1 вҲ’ ri , (21) but with two major changes. First, instead of parameterising ВҜfОё using a neural network, it is constructed using the iterated Lie brackets of a NCDEвҖҷs neural network, fОё, via (19) and (20). Second, fОё is ensured to be a Lip(Оі) function for Оі вҲҲ (N вҲ’ 1, N]. Figure 2 is a schematic diagram of a Log-NCDE. These changes have a major benefit. For N > 1, Log- NCDEs are exploring a drastically smaller output space during training than NRDEs, while maintaining the same expressivity, as NCDEs are universal approximators. This is because the output dimension of fОё is u Г— v, whereas the output dimension of ВҜfОё is u Г— ОІ(v, N). Figure 3 compares these values for paths of dimension v from 1 to 15 and truncation depths of N = 1 and N = 2. This benefit comes at the cost of needing to calculate the iterated Lie brackets when evaluating Log-NCDEs, which will be quantified in Section 3.4 and explored empirically in Section 5.2. When N = 1, (21) simplifies to gОё,X (hs) = fОё (hs) Xri+1 вҲ’ Xri ri+1 вҲ’ ri . (22) Hence, in this case the only difference between Log-NCDEs and NRDEs is the regularisation of fОё. Furthermore, (22) and (3) are equivalent when X is a linear interpolation. Therefore, the approach of NCDEs, NRDEs, and Log- NCDEs coincide when using a depthвҲ’1 Log-ODE approxi- mation (Morrill et al., 2021). 3.2. Lip(Оі) Neural Networks The composition of two Lip(Оі) functions is Lip(Оі) (Cass et al., 2012, Lemma 2.2). Hence, a simple approach to ensur- ing a fully connected neural network (FCNN) isLip(Оі) is to make each layer Lip(Оі). This can be achieved by choosing an infinitely differentiable activation function, such asSiLU (Elfwing et al., 2018). However, in practice this may not 4Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations Figure 2.A schematic diagram of the training loop for a Log-NCDE. The coloured circles labelled data observations represent irregular samples from a time series. The purple line labelled system response is a potentially time varying label one wants to predict. The log-signatures of the data observations over each interval [ri, ri+1] are combined with the iterated Lie brackets of fОё to produce the vector field gОё,X from (21). The pink dashed line represents the solution of (1) and the solid black line represents the approximation obtained by solving (21). A linear map lПҲ gives the Log-NCDEвҖҷs prediction and a loss function L(В·, В·) is used to update fОёвҖҷs parameters. Figure 3.A plot of ОІ(v, N) against v for N = 1, 2. The output dimension of a NRDEвҖҷs neural network isRuГ—ОІ(v,N), whereas for a Log-NCDE it is RuГ—v. ensure sufficient regularity, as demonstrated by Theorem 3.1. Theorem 3.1. Let fОё be a FCNN with input dimension nin, hidden dimension nh, depth m, and activation function SiLU. Assuming the input x = [ x1, . . . , xnin]T satisfies |xj| вүӨ1 for j = 1, . . . , nin, then fОё вҲҲ Lip(2) and ||fОё||Lip(2) вүӨ CPm! � {||Wi||2, ||bi||2}m i=1  (23) where C is a constant depending on nin, nh, and m, {Wi}m i=1 and {bi}m i=1 are the weights and biases of ith layer of fОё, and Pm! is a polynomial of order m!. Proof. A proof is given in Appendix B. Assuming that each layer {Li}m i=1 of fОё satisfies ||Li||Lip(2) = 1, an explicit evaluation of (23) gives ||fОё||Lip(2) вүӨ 52mвҲ’1вҲ’1. (24) For a depth 7 FCNN, this bound is greater than the max- imum value of a single precision floating point number. Hence, it may be necessary to control ||fОё||Lip(2) explicitly during training. This is achieved by modifying the neural networkвҖҷs loss function L to L 7вҶ’ L + О»  mX i=1 ||Wi||2 + ||bi||2 ! , (25) where О» is a hyperparameter controlling the weight of the penalty. This is an example of weight regularisation, which has long been understood to improve generalisation in NNs (Hinton, 1987; Krogh & Hertz, 1991). Equation 25 is specif- ically a variation of spectral norm regularisation (Yoshida & Miyato, 2017). 3.3. Constructing the Log-ODE Vector Field The linear map ВҜfОё in (21) is defined recursively by (19) and (20). Assuming fОё(В·)a is infinitely differentiable, then fОё(В·)a is an element of the Lie algebra CвҲһ(Ru, Ru) and [fОё(В·)a, fОё(В·)b] = JfОё(В·)afОё(В·)b вҲ’ JfОё(В·)bfОё(В·)a, (26) as discussed in Section 2.3. Let {ej}v j=1 be the usual basis of Rv. A choice of basis forLN (Rv) is a Hall basis, denoted 5Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations {ЛҶek}ОІ(v,N) k=1 , which is a specific subset of up to the(N вҲ’1)th iterated Lie brackets of {ej}v j=1 (Hall, 1950). Rewriting (21) using a Hall basis, ВҜfОё (hs) log(SN (X)[ri,ri+1]) ri+1 вҲ’ ri = ОІ(v,N)X k=1 О»k ВҜfОё(hs)ЛҶek, (27) where О»k is the term in the scaled log-signature correspond- ing to the basis element ЛҶek. Since each ЛҶek can be written as iterated Lie brackets of {ej}v j=1, it is possible to replace ВҜfОё(В·)ЛҶek with the iterated Lie brackets of fОё(В·)ei using (19) and (20). Each fОё(В·)ei : Ru вҶ’ Ru is a vector field defined by the ith column of the neural networkвҖҷs output. Hence, gОё,X can be evaluated at a point using iterated Jacobian- vector products (JVPs) of fОё. 3.4. Computational Cost When the signature truncation depth N is greater than 1, NRDEs and Log-NCDEs incur an additional computational cost for each evaluation of the vector field, which we quan- tify here for N = 2. Assume that a NCDE, NRDE, and Log-NCDE are all using an identical FCNN as their vec- tor field, except for the dimension of the final layer in the NRDE. Let m and nh be the depth and dimension of the FCNNвҖҷs hidden layers respectively, andu and v be the di- mension of ht and X from (1) respectively. Letting Fx be the number of FLOPs to evaluate model xвҖҷs vector field, FNCDE = 2unh + 2(m вҲ’ 1)n2 h + 2uvnh, FNRDE = 2unh + 2(m вҲ’ 1)n2 h + u(v2 вҲ’ v)nh, FLog-NCDE = 3vFNCDE, (28) where the number of FLOPs to calculate a JVP is 3 times that of evaluating the FCNN and v JVPs of fОё are needed to evaluate (27) when N = 2 (Griewank & Walther, 2008, Chapter 4). Log-NCDEs and NRDEs have the same asymp- totic computational complexity in each variable. However, each JVP is evaluated at the same point hs. This allows the computational burden of Log-NCDEs on high-dimensional time series to be reduced by constructing a batched function using JaxвҖҷs vmap (Bradbury et al., 2018). The computa- tional cost is evaluated empirically in Section 5.2. 3.5. Limitations In this paper, we only consider Log-NCDEs which use a depthвҲ’1 or depthвҲ’2 Log-ODE approximation. This is due to the following two limitations. First, there are no the- oretical results explicitly bounding the Lip(Оі) norm of a neural network for Оі >2. Second, the computational cost required to evaluate gОё,X grows rapidly with the depth N. This can makeN >2 computationally infeasible, especially for high-dimensional time series. Another general limita- tion of NCDEs is the need to solve the differential equation Figure 4.An example path from the toy dataset, where each colour represents a channel in the path. recursively, preventing parallelisation. This is in contrast to non-selective structured state-space models, whose un- derlying model is a differential equation that can be solved parallel in time (Gu et al., 2022). 4. Experiments 4.1. Baseline Methods Log-NCDEs are compared against six models, which rep- resent the state-of-the-art for a range of deep learning ap- proaches to time series modelling. Four of these models are stacked recurrent models, which use the same general architecture, but with different recurrent layers. The archi- tecture used in this paper is based on the one introduced by Smith et al. (2023) and the four different recurrent lay- ers considered are LRU, S5, MAMBA, and S6, the selec- tive state-space recurrence introduced as a component of MAMBA (Orvieto et al., 2023; Smith et al., 2023; Gu & Dao, 2023). The other two baseline models are continuous models; a NCDE using a Hermite cubic spline with back- ward differences as the interpolation and a NRDE (Kidger et al., 2020; Morrill et al., 2021). Further details on all model architectures can be found in Appendices C.1 and C.2. 4.2. Toy Dataset We construct a toy dataset of 100,000 time series with 6 channels and 100 regularly spaced samples each. For every time step, the change in each channel is sampled indepen- dently from the discrete probability distribution with density p(n) = Z n+0.5 nвҲ’0.5 1вҲҡ 2ПҖ eвҲ’1 2 x2 dx, (29) where n вҲҲ Z. In other words, the change in a channel at each time step is a sample from a standard normal distribution rounded to the nearest integer. Figure 4 is a plot of a sample path from the toy dataset. 6Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations We consider four different binary classifications on the toy dataset. Each classification is a specific term in the signature of the path which depends on a different number of channels. 1. Was the change in the third channel, R1 0 dX3 s , greater than zero? 2. Was the area integral of the third and sixth channels,R1 0 Ru 0 dX3 s dX6 u, greater than zero? 3. Was the volume integral of the third, sixth, and first channels, R1 0 Rv 0 Ru 0 dX3 s dX6 udX1 v , greater than zero? 4. Was the 4D volume integral of the third, sixth, first, and fourth channels, R1 0 Rw 0 Rv 0 Ru 0 dX3 s dX6 udX1 v dX4 w, greater than zero? On this dataset, all models use a hidden state of dimension 64 and Adam with a learning rate of 0.0003 (Kingma & Ba, 2017). LRU, S5, S6, and MAMBA use six blocks. NRDE and Log-NCDE take ri+1 вҲ’ ri to be 4 observations and the signature truncation depth N to be 2. Full hyperparameter choices are given in Appendix C.3. 4.3. UEA Multivariate Time Series Classification Archive The models considered in this paper are evaluated on six datasets from the UEA multivariate time series classification archive (UEA-MTSCA)2. These six datasets were chosen via the following two criteria. First, only datasets with more than 200 total cases were considered. Second, the six datasets with the most observations were chosen, as datasets with many observations have previously proved challenging for deep learning approaches to time series modelling. Further details on the chosen datasets can be found in Appendix C.4. Following Morrill et al. (2021), the original train and test cases are combined and resplit into new random train, validation, and test cases using a 70 : 15 : 15 split. Hyperparameters for all models are found using a grid search over the validation accuracy on a fixed random split of the data. Full details on the hyperparameter grid search are in Appendix C.4. Having fixed their hyperparameters, models are compared on their average test set accuracy over five different random splits of the data. In order to compare models on their average GPU memory and run time, 1000 steps of training are run on an NVIDIA RTX 4090. The av- erage run time is estimated by combining the time for 1000 training steps with the average total number of training steps from the five runs over the random data splits. 2As of June 1st 2024, the EigenWorms dataset at https:// timeseriesclassification.com has 23 duplicated time series, which were removed for the experiments in this paper. 4.4. PPG-DaLiA PPG-DaLiA is a multivariate time series regression dataset, where the aim is to predict a personвҖҷs heart rate using data collected from a wrist-worn device (Reiss et al., 2019). The dataset consists of fifteen individuals with around 150 minutes of recording each at a maximum sampling rate of 128 Hz. There are six channels; blood volume pulse, electrodermal activity, body temperature, and three-axis ac- celeration. The data is split into a train, validation, and test set following a 70 : 15 : 15 split for each individual. After splitting the data, a sliding window of length49920 and step size 4992 is applied. Hyperparameters are found using the same method as for the UEA-MTSCA, but with validation mean squared error and slightly different hyperparameter choices given the high number of observations. Full details can be found in Ap- pendix C.4. Having fixed their hyperparameters, models are compared on their average mean squared error over five different runs on the same fixed data split. 5. Results 5.1. Toy Dataset Figure 5 compares the performance of the models consid- ered in this paper on the four different toy dataset classifica- tions. As expected, given that the classifications considered are solutions to CDEs, NCDEвҖҷs are the best performing model. Since NRDEs and Log-NCDEs are fixed tori+1 вҲ’ri being 4 observations and N = 2, they are both approxima- tions of a CDE. Notably, Log-NCDEs consistently outper- form NRDEs, providing empirical evidence that NRDEs do not always accurately learn the Lie bracket structure of ВҜfОё. All of the stacked recurrent models perform well when the label depends on one or two channels. However, their performance begins to decrease for three channels, and only MAMBA performs well when the label depends on four channels. 5.2. UEA-MTSCA Table 1 reports the average and standard deviation of each modelвҖҷs test set accuracy over five data splits. MAMBA achieves the lowest average accuracy. NCDEs and NRDEs have similar accuracies except on EigenWorms, the dataset with the most observations, where NRDEs significantly improve over NCDEs. However, NRDEs are still outper- formed on average accuracy by three stacked recurrent mod- els: LRU, S5, and S6. Log-NCDEs are the best performing model on average accuracy and rank. Compared to NRDEs, they achieve an equal or higher average accuracy on all six datasets and a lower standard deviation on four datasets. These results highlight the improvement in performance which can be achieved by calculating the Lie brackets. 7Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations Figure 5.Validation accuracy against number of steps for LRU, S5, S6, MAMBA, NCDE, NRDE, and Log-NCDE on the four different classifications considered for the toy dataset. The major contributors to NCDEs high average run time are time series with many observations. Using a depth вҲ’2 Log-ODE method decreases the com- putational burden on datasets with many observations for both NRDEs and Log-NCDEs. Although NRDEs and Log- NCDEs have the same asymptotic computational complex- ity, using a batched function to calculate the Lie brackets leads to Log-NCDEs having a lower computational burden on high-dimensional time series than NRDEs, as discussed in Section 3.4. Hence, Log-NCDEs have a lower average run time than both NCDEs and NRDEs. Despite these im- provements, all four stacked recurrent models have lower average run times than Log-NCDEs. 5.3. In contrast to the UEA-MTSCA experiments, MAMBA is the best performing stacked recurrent model. However, Log-NCDEs still achieve the best performance, obtaining the lowest average test set mean squared error and the second lowest standard deviation. 6. Discussion Recent theoretical work on the expressive power of struc- tured state space models may provide an explanation for their performance on the toy dataset. It has been shown that the recurrent layer of non-selective state space models, such as S5, are unable to capture terms in the signature that de- pend on more than one channel. Instead, the computational burden is placed on the non-linear mixing in-between recur- rent blocks. Furthermore, it has been shown that stacked selective state-space models, such as MAMBA, can capture higher order terms in the signature with only linear mix- ing layers (Cirone et al., 2024). Although the toy dataset highlights a potential limitation of the stacked recurrent models, this did not translate into poor performance on the real-world datasets considered in this paper. Log-NCDEs achieve the highest average accuracy on the UEA-MTSCA datasets and the lowest mean squared error on the PPG-DaLiA dataset. In particular, they improve upon NRDEs on every dataset. These results highlight the effectiveness of the Log-ODE method for improving the per- formance of NCDEs and the importance of calculating the Lie brackets when applying the Log-ODE method. Further- more, despite increasing the computational cost of each vec- tor field evaluation, Log-NCDEs have a lower average run time than both NCDEs and NRDEs on the UEA-MTSCA datasets. Empirical evidence suggests this is due to the Log-ODE method improving efficiency on time series with many observations, and calculating the Lie brackets lower- ing the computational burden of the Log-ODE method on high-dimensional time series. In addition to achieving state- of-the-art performance on the regularly sampled datasets considered in this paper, Log-NCDEs maintain the ability of NCDEs to naturally handle irregular sampling, making them an attractive option for real-world applications. 7. Conclusion Building on NRDEs, this paper introduced Log-NCDEs, which utilise the Log-ODE method to train NCDEs in an effective and efficient manner. This required proving a novel theoretical result bounding the Lip(Оі) norm of fully connected neural networks for 1 < Оі вүӨ 2, as well as de- veloping an efficient method for calculating the iterated Lie brackets of a neural network. A thorough empirical evaluation demonstrated the benefits of calculating the Lie brackets when applying the Log-ODE method. Furthermore, it showed that Log-NCDEs can achieve state-of-the-art per- formance on a range of multivariate time series datasets. A reasonable direction of future work is extending Log- NCDEвҖҷs to depthвҲ’N Log-ODE methods for N >2. This would require proving an equivalent result to Theorem 3.1 for Оі >2. Furthermore, it would be necessary to address the computational cost of the iterated Lie brackets. This could be achieved by using a structured neural network with cheap Jacobian-vector products as the CDE vector field. Another avenue of future work could be incorporating the recently developed adaptive version of the Log-ODE method (Bayer et al., 2023). 9Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations Impact Statement This paper presents Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations, a novel approach aimed at advancing the field of time series modelling. Potential applications of the method include healthcare, finance, and biology, where accurate time series modeling plays a crucial role. Despite the clear potential for positive impact, care must be taken to further understand the capabilities and limitations of the model be- fore real-world deployment. Additionally, structured state- space models, an alternative approach to time series mod- elling, have recently been integrated into large language models (LLMs). The advancement of LLMs has many po- tential societal consequences, both positive and negative. Existence and Uniqueness Let V and W be Banach spaces, X : [0, T] вҶ’ V and y : [0, T] вҶ’ W be continuous paths, and f(В·)ОҪ be a linear map from ОҪ вҲҲ V to vector fields on W. Assume that X, Y , and f are regular enough for the integral Z t 0 f(Ys)dXs (30) to be defined for all t вҲҲ [0, T] in the Young sense (Young, 1936). The path Y is said to obey a controlled differential equation (CDE) if Yt = Y0 + Z t 0 f(Ys)dXs, (31) for t вҲҲ [0, T], where Y0 вҲҲ W is the initial condition and X is the control (Lyons et al., 2007). The existence and uniqueness of the solution to a CDE depends on the smoothness of the control path X and the vector field f. We will measure the smoothness of a path by the smallest p вүҘ 1 for which the pвҲ’variation is finite and the smoothness of a vector field by the largest Оі >0 such that the function is Lip(Оі) (defined in Section 2.2). Definition A.1. (Partition) A partition of a real interval [0, T] is a set of real numbers {ri}m i=0 satisfying 0 = r0 < . . . < rm = T. Definition A.2. (pвҲ’variation (Young, 1936)) Let V be a Banach space, D = (r0, В·В·В· , rm) вҠӮ [0, T] be a partition of [0, T], and p вүҘ 1 be a real number. The pвҲ’variation of a path X : [0, T] вҶ’ V is defined as ||X||p = " sup D X riвҲҲD |Xri вҲ’ Xri+1 |p #1 p . (32) Theorem A.3. Let 1 вүӨ p <2 and p вҲ’ 1 < ОівүӨ 1. If W is finite-dimensional, X has finite pвҲ’variation, and f is Lip(Оі), then (31) admits a solution for every y0 вҲҲ W (Lyons, 1994). Theorem A.4. Let 1 вүӨ p <2 and p < Оі. If X has finite pвҲ’variation and f is Lip(Оі), then (31) admits a unique solution for every y0 вҲҲ W (Lyons, 1994). These theorems extend the classic differential equation existence and uniqueness results to controls with unbounded variation but finite pвҲ’variation for p <2. A proof of these theorems is can be found in (Lyons et al., 2007). These theorems are sufficient for the differential equations considered in this paper. However, there are many settings where the control has infinite pвҲ’variation for all p <2, such as Brownian motion. The theory of rough paths was developed in order to give meaning to (31) when the controlвҖҷs pвҲ’variation is finite only for p вүҘ 2 (Lyons, 1998). An introduction to rough path theory can be found in (Lyons et al., 2007). A.2. Proof of Theorem 3.1 The proof of Theorem 3.1 relies on two lemmas. The first is a bound on the Lip(Оі)вҲ’norm of the composition of two Lip(Оі) functions. The second is a bound on the Lip(2)вҲ’norm of each layer of a fully connected neural network (FCNN). B.1. Composition of Lip(Оі) Functions Lemma B.1. (Composed Lip(Оі)вҲ’norm (Cass et al., 2012)) Let U, V , and W be Banach spaces and ОЈ вҠӮ U and в„Ұ вҠӮ V be closed. For Оі вүҘ 1, let f вҲҲ Lip(Оі, ОЈ, в„Ұ) and g вҲҲ Lip(Оі, в„Ұ, W). Experimental Details C.1. Stacked Recurrent Models The stacked recurrent models considered in this paper are based on the official implementation of S5 located at https: //github.com/lindermanlab/S5 (Smith et al., 2023). A recurrent block consists of a batch or layer normalisation, a recurrent layer, a GLU layer (Dauphin et al., 2017), dropout with rate 0.1, and a skip connection. A full model consists of a linear encoder, a number of stacked recurrent blocks, and a final linear layer. The four different recurrent layers considered are the linear recurrent unit, S5, S6, and MAMBA, where S6 refers to the selective state-space recurrence introduced by Gu & Dao (2023) and MAMBA refers to the combination of a gated MLP, convolution, and S6 recurrence that was also introduced by Gu & Dao (2023). S5 and LRU use batch normalisation, whereas S6 and MAMBA use layer normalisation. C.2. CDE Models NCDEs, NRDEs, and Log-NCDEs use a single linear layer as ОҫП•. NCDEs and NRDEs use FCNNs as their vector fields configured in the same way as their original papers (Kidger et al., 2020; Morrill et al., 2021). NCDEs use ReLU activation functions for the hidden layers and a final activation function of tanh. NRDEs use the same, but move the tanh activation function to be before the final linear layer in the FCNN. Log-NCDEs use a FCNN with SiLU activation functions for the hidden layers and a final activation function of tanh. NRDEs and Log-NCDEs take their intervals ri+1 вҲ’ ri to be a fixed number of observations, referred to as the Log-ODE step. C.3. Toy Dataset Details On the toy dataset, all models use a hidden state of dimension 64 and Adam with a learning rate of 0.0003 (Kingma & Ba, 2017). LRU, S5, S6, and MAMBA use 6 blocks and S5, S6, and MAMBA use a state dimension of 64. S5 uses 2 initialisation blocks and MAMBA uses a convolution dimension of 4 and an expansion factor of 2. NCDEs, NRDEs, and Log-NCDEs use a FCNN with width 128 and depth 3 as their vector field. Furthermore, they all use Heun as their differential equation solver with a fixed stepsize of 0.01. NRDEs and Log-NCDEs use a Log-ODE step of 4 and a signature truncation depth of 2. Log-NCDEs do not use any Lip(Оі) regularisation, i.e. О» = 0. C.4. UEA-MTSCA and PPG-DaLiA Details Table 4 provides details on the dimension, number of observations, and number of classes for the datasets chosen from the UEA-MTSCA for the experiments conducted in this paper. Care is necessary when using the EigenWorms dataset. As of June 1st 2024, the train and test splits obtained from https://timeseriesclassification.com/description. php?Dataset=EigenWorms contain repeated time series. The repeated data was removed for the experiments in this paper. Tables 5 and 6 give an overview of the hyperparameters optimised over during the UEA-MTSCA and PPG-DaLiA experiments for the stacked recurrent models and the NCDE models respectively. The optimisation was performed using a grid search of the validation accuracy for the UEA-MTSCA datasets and the mean squared error for the PPG-DaLiA dataset. All models and experiments used Adam as their optimiser and a batch size of 32, except the stacked recurrent models on PPG-DaLiA, which used a batch size of 4 due to memory constraints. NCDEs, NRDEs, and Log-NCDEs use Heun as their differential equation solver with a fixed stepsize of 1/ max{500, 1 + (Time series length/Log-ODE step)}, with Log-ODE step = 1 for NCDEs. Additionally, Log-NCDEs scale down their initial FCNN parameters by a factor of 1000 to reduce the starting Lip(2) norm of the vector field. 19Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations Table 5.Hyperparameters selected by the optimisation for LRU, S5, S6, and MAMBA on the UEA-MTSCA datasets and PPG-DaLiA dataset. The following abbreviations are used: EigenWorms (EW), EthanolConcentration (EC), Heartbeat (HB), MotorImagery (MI), SelfRegulationSCP1 (SCP1), SelfRegulationSCP2 (SCP2), and PPG-DaLiA (PPG). A вң— denotes that the hyperparameter is not applicable to that model. The following abbreviations are used: EigenWorms (EW), EthanolConcentration (EC), Heartbeat (HB), MotorImagery (MI), SelfRegulationSCP1 (SCP1), SelfRegulationSCP2 (SCP2), and PPG-DaLiA (PPG). Additional Memory and run time Results Models are compared on their average GPU memory usage and run time for the UEA-MTSCA datasets. In order to compare the models, 1000 steps of training were run on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 with each model using the hyperparameters obtained from the hyperparameter optimisation. The specific choices of the hyperparameters are listed in Tables 5 and 6. In addition to the time for 1000 steps and GPU memory usage, shown in Figures 6a and 6b respectively, the average number of total training steps taken to produce the results in Table 1 is recorded in Figure 6c. Combining the results for time for 1000 training steps and total number of training steps gives an approximation of the total run time on the same hardware, and these results are shown in Figure 6d. Although the time per training step is lower for stacked recurrent models than NCDEs, NRDEs, or Log-NCDEs, they also require more training steps to converge. Additionally, NCDEs, NRDEs, and Log-NCDEs require less GPU memory. The largest contributors to the average run time of NCDEs are the datasets with the most observations, EigenWorms and MotorImagery. The positive impact of the Log-ODE method on computational burden is demonstrated empirically by the decrease in run time achieved by NRDEs and Log-NCDEs on EigenWorms when using a depthвҲ’2 Log-ODE method. When a depthвҲ’1 Log-ODE method is used, such as NRDEs on MotorImagery, the same decrease is not observed. Section 3.4 demonstrated that Log-NCDEs and NRDEs have the same asymptotic computational complexity. However, when using a depthвҲ’2 Log-ODE approximation and the same stepsize, NRDEs and Log-NCDEs exhibit drastically different run times on Heartbeat, a high-dimensional dataset. This difference is partly explained by the modelвҖҷs having different optimal hyperparameter choices, but even when using identical hyperparameters to the NRDE, Log-NCDEвҖҷs run time for 1000 steps of training is 1673 seconds, whereas NRDEвҖҷs run time is 9539 seconds. The remaining difference in run time is due to calculating the JVPs of fОё using a batched function, as discussed in Section 3.4. If instead the JVPs are calculated using a for-loop, then Log-NCDEs run time increases to 17045 seconds. 22Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations (a) Memory  (b) Time (c) Number of steps  (d) Total time Figure 6.Memory, time for 1000 steps, number of steps, and approximate total time for each model and dataeset from the UEA-MTSCA considered in this paper on an NVIDIA RTX 4090. The following abbreviations are used: EigenWorms (EW), EthanolConcentration (EC), Heartbeat (HB), MotorImagery (MI), SelfRegulationSCP1 (SCP1), and SelfRegulationSCP2 (SCP2). 23

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Log-NCDEs, a training method for Neural CDEs that applies the Log-ODE method while explicitly constructing the Log-ODE vector field via iterated Lie brackets of the NCDE vector field. Provides a new theoretical bound on the Lip(Оі) norm of fully connected neural networks for 1<ОівүӨ2 (enabling N=2 Log-ODE use) and an efficient implementation of Lie brackets via JacobianвҖ“vector products. Empirically shows improved accuracy and efficiency over NCDEs, NRDEs, LRU, S5, S6, and MAMBA on multiple multivariate time-series datasets, including very long sequences, with favorable runtime vs NCDE/NRDE and best average accuracy across UEA benchmarks and best MSE on PPG-DaLiA.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Model: Treats time series as control paths X and solves a CDE for hidden state h_t; prediction via linear readout. Uses Log-ODE approximation over intervals [r_i,r_{i+1}].
- Key change vs NRDE: Instead of learning an autonomous vector field mapping from log-signature to Ru (dimension uГ—ОІ(v,N)), constructs it by applying a Lie algebra homomorphism to the truncated log-signature log S_N(X), using iterated Lie brackets of the NCDE vector field f_Оё (dimension uГ—v). This preserves expressivity and reduces output dimensionality for NвүҘ2.
- Regularity: Enforces f_Оё вҲҲ Lip(Оі) with ОівҲҲ(NвҲ’1,N]; proves Lip(2) bounds for FCNNs with SiLU activations; adds spectral-norm-style penalty on weights/biases to control the norm.
- Computation: Evaluates Lie brackets via recursive bracket rules with Hall basis and JVPs of f_Оё; batches JVPs (e.g., with vmap) for efficiency. Uses Heun ODE solver; Log-ODE depth N=1 or 2.
- Complexity: Derives FLOP counts; shows Log-NCDE and NRDE have same asymptotic cost, with practical gains from batched JVPs on high-dimensional inputs.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Baselines: LRU, S5, S6, MAMBA (stacked recurrent variants), NCDE (with differentiable interpolation), NRDE.
- Toy dataset: 100,000 synthetic 6-channel sequences, length 100, discrete-time Gaussian-rounded increments. Four binary tasks targeting signature terms (1D integral, 2D area, 3D volume, 4D volume). All models: hidden dim 64, Adam lr=3e-4; NRDE/Log-NCDE: Log-ODE depth N=2, step=4.
- UEA-MTSCA: Six datasets with many observations (EigenWorms, EthanolConcentration, Heartbeat, MotorImagery, SelfRegulationSCP1/2). Combine original train/test, resplit 70/15/15 (train/val/test). Hyperparameters selected via grid search on validation accuracy (fixed split). Report meanВұstd test accuracy over 5 random splits. Runtime/memory measured on NVIDIA RTX 4090: 1000 training steps per model with chosen hyperparameters; estimate total runtime from steps-to-convergence.
- PPG-DaLiA: Wrist-worn multimodal signals (6 channels) to regress heart rate. Per-person splits 70/15/15; sliding window length 49920, stride 4992. Hyperparameters via grid search on validation MSE; report meanВұstd test MSE over 5 runs (fixed split). For long sequences, varied Log-ODE depths/steps explored (e.g., NвҲҲ{1,2}, steps {10,100,1000}).
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Experiments limited to Log-ODE depths N=1 or 2; no theoretical Lipschitz bounds provided for Оі>2 and computational cost of higher-order Lie brackets grows rapidly.
- Differential equation solvers impose sequential-in-time computation (limited parallelism) vs SSMs.
- Practical performance still slower on average than stacked recurrent models, despite improvements over NCDE/NRDE.
- Convergence of the series of Log-ODE vector fields is not guaranteed in general; choice of N and partition affects approximation.
- Requires vector field regularity (Lip(Оі)); may need explicit weight regularization to control Lip(2) norm.
- Results focused on selected datasets; generalization to broader domains or highly irregular/noisy controls not exhaustively evaluated.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend Log-NCDEs to higher Log-ODE depths (N>2) by establishing Lip(Оі) bounds for Оі>2 and developing scalable methods for higher-order Lie bracket computation.
- Design structured vector fields (e.g., architectures with cheap JVPs or implicit layers) to reduce bracket computation cost.
- Integrate adaptive Log-ODE algorithms to jointly choose truncation depth and interval partition during training/inference.
- Explore parallel-in-time or hybrid methods (e.g., combining SSM recurrences with Log-NCDEs) for improved efficiency.
- Strengthen theoretical guarantees on approximation error and convergence of the Log-ODE series under neural parameterizations.
- Apply to broader irregularly sampled, noisy, or high-frequency domains (healthcare, finance, sensor networks) and investigate robustness, calibration, and uncertainty quantification.

research_study_list.title: Scalable Inference in SDEs by Direct Matching of the FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov Equation
research_study_list.abstract:
Simulation-based techniques such as variants of stochastic Runge-Kutta are
the de facto approach for inference with stochastic differential equations
(SDEs) in machine learning. These methods are general-purpose and used with
parametric and non-parametric models, and neural SDEs. Stochastic Runge-Kutta
relies on the use of sampling schemes that can be inefficient in high
dimensions. We address this issue by revisiting the classical SDE literature
and derive direct approximations to the (typically intractable)
Fokker-Planck-Kolmogorov equation by matching moments. We show how this
workflow is fast, scales to high-dimensional latent spaces, and is applicable
to scarce-data applications, where a non-parametric SDE with a driving Gaussian
process velocity field specifies the model.
research_study_list.full_text: Scalable Inference in SDEs by Direct Matching of the FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov Equation Arno Solin Aalto University Espoo, Finland arno.solin@aalto.fi Ella Tamir Aalto University Espoo, Finland ella.tamir@aalto.fi Prakhar Verma Aalto University Espoo, Finland prakhar.verma@aalto.fi Abstract Simulation-based techniques such as variants of stochastic RungeвҖ“Kutta are the de facto approach for inference with stochastic differential equations (SDEs) in machine learning. These methods are general-purpose and used with parametric and non-parametric models, and neural SDEs. Stochastic RungeвҖ“Kutta relies on the use of sampling schemes that can be inefп¬Ғcient in high dimensions. We address this issue by revisiting the classical SDE literature and derive direct approximations to the (typically intractable) FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov equation by matching moments. We show how this workп¬Ӯow is fast, scales to high-dimensional latent spaces, and is applicable to scarce-data applications, where a non-parametric SDE with a driving Gaussian process velocity п¬Ғeld speciп¬Ғes the model. 1 Introduction 1. EulerвҖ“Maruyamasample trajectories 3. Probabilitydensityp(z, t)assolution to theFokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov PDE 2. Gaussianapproximation GP-SDE(SDE modelspeciп¬Ғcationby a GP prior) Figure 1: Views into solutions to SDEs. Differential equations are the standard method of modelling change over time. In deterministic systems the dynamics specifying how the system evolves, are typically written in the form of an ordinary differential equation (ODE). The dynamics act as prior knowledge and often stem from п¬Ғrst-principles in application areas such as physics, control engineering, chemistry, or compartmental models in epi- demiology and pharmacokinetics. Recently, learning ODE dynamics with modern automatic differentiation packages in machine learning has awakened an interest in black-box learning of continuous-time dynamics ( e.g., [6, 37]) and enabled their more general use across time-series modelling applications. A stochastic differential equation (SDE, [ 30, 40]) can be seen as a generalization of ODEs to stochastic dynamical settings, where the driving forces п¬Ӯuctuate or are uncertain. Stochastic dynamics appear naturally in applications where small (and typically unobserved) forces interact with the process, such as tracking applications, molecule motion, gene modelling, or stock markets. In machine learning, SDE models have received wide-spread attention due to their robustness and appealing properties for uncertainty quantiп¬Ғcation. The concept of a вҖҳsolutionвҖҷ to an SDE is broader than that of an ODE. As the process is stochastic, the full solution entails a probability distribution, p(z,t), depending on time tand covering the space z (see, e.g., [36]). For ItГҙ type SDEs, the evolution of the probability mass can be described in terms of the FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov (FPK) partial differential equation (backward Kolmogorov equation). This equation is typically intractable, and instead the de facto approach for inference in SDEs in machine learning is sampling. The most common approaches in this space are based on 35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021). arXiv:2110.15739v1  [cs.LG]  29 Oct 2021Stochastic RungeвҖ“Kutta schemes (such as the EulerвҖ“Maruyama scheme) which are derived from the ItГҙвҖ“Taylor series. These schemes sample realization trajectories of the SDE by driving the dynamics with numerical simulation of Brownian motion. However, these schemes suffer from drawbacks both related to (ordinary) RungeвҖ“Kutta methodsвҖ”such as step-size and sensitivity to stiffnessвҖ”as well as problems associated with any sampling schemes, such as a high number of samples required for an accurate representation of the underlying distribution. Despite these problems, few contemporary SDE approaches in machine learning explore SDE solutions beyond stochastic RungeвҖ“Kutta (or even the EulerвҖ“Maruyama scheme). Our aim is to try to broaden this view, and in Fig. 1 we sketch an example where we show three alternative solution perspectives to a Gaussian process prior SDE model (GP-SDE): the FPK probability density п¬Ғeld, EulerвҖ“Maruyama samples, and a Gaussian assumed density approximation. We argue that a Gaussian approximation in latent space SDEs is reasonable, as Gaussian approximations are typically employed anyway in observation models, and allow for speeding-up learning by an order of magnitude. The contributions of this paper are as follows. (i) We go through the workп¬Ӯow connecting вҖҳrandom ODEвҖҷ models with ItГҙ SDEs driven by a Gaussian process prior over the velocity п¬Ғeld, which allows for convenient speciп¬Ғcation of prior knowledge on the vector п¬Ғeld and induces an implicit prior over the SDE trajectories; (ii) We revisit the classical SDE literature and derive direct approximations to the (typically intractable) FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov equation in an assumed density Gaussian form that avoids sampling-based inference in the latent space, which makes inference fast and does not require sampling a high number of trajectories;(iii) We show how this workп¬Ӯow is fast, applicable to scarce-data applications, and how it also extends to previously presented latent SDE models. 1.1 Related Work Neural ODEs [6] model ODE dynamics by a neural network. Such models were developed further in [37], where the encoder is an ODE-RNN that improves modelling of irregularly sampled time series. A latent Bayesian neural ODE model, ODE2V AE, was examined in YДұldДұz et al.[49], where an encoder is combined with an ODE model whose second order dynamics are given by a Bayesian neural network. The neural ODE paradigm of modelling latent dynamics has been expanded to neural SDEs [26, 12, 46, 19], where the typical workп¬Ӯow is that a variational autoencoder (V AE, [21, 35]) is combined with a latent neural SDE, whose drift and diffusion are modelled by neural networks. In addition to modelling time series, neural SDEs have been used in generative models [16, 43, 44], where the generation of images from noise is modelled as the reverse-time process of a diffusion SDE by using Langevin dynamics on score-based models. Continuous normalizing п¬Ӯows are another model family, which applies ODE dynamics in a generative model [6, 11]. These works leverage simulation/sampling for solving the SDE in the latent space. The model can be trained by a stochastic adjoint method [ 26, 18]. More recently, latent neural SDEs have been trained deterministically by moment matching [ 27]. However, they discretized the system before matching the moments, while we form a direct approximation to the solution of the FPK. Compared to optimizing the moment ODEs, as discussed in [ 27], by maximizing likelihood, we regularize during inference by Gaussian process priors, or prior stochastic processes as in [ 26]. Approximative solutions to non-linear SDEs have been applied earlier in п¬Ғltering theory, where the optimal п¬Ғlter is approximated by a Gaussian assumed density п¬Ғlter [23]. In [39], such approximations are used for continuous-discrete state-space modelling. An alternative to assumed density п¬Ғlters are local linearization methods [ 31, 41], and simulation-based ItГҙвҖ“Taylor series solutions, stochastic RungeвҖ“Kutta methods, and leapfrog methods such as Verlet for second-order SDEs (see [22, 40]). The approximations presented in this work are also related to GP approximations [ 4, 2] of SDEs. The linearization approximations are related to statistical linearization [ 10, 42], and variational approximations [4]. Orthogonally to the SDE inference, we also consider SDE model speciп¬Ғcation in terms of GP priors. The seminal work by Ruttor et al. [38] considered GP-SDE models with unit diffusion. Yildiz et al. [48] built a model, where the drift and diffusion are sparse Gaussian processes with time-independent kernels. In Hegde et al. [13], a spatio-temporal SDE with GP priors for the drift and diffusion was combined with a GP as a continuous version of deep Gaussian processes. State-space models with a GP latent state transition function [8] train a non-parametric latent process to approximate unobserved dynamics. These are related to hierarchical GP dynamics [ 45, 24], where prior knowledge of the system can be encoded in multi-level hierarchies, for modelling, e.g., walking dynamics. 2Encoder Decoder OBSERVATION SPACE LATENT SPACE PREDICTION SPACE Observations are encoded to a latent space representation The dynamics in the la- tent space is described by a differential equation: Лҷz(t) =vОё(z, t) Repetitive features are learned as limit cycles We approximate p(z, t)=N(m(t),P(t)) Predictions are decoded back to observation space with uncertainties Figure 2: Latent dynamics workп¬Ӯow. The observations (left) are encoded into the latent space, where the dynamics of the system are learned as an SDE model. We approximate the solution to the SDE by a Gaussian for which we can approximate the dynamics of the п¬Ғrst moments by an ODE system, thus avoiding sampling in the latent space. Predictions are п¬Ғnally mapped back. 2 Methods We draft the methodology based on the latent dynamics workп¬Ӯow components as presented in Fig. 2. The focus is п¬Ғrst on specifying models for the latent space dynamics, starting from implicit priors in terms of random ODEs which we frame as ItГҙ SDEs. Thereafter, the focus shifts from model speciп¬Ғcation to inference, where we show that the FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov equation can be efп¬Ғciently approximated in an assumed density form, and п¬Ғnally brings us to cover the likelihood structure of these models as well. 2.1 Random Field Ordinary Differential Equations as SDEs The continuous dynamics of a latent (unobserved) z(t) вҲҲRd can conveniently be written in the form of a general п¬Ғrst-order ordinary differential equation d dtz(t) = vОё(z(t),t), (1) where vОё(В·) : Rd Г—R+ вҶ’Rd denotes the velocity п¬Ғeld parametrized by Оё. This is a general form of a non-linear ODE system, where the dynamics are deterministic and fully characterized by vОё(В·). The methodology presented in this section directly extends to the case where the vector п¬Ғeld vОё is time-dependent, but we omit the time dimension for simplicity of notation. Previously, the implicit prior on z(t) over tspeciп¬Ғed by Eq. (1) has been generalized to stochastic models by considering vОё(В·) to be stochastic. These models are known as вҖҳrandomвҖҷ ODE models, and the random п¬ҒeldvОё(В·) is typically either characterized by a Gaussian random п¬Ғeld or Gaussian process model (see, e.g., [38, 13]) or some parametric model (e.g., [26]). We consider an unconventional ODE model (or actually no ODE model at all, to be precise), where we specify a GP prior [34] over the velocity п¬Ғeld in form of a multi-output Gaussian process prior: v(z,t) вҲјGP(Вө(z),Оә(z,zвҖІ)), (2) where Вө : Rd вҶ’Rd is a mean function and Оә : RdГ—Rd вҶ’RdГ—d is a matrix-valued covariance function. The Gaussian process prior is completely speciп¬Ғed by its mean and covariance function, which encapsulate the assumptions about the sample processes/п¬Ғelds v (such as continuity, differen- tiability, curl, divergence,etc.): Вө(z) := E[v(z)] and Оә(z,zвҖІ) := E[(v(z) вҲ’Вө(z))(v(zвҖІ) вҲ’Вө(zвҖІ))вҲ—]. In Fig. 3, we will consider examples of useful vector-valued covariance functions that encode prop- erties on the vector п¬Ғeld. For inference, the GP is conditioned on inputвҖ“output pair observations D= {(zi,вҲҶzi)}n i=1 of the vector п¬Ғeld, where вҲҶzi represents the observed derivative at zi. The conditioned vector п¬Ғeld representation for an arbitrary point zвҲ—in the latent space can be given by v(zвҲ—) |DвҲј GP(E[v(z) |D],Cov[v(z) |D]), (3) where the E[В·] and Cov[В·] denote the marginal mean and (co)variance (for the multi-output GP, which means that the marginals are vector-valued). These take the form [34]: E[v(zвҲ—)] = KвҲ—ЛҶKвҲ’1y and 3вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 z1 z2 (a) Independent RBF priors вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 z1 z2 (b) Curl-free prior вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 вҲ’1 вҲ’0.5 0 0.5 1 z1 z2 (c) Divergence-free prior Figure 3: Effect of different GP priors in an GP-SDE with 8 observations (large red arrows) to the GP posterior (small red arrows): (a) Shows results for independent RBF priors over z1 and z2; (b) shows results for the curl-free covariance function (encoding вҖҳloop-aversionвҖҷ); (c) shows results for the divergence-free covariance function (encoding вҖҳenergy preservationвҖҷ). The hyperparameters (в„“= 0.2,Пғ2 = 0.1) are the same in each. Cov[v(zвҲ—)] = Оә(zвҲ—,zвҲ—) вҲ’KвҲ—ЛҶKвҲ’1KвҠӨ вҲ—, where y are the stacked observations of the derivatives such that y = (вҲҶzвҠӨ 1 ,..., вҲҶzвҠӨ n)вҠӨ. The Gram matrix K corresponds to evaluations of the covariance function such that Kij = Оә(zi,zj) are sub-blocks of K corresponding to the observation pairs (i,j), and ЛҶK = K + ОіI, where Оі is a nugget (observation noise/discrepancy) term, and KвҲ—is the cross-covariance between z and zвҲ—. The prohibitive cubic computational scaling associated with GP models manifests in the inversion of ЛҶK, and thus for large n, approximations based on inducing points or projections are used in practice to avoid this explicit inversion. In the light of Eq. (1) with v(z) вҲјGP(В·,В·), the ODE is driven by a multi-dimensional Gaussian random п¬Ғeld conditioned on D. A straightforward way of dealing with a model of this kind, is to do inference by sampling random draws of the velocity п¬Ғeld from the GP, and then drive the ODE with those samples (can be viewed as an Monte Carlo approach for drawing ODE realizations). However, a more convenient way is to specify the prior over the stochastic dynamics in a stochastic differential equation form. At its core, a lot of previous work in this space hinges on the realization that if everything is essentially Gaussian, an equivalent model can be speciп¬Ғed in terms of an ItГҙ SDE describing the stochastic evolution of trajectories affected by the GP velocity п¬Ғeld [see 13, for discussion]. Informally, this takes the white noise form d dtz(t) = f(z) + L(z) w(t), where f(z) = E[ v(z)] and L(z) denotes a square-root factor such that LLвҠӨ = Cov[v(z)] (in the scalar case, just the square-root, and in the multi-output case, e.g., the Cholesky factor). Here w(t) is a white noise process with unit spectral density. It is worth noting that we do not give guarantees for a direct link between the random ODE in Eq. (2) and the following SDE formulation (see App. A.3 for discussion). Yet, formally we write a similarly-behaving SDE in the standard ItГҙ SDE form: dz(t) = f(z,t) dt+ L(z,t) dОІ(t), (4) where dОІ(t) is vector-valued unit Brownian motion (the spectral density Q is set to I). For a GP-SDE, the drift is driven by the GP mean, f(z,t) := E[v(z)] and the diffusion by the square-root factor of the marginal covariance at z(t), L(z,t) := вҲҡ Cov[v(z)]. To be precise, the GP-SDE drift and diffusion at a point zвҲ—are determined by the GP predicted mean and variance at zвҲ—, which can be written as f(zвҲ—,t)= E[v(zвҲ—)]=KвҲ—ЛҶKвҲ’1y and L(zвҲ—,t)= вҲҡ Cov[v(zвҲ—)]= вҲҡ Оә(zвҲ—,zвҲ—) вҲ’KвҲ—ЛҶKвҲ’1KвҠӨвҲ—. (5) 2.2 FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov Equation We are interested insolving SDE models of the form in Eq. (4), but without the restriction that the drift and diffusion are deп¬Ғned by a Gaussian process, and present the related theory with a model-agnostic view on the problem. Because the resulting solutions are stochastic processes, the full solution to the SDE can be characterized by its time-evolving probability density function. Let z(t0) вҲјp(z(t0)) be some initial condition which we assumed to be independent of the Brownian motion.
