Non-Ambiguity Condition in Partial-Label Learning In theoretical analyses of partial-label learning, the so-called non-ambiguity condition has been used (Cour et al., 2011; Cabannnes et al., 2020). In this section, we discuss the relation between the non-ambiguity and the left-invertibility of T. A partial label yвҲҲY is a candidate set of labels, only one of which is correct. Obviously, YвҠӮ 2Z\{вҲ…}, where 2Zis the power set of Z. The empty set вҲ…is not in Ybecause a partial label always contains a correct label. Deп¬Ғnition 17 (Non-ambiguity condition). Let P(zвҖІвҲҲY \{z}| Z = z) be the probability that a partial label contains an incorrect label zвҖІ, given a true label z. Then, the ambiguity degree Пөis deп¬Ғned as follows3: ПөвүЎ sup z,zвҖІвҲҲZ,P(Z=z)>0 P(zвҖІвҲҲY \{z}| Z = z). (24) Partial labels are said to satisfy the non-ambiguity condition if Пө< 1. The ambiguity degree is the maximum probability of co-occurrence of an incorrect label zвҖІwith a correct label z. To gain some intuition into the ambiguity degree and the non-ambiguity condition, let us consider two extreme cases: Пө= 0 and Пө = 1. The equality Пө = 0 implies that a weak label yis always a singleton {z}if the correct label is z. That is, every instance is given only the correct label, and therefore, this is equivalent to supervised learning. On the other hand, when Пө= 1 and the non-ambiguity condition is not satisп¬Ғed, there is a pair of labels zand zвҖІin Zsuch that if a true label of an instance is z, an incorrect label zвҖІis always given to that instance as well. There is a simple example that does not satisfy the non-ambiguity condition but has a left-invertible label transition matrix T. Consider a binary classiп¬Ғcation problem Z= {1,2}with a partial label set Y= {{1},{1,2}}. If we identify 1 with the positive label and 2 with the negative label, this problem is often referred to as positive-unlabeled (PU) learning or learning with totally asymmetric label noise. The label transition matrix T has the following form: T = ( r 0 1 вҲ’r 1 ) , (25) where r(0 <r <1) is the proportion of positively labeled instances in truly positive instances. This T is left-invertible and yet breaks the non-ambiguity condition, because examples with the correct label 2 always have a partial label {1,2} including the incorrect label 1. Weak Noise Condition in Learning from Noisy Labels Theoretical analyses of noisy-label learning have assumed that the noise rate is smaller than some threshold, which often coincides with the point at which T is not left-invertible (Angluin & Laird, 1988; Natarajan et al., 2013). For example, (Natarajan et al., 2013) considered label noise that has the following transition matrix: T = ( 1 вҲ’r+ rвҲ’ r+ 1 вҲ’rвҲ’ ) . (33) They assumed that r+ + rвҲ’<1, and T ceases to be left-invertible at the boundary r+ + rвҲ’= 1. However, T recovers left-invertibility for a noise rate above the threshold (e.g., complementary label learning, which can be seen as the extreme case in which labels are п¬Ӯipped with probability 1). There, our framework is still applicable. B. Multiple Weak-Label Datasets The arguments in the main text deal with scenarios with only one weak-label set Yand an associated transition matrix T. In this appendix, we show that without making formal changes, we can extend the formulation to scenarios with multiple samples having different noise characteristics. Let N be the number of training sets. They all have the same true-label set Z= {z1,z2,...,z C}and base distribution p(x,z), but each has its own weak-label set Y(d) = {y(d) 1 ,y(d) 2 ,...,y (d) Cd }and label transition matrix T(d). Note that this further implies that TT(RT1ZвҲ’1Y) = 0, or that RT1ZвҲ’1YвҲҲcoker T. A transition matrix T satisп¬Ғes the identity TT1Y= 1Z. For such R, it holds that RT1ZвҲқ1Y, but because TTRT1Z= 1Zand TT1Y= 1Z, we conclude that RT1Z= 1Y. G. Experimental Details Datasets We used the MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) datasets. Each dataset deп¬Ғnes its training and test splits. In our experiment, we split the training split into two splits: one was used for training, and the other was used for validation. Table 3 lists the numbers of examples in the dataset splits. Before starting the experiments, we converted the ground-truth labels in the training splits into complementary labels. A complementary label for an instance was randomly chosen with probabilities given by the transition matrix in Eq. (18). Training procedure We used stochastic gradient descent with momentum to optimize the models. The momen- tum and the mini-batch size were п¬Ғxed to 0.9 and 256, respectively. The initial learning rates were chosen from {0.1,0.03,0.01,0.003,0.001,0.0003,0.0001}as those giving the best validation accuracy. When a learning rate of 0.1 or 0.0001 achieved the best validation accuracy, we also tried two more values beyond the predeп¬Ғned range. In all such cases, we conп¬Ғrmed that the chosen values were at a peak or on a plateau of the validation accuracy. The chosen values are listed in Table 4. The default value of the weight decay coefп¬Ғcient is 10вҲ’4, but when it is tuned, it is chosen from {0.1,0.03,0.01,0.003,0.001,0.0003,0.0001}. The values of the weight decay coefп¬Ғcient that achieved the best validation Table 5.Weight decay coefп¬Ғcient with which the best validation accuracy was achieved for BC and BC + GA. MNIST, linear MNIST, MLP CIFAR-10, ResNet-20 CIFAR-10, WRN-28-2 BC 0.0003 0.001 0.001 0.01 BC + GA 0.1 0.001 0.0003 0.0003Lower-Bounded Proper Losses for Weakly Supervised Classiп¬Ғcation Table 6.Values of the coefп¬Ғcient kin Eq. (17) as chosen by the validation accuracy. Dataset and model k MNIST, linear 0.03 MNIST, MLP 1.0 CIFAR-10, ResNet-20 1.0 CIFAR-10, WRN-28-2 1.0 Table 7.Numbers of epochs at which the best validation accuracy was achieved for each setting. MNIST, linear MNIST, MLP CIFAR-10, ResNet-20 CIFAR-10, WRN-28-2 BC 4.1 46.6 22.6 24.6 BC + GA 54.1 62.7 83.6 76.1 BC + gLS 19.5 59.1 47.9 39.7 accuracy are listed in Table 5. Our proposed method, generalized logit squeezing (gLS), has two hyperparameters: the exponent Оұand the coefп¬Ғcient k. For a п¬Ғxed Оұ, we searched for the value of kthat achieved the best validation accuracy. The candidate values were 10, 3, 1, 0.3, 0.1, 0.03, and 0.01. These results are listed in Table 6. We adopted early stopping to determine the training time. Speciп¬Ғcally, when the validation accuracy had not improved for the last 10 epochs, the learning rate was reduced by a factor of 10, and the third time the same condition was satisп¬Ғed, the training was terminated. The test accuracy reported here is for the epochs with the best validation accuracy. Table 7 lists the numbers of epochs at which the best validation accuracy was achieved. We used a simple grid search strategy for the hyperparameter search. The best hyperparameters (i.e., the learning rate and the gLS coefп¬Ғcient) were used in the evaluation step, in which a randomly initialized model was trained on the training split and evaluated on the test split. The training duration in the evaluation step was also determined by the early stopping strategy as described above. Other details All the experiments were performed using on-premise computation servers equipped with NVIDIAвҖҷs GeForce GTX 1080Ti and Tesla V100. The training duration varied signiп¬Ғcantly, depending on the methods and the model size, but the longest run took less than one hour on the Tesla V100. We used PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) to implement the experiments.

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses weakly supervised classification under label corruption by establishing when weak-label losses are both proper (enable correct class-posterior estimation) and lower-bounded (avoid divergence). Introduces a dual representation theorem for proper losses (dual to Savage representation), characterizes T-proper weak-label losses via a convex function F and reconstruction matrix R (including a cokernel term), derives a sufficient condition for lower-boundedness of T-proper losses, and proposes generalized logit squeezing (gLS) that makes any proper weak-label loss lower-bounded without losing properness. Empirically demonstrates that gLS outperforms or matches baselines on MNIST and CIFAR-10 with complementary labels, highlighting the importance of properness and lower-boundedness.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Theoretical framework: Convex analysis and FenchelвҖ“Young duality. Derives a dual representation (Theorem 5) expressing any regular proper loss as l(q,z)=О»_F(вҲҮF*(q),z) with О»_F(v,z)=-v_z+F(v), where F is a closed convex function and F* its conjugate.
- Characterization of T-proper weak-label losses (Theorem 7): l_W(q,y)=О»_{F,R}(вҲҮF*(q),y)+О”_y(q) with О»_{F,R}(v,y)=-(RTv)_y+F(v), R a left-inverse (reconstruction matrix) of the label transition matrix T, and О”(q) in coker(T); О” does not affect expected risk.
- Lower-boundedness: Shows standard proper losses (e.g., softmax cross-entropy) can become unbounded under weak labels; provides a sufficient condition (Theorem 13) that F(v)вҲ’max_y (RTv)_y is bounded below, equivalently F*(Re_y)<вҲһ for all weak labels y (Proposition 14).
- Regularization scheme (gLS, Corollary 15): Replace F by F'(v)=F(v)+ (k/2)вҲ‘_z |v_z|^Оұ with Оұ>1 to ensure superlinear growth, yielding T-proper and lower-bounded losses. Presents training pseudocode for BC+gLS.
- Unifies forward and backward corrections within the framework; shows how each fits the characterization.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Weak-label setting: Complementary labels (each instance labeled as NOT class c), transition T=(1/(KвҲ’1))(1_KвҲ’I_K).
- Datasets: MNIST and CIFAR-10. Training splits further split into train/validation; labels in training converted to complementary labels. Test on original test sets.
- Models: MNISTвҖ”linear classifier and MLP (1 hidden layer). CIFAR-10вҖ”ResNet-20 and Wide-ResNet-28-2.
- Methods compared: (1) BC: backward-corrected softmax cross-entropy (proper but unbounded). (2) BC+GA: BC with gradient-ascent trick to enforce non-negativity of partial risks (bounded but improper). (3) BC+gLS: proposed lower-bounded, T-proper loss with Оұ>1 (Оұ=2 by default; sensitivity to Оұ and k studied).
- Optimization: SGD with momentum 0.9, batch size 256, grid search over initial learning rates; weight decay default 1e-4 and also tuned for BC and BC+GA; early stopping with LR reductions. 16 independent trials per setting; report meanВұstd test accuracy. Implementation in PyTorch; code link provided; GPUs used (1080Ti, V100).
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Assumes input-independent, known label transition matrix T and existence of a left inverse R (reconstructibility), requiring |Z|вүӨ|Y|; may not hold in many real datasets.
- Sufficient (not necessary) condition for lower-boundedness; does not fully characterize all bounded T-proper losses, especially when О”(q)вү 0 (|Y|>|Z| cases).
- Empirical evaluation limited to complementary labels (extreme noise) on MNIST/CIFAR-10; lacks experiments on other weak-supervision regimes (e.g., general label noise, partial labels, PU) and larger/real-world datasets.
- Baseline comparisons focus on BC and GA variants; forward-correction losses and other robust methods are not empirically compared.
- gLS introduces hyperparameters (k, Оұ); although some robustness is shown, practical tuning is still required and large k may underfit.
- Requires convex F and regularity assumptions; analysis does not fully address scenarios with input-dependent T(x) or unknown T estimation errors.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend theory and practice to input-dependent or unknown transition matrices T(x); develop estimation/joint-learning of T with guarantees under the proposed framework.
- Strengthen boundedness results to necessary-and-sufficient conditions; handle the О”(q) term explicitly and study strict properness conditions operationally.
- Broaden empirical validation to diverse weak supervision types (noisy labels with varying rates, partial labels, PU, multiple noisy sources) and larger benchmarks; compare against forward-correction and other robust losses.
- Automate/adapt hyperparameter selection for gLS (k, Оұ), e.g., via data-dependent schedules or bilevel optimization; analyze generalization and optimization dynamics under gLS.
- Explore connections to and differences from Lp regularization; study effects on robustness (e.g., adversarial), calibration, and uncertainty estimation.
- Investigate composite/link-function design leveraging the dual representation for improved optimization and calibration.
- Empirically evaluate the multi-source setting with heterogeneous noises as formalized in the appendix and integrate domain adaptation techniques.

reference_research_study_list.title: Stochastic Calculus: An Introduction Through Theory and Exercises
Machine learning approximation algorithms for high-dimensional fully nonlinear partial differential equations and second-order backward stochastic differential equations
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
High-dimensional partial differential equations (PDE) appear in a number of
models from the financial industry, such as in derivative pricing models,
credit valuation adjustment (CVA) models, or portfolio optimization models. The
PDEs in such applications are high-dimensional as the dimension corresponds to
the number of financial assets in a portfolio. Moreover, such PDEs are often
fully nonlinear due to the need to incorporate certain nonlinear phenomena in
the model such as default risks, transaction costs, volatility uncertainty
(Knightian uncertainty), or trading constraints in the model. Such
high-dimensional fully nonlinear PDEs are exceedingly difficult to solve as the
computational effort for standard approximation methods grows exponentially
with the dimension. In this work we propose a new method for solving
high-dimensional fully nonlinear second-order PDEs. Our method can in
particular be used to sample from high-dimensional nonlinear expectations. The
method is based on (i) a connection between fully nonlinear second-order PDEs
and second-order backward stochastic differential equations (2BSDEs), (ii) a
merged formulation of the PDE and the 2BSDE problem, (iii) a temporal forward
discretization of the 2BSDE and a spatial approximation via deep neural nets,
and (iv) a stochastic gradient descent-type optimization procedure. Numerical
results obtained using ${\rm T{\small ENSOR}F{\small LOW}}$ in ${\rm P{\small
YTHON}}$ illustrate the efficiency and the accuracy of the method in the cases
of a $100$-dimensional Black-Scholes-Barenblatt equation, a $100$-dimensional
Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation, and a nonlinear expectation of a $ 100
$-dimensional $ G $-Brownian motion.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Machine learning approximation algorithms for high-dimensional fully nonlinear partial diп¬Җerential equations and second-order backward stochastic diп¬Җerential equations Christian Beck1, Weinan E2, and Arnulf Jentzen3 1ETH Zurich (Switzerland), e-mail: christian.beck (at) math.ethz.ch 2Beijing Institute of Big Data Research (China), Princeton University (USA), and Peking University (China), e-mail: weinan (at) math.princeton.edu 3ETH Zurich (Switzerland), e-mail: arnulf.jentzen (at) sam.math.ethz.ch September 19, 2017 Abstract High-dimensional partial diп¬Җerential equations (PDE) appear in a number of models from the п¬Ғnancial industry, such as in derivative pricing models, credit val- uation adjustment (CVA) models, or portfolio optimization models. The PDEs in such applications are high-dimensional as the dimension corresponds to the num- ber of п¬Ғnancial assets in a portfolio. Moreover, such PDEs are often fully nonlinear due to the need to incorporate certain nonlinear phenomena in the model such as default risks, transaction costs, volatility uncertainty (Knightian uncertainty), or trading constraints in the model. Such high-dimensional fully nonlinear PDEs are exceedingly diп¬ғcult to solve as the computational eп¬Җort for standard approxima- tion methods grows exponentially with the dimension. In this work we propose a new method for solving high-dimensional fully nonlinear second-order PDEs. Our method can in particular be used to sample from high-dimensional nonlinear expec- tations. The method is based on (i) a connection between fully nonlinear second-order PDEs and second-order backward stochastic diп¬Җerential equations (2BSDEs), (ii) a merged formulation of the PDE and the 2BSDE problem, (iii) a temporal forward discretization of the 2BSDE and a spatial approximation via deep neural nets, and (iv) a stochastic gradient descent-type optimization procedure. Numerical results obtained using TensorFlow in Python illustrate the eп¬ғciency and the accuracy of the method in the cases of a 100-dimensional Black-Scholes-Barenblatt equation, 1 arXiv:1709.05963v1  [math.NA]  18 Sep 2017a 100-dimensional Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation, and a nonlinear expectation of a 100-dimensional G-Brownian motion. Keywords: deep learning, second-order backward stochastic diп¬Җerential equation, 2BSDE, numerical methhod, Black-Scholes-Barentblatt equation, Knightian uncertainty, Hamiltonian-Jacobi-Bellman and 100 space-dimensions . . . . . . . . . . . . . 30 2A Source codes 34 A.1 A Python code for the deep 2BSDE method used in Subsection 4.1 . . . 34 A.2 A Matlab code for the Branching diп¬Җusion method used in Subsection 4.1 38 A.3 A Python code for the deep 2BSDE method used in Subsection 4.3 . . . 40 A.4 A Matlab code for the classical Monte Carlo method used in Subsection 4.4 46 A.5 A Matlab code for the п¬Ғnite diп¬Җerences method used in Subsection 4.6 . 46 1 Introduction Partial diп¬Җerential equations (PDE) play an important role in an abundance of models from п¬Ғnance to physics. Objects as the wave function associated to a quantum physical system, the value function describing the fair price of a п¬Ғnancial derivative in a pricing model, or the value function describing the expected maximal utility in a portfolio opti- mization problem are often given as solutions to nonlinear PDEs. Roughly speaking, the nonlinearities in the above mentioned PDEs from п¬Ғnancial engineering applications appear due to certain nonlinear eп¬Җects in the trading portfolio (the trading portfolio for hedging the п¬Ғnancial derivative claim in the case of derivative pricing problems and the trading portfolio whose utility has to be maximized in the case of portfolio optimization problems); see, e.g., [7, 9, 37, 45, 66, 71] for derivative pricing models with diп¬Җerent interest rates for borrowing and lending, see, e.g., [24, 53] for derivative pricing models incorporating the default risk of the issuer of the п¬Ғnancial derivative, see, e.g., [4, 5, 109] for models for the pricing of п¬Ғnancial derivatives on untradable underlyings such as п¬Ғnancial derivatives on the temperature or mortality-dependent п¬Ғnancial derivatives, see, e.g., [1, 36, 83] for models incorporating that the trading strategy inп¬Ӯuences the price processes though de- mand and supply (so-called large investor eп¬Җects), see, e.g., [39, 50, 70, 94] for models taking transaction costs in the trading portfolio into account, and see, e.g., [2, 50] for models incorporating uncertainties in the model parameters for the underlyings (Knight- ian uncertainty). The PDEs emerging from such models are often high-dimensional as the associated trading portfolios frequently contain a whole basket of п¬Ғnancial assets (see, e.g., [7, 24, 39]). These high-dimensional nonlinear PDEs are typically exceedingly diп¬ғcult to solve approximatively. Nonetheless, there is a strong demand from the п¬Ғnancial engineer- ing industry to approximatively compute the solutions of such high-dimensional nonlinear parabolic PDEs due to the above mentioned practical relevance of these PDEs. There are a number of numerical methods for solving nonlinear parabolic PDEs approxi- matively in the literature. Some of these methods are deterministic approximation methods and others are random approximation methods which rely on suitable probabilistic repre- sentations of the corresponding PDE solutions such as probabilistic representations based on backward stochastic diп¬Җerential equations (BSDEs) (cf., e.g., [10, 11, 42, 85, 86, 87, 88]), probabilistic representations based on second-order backward stochastic diп¬Җerential equa- tions (2BSDEs) (cf., e.g., [22]), probabilistic representations based on branching diп¬Җusions 3(cf., e.g., [53, 54, 55, 77, 102, 108]), and probabilistic representations based on exten- sions of the classical Feynman-Kac formula (cf., e.g., [34, 58, 84]). We refer, e.g., to [23, 30, 65, 69, 95, 103, 104, 106] for deterministic approximation methods for PDEs, to [3, 6, 7, 13, 18, 19, 20, 21, 25, 26, 27, 28, 31, 32, 43, 44, 45, 46, 47, 48, 56, 64, 71, 72, 73, 74, 75, 81, 82, 93, 98, 99, 100, 105, 111] for probabilistic approximation methods for PDEs based on temporal discretizations of BSDEs, to [33, 52] for probabilistic approximation methods for PDEs based on suitable deep learning approximations for BSDEs, to [40, 110] for probabilistic approximation methods for BSDEs based on sparse grid approximations, to [14, 41] for probabilistic approximation methods for BSDEs based on Wiener Chaos expansions, to [12, 22, 38, 49, 62, 112] for probabilistic approximation methods for PDEs based on temporal discretization of 2BSDEs, to [17, 53, 54, 55, 96, 107] for probabilis- tic approximation methods for PDEs based on branching diп¬Җusion representations, and to [34, 35] for probabilistic approximation methods based on extensions of the classical Feynman-Kac formula. Most of the above named approximation methods are, however, only applicable in the case where the PDE/BSDE dimension d is rather small or work exclusively in the case of serious restrictions on the parameters or the type of the considered PDE (e.g., small nonlinearities, small terminal/initial conditions, semilinear structure of the PDE). The numerical solution of a high-dimensional nonlinear PDE thus remains an exceedingly diп¬ғcult task and there is only a limited number of situations where practical algorithms for high-dimensional PDEs have been developed (cf., e.g., [29, 33, 34, 35, 52, 54, 55, 101]). In particular, to the best of our knowledge, at the moment there exists no practical algorithm for high-dimensional fully nonlinear parabolic PDEs in the scientiп¬Ғc literature. In this work we intend to overcome this diп¬ғculty, that is, we propose a new algorithm for solving fully-nonlinear PDEs and nonlinear second-order backward stochastic diп¬Җeren- tial equations 2BSDEs. Our method in particular can be used to sample from Shige PengвҖҷs nonlinear expectation in high space-dimensions (cf., e.g., [89, 90, 91, 92]). The proposed algorithm exploits a connection between PDEs and 2BSDEs (cf., e.g., Cheridito et al. [22]) to obtain a merged formulation of the PDE and the 2BSDE, whose solution is then ap- proximated by combining a time-discretization with a neural network based deep learning procedure (cf., e.g., [8, 15, 16, 23, 30, 33, 51, 52, 59, 63, 65, 68, 67, 69, 78, 79, 95, 97]). Roughly speaking, the merged formulation allows us to formulate the original PDE prob- lem as a learning problem. The random loss function for the deep neural network in our algorithm is, loosely speaking, given by the error between the prescribed terminal condition of the 2BSDE and the neural network based forward time discretization of the 2BSDE. A related deep learning approximation algorithm for PDEs of semilinear type based on for- ward BSDEs has been recently proposed in [33, 52]. A key diп¬Җerence between [33, 52] and the present work is that here we rely on the connection between fully nonlinear PDEs and 2BSDEs given by Cheridito et al. [22] while [33, 52] rely on the nowadays almost classical connection between PDEs and BSDEs (cf., e.g., [86, 85, 87, 88]). This is the reason why 4the method proposed in [33, 52] is only applicable to semilinear PDEs while the algorithm proposed here allows to treat fully nonlinear PDEs and nonlinear expectations. The remainder of this article is organized as follows. In Section 2 we derive (see Subsec- tions 2.1вҖ“2.6 below) and formulate (see Subsection 2.7 below) a special case of the algorithm proposed in this work. In Section 3 the proposed algorithm is derived (see Subsections 3.1вҖ“ 3.5 below) and formulated (see Subsection 3.7 below) in the general case. The core idea is most apparent in the simpliп¬Ғed framework in Subsection 2.7 (see Framework 2.1 below). The general framework in Subsection 3.7, in turn, allows for employing more sophisticated machine learning techniques (see Framework 3.2 below). In Section 4 we present numer- ical results for the proposed algorithm in the case of several high-dimensional PDEs. In Subsection 4.1 the proposed algorithm in the simpliп¬Ғed framework in Subsection 2.7 is em- ployed to approximatively calculate the solution of a 20-dimensional Allen-Cahn equation. In Subsections 4.3, 4.4, 4.5, and 4.6 the proposed algorithm in the general framework in Subsection 3.7 is used to approximatively calculate the solution of a 100-dimensional Black- Scholes-Barenblatt equation, the solution of a 100-dimensional Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation, the solution of a 50-dimensional Allen-Cahn equation, and nonlinear expecta- tions of G-Brownian motions in 1 and 100 space-dimensions. Python implementations of the algorithms are provided in Section A. 2 Main ideas of the deep 2BSDE method In Subsections 2.1вҖ“2.6 below we explain the main idea behind the algorithm proposed in this work which we refer to as deep 2BSDE method . This is done at the expense of a rather sketchy derivation and description. More precise and more general deп¬Ғnitions of the deep 2BSDE method may be found in Sections 2.7 and 3.7 below. In a nutshell, the main ingredients of the deep 2BSDE method are (i) a certain connection between PDEs and 2BSDEs, (ii) a merged formulation of the PDE and the 2BSDE problem, (iii) a temporal forward discretization of the 2BSDE and a spatial approximation via deep neural nets, and (iv) a stochastic gradient descent-type optimization procedure. The derivation of the deep 2BSDE method is mainly based on ideas in E, Han, & Jentzen [33] and Cheridito et al. [22] (cf., e.g., [33, Section 2] and [22, Theorem 4.10]). Let us start now by describing the PDE problems which we want to solve with the deep 2BSDE method. 52.1 Fully nonlinear second-order PDEs Let d вҲҲN = {1,2,3,... }, T вҲҲ(0,вҲһ), u = (u(t,x))tвҲҲ[0,T],xвҲҲRd вҲҲC1,2([0,T] Г—Rd,R), f вҲҲC([0,T] Г—Rd Г—R Г—Rd Г—RdГ—d,R), gвҲҲC(Rd,R) satisfy for all tвҲҲ[0,T), xвҲҲRd that u(T,x) = g(x) and вҲӮu вҲӮt(t,x) = f ( t,x,u (t,x),(вҲҮxu)(t,x),(Hessxu)(t,x) ) . (1) The deep 2BSDE method allows us to approximatively compute the function u(0,x), xвҲҲ Rd. To п¬Ғx ideas we restrict ourselves in this section to the approximative computation of the real number u(0,Оҫ) вҲҲR for some Оҫ вҲҲRd and we refer to Subsection 3.7 below for the general algorithm. Moreover, the deep 2BSDE method can easily be extended to the case of systems of fully nonlinear second-order parabolic PDEs but in order to keep the notational complexity as low as possible we restrict ourself to the scalar case in this work (cf. (1) above). Note that the PDE (1) is formulated as a terminal value problem. We chose the terminal value problem formulation over the in the PDE literature more common initial value problem formulation because, on the one hand, the terminal value problem formulation seems to be more natural in connection with second-order BSDEs (which we are going to use below in the derivation of the proposed approximation algorithm) and because, on the other hand, the terminal value problem formulation shows up naturally in п¬Ғnancial engineering applications like the Black-Scholes-Barenblatt equation in derivative pricing (cf. Section 4.3). Clearly, terminal value problems can be transformed into initial value problems and vice versa; see, e.g., Remark 3.3 below. 2.2 Connection between fully nonlinear second-order PDEs and 2BSDEs Let (в„Ұ,F,P ) be a probability space, let W: [0,T] Г—в„Ұ вҶ’Rd be a standard Brown- ian motion on (в„Ұ ,F,P ) with continuous sample paths, let F = (Ft)tвҲҲ[0,T] be the normal п¬Ғltration on (в„Ұ ,F,P ) generated by W, and let Y : [0,T] Г—в„Ұ вҶ’R, Z: [0,T] Г—в„Ұ вҶ’Rd, О“: [0 ,T] Г—в„Ұ вҶ’RdГ—d, and A: [0,T] Г—в„Ұ вҶ’Rd be F-adapted stochastic processes with continuous sample paths which satisfy that for all tвҲҲ[0,T] it holds P -a.s. that Yt = g(Оҫ+ WT) вҲ’ вҲ« T t ( f(s,Оҫ + Ws,Ys,Zs,О“s) + 1 2 Trace(О“s) ) dsвҲ’ вҲ« T t вҹЁZs,dWsвҹ©Rd (2) and Zt = Z0 + вҲ« t 0 Asds+ вҲ« t 0 О“sdWs. More speciп¬Ғcally, in Subsec- tion 4.1 we employ an implementation of the deep 2BSDE method in Framework 2.1 to approximate a 20-dimensional Allen-Cahn equation, in Subsection 4.3 we employ an imple- mentation of the deep 2BSDE method in Framework 3.2 to approximate a 100-dimensional Black-Scholes-Barenblatt equation, in Subsection 4.4 we employ an implementation of the deep 2BSDE method in Framework 3.2 to approximate a 100-dimensional Hamilton-Jacobi- Bellman equation, in Subsection 4.5 we employ an implementation of the deep 2BSDE method in Framework 3.2 to approximate a 50-dimensional Allen-Cahn equation, and in Subsection 4.6 we employ implementations of the deep 2BSDE method in Framework 3.2 to approximate nonlinear expectations of G-Brownian motions in 1 and 100 space-dimensions. The Python code used for the implementation of the deep 2BSDE method in Subsec- tion 4.1 can be found in Subsection A.1 below. The Python code used for the implemen- tation of the deep 2BSDE method in Subsection 4.3 can be found in Subsection A.3 below. All of the numerical experiments presented below have been performed in Python 3.6 using TensorFlow 1.2 or TensorFlow 1.3, respectively, on a Lenovo X1 Carbon with a 2.40 Gigahertz (GHz) Intel i7 microprocessor with 8 Megabytes (MB) RAM. 4.1 Allen-Cahn equation with plain gradient descent and no batch normalization In this subsection we use the deep 2BSDE method in Framework 2.1 to approximatively calculate the solution of a 20-dimensional Allen-Cahn equation with a cubic nonlinearity (see (98) below). reference_research_study_list.title: Solving the Kolmogorov PDE by means of deep learning
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
Stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and the Kolmogorov partial
differential equations (PDEs) associated to them have been widely used in
models from engineering, finance, and the natural sciences. In particular, SDEs
and Kolmogorov PDEs, respectively, are highly employed in models for the
approximative pricing of financial derivatives. Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs,
respectively, can typically not be solved explicitly and it has been and still
is an active topic of research to design and analyze numerical methods which
are able to approximately solve Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs, respectively. Nearly
all approximation methods for Kolmogorov PDEs in the literature suffer under
the curse of dimensionality or only provide approximations of the solution of
the PDE at a single fixed space-time point. In this paper we derive and propose
a numerical approximation method which aims to overcome both of the above
mentioned drawbacks and intends to deliver a numerical approximation of the
Kolmogorov PDE on an entire region $[a,b]^d$ without suffering from the curse
of dimensionality. Numerical results on examples including the heat equation,
the Black-Scholes model, the stochastic Lorenz equation, and the Heston model
suggest that the proposed approximation algorithm is quite effective in high
dimensions in terms of both accuracy and speed.

In particular, SDEs and Kolmogorov PDEs, respectively, are highly employed in models for the approximative pricing of п¬Ғnancial derivatives. Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs, respectively, can typically not be solved explicitly and it has been and still is an active topic of research to design and analyze numerical methods which are able to approximately solve Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs, respectively. Nearly all approximation methods for Kolmogorov PDEs in the literature suп¬Җer under the curse of dimensionality or only provide approximations of the solution of the PDE at a single п¬Ғxed space-time point. In this paper we derive and propose a numerical approximation method which aims to overcome both of the above mentioned drawbacks and intends to deliver a numerical approximation of the Kolmogorov PDE on an entire region [ a, b]d without suп¬Җering from the curse of dimensionality. Numerical results on examples including the heat equation, the Black-Scholes model, the stochastic Lorenz equation, and the Heston model suggest that the proposed approximation algorithm is quite eп¬Җective in high dimensions in terms of both accuracy and speed. 1 arXiv:1806.00421v2  [math.NA]  14 Jul 2021Contents 1 Introduction 2 2 Derivation and description of the proposed approximation . . . . . 47 4.5 A Python source code associated to Subsection 3.5 . . . . . . . . . . . . . 48 4.6 A Python source code associated to Subsection 3.6 . . . . . . . . . . . . . 50 1 Introduction Stochastic diп¬Җerential equations (SDEs) and the Kolmogorov partial diп¬Җerential equations (PDEs) associated to them have been widely used in models from engineering, п¬Ғnance, and the natural sciences. In particular, SDEs and Kolmogorov PDEs, respectively, are highly employed in models for the approximative pricing of п¬Ғnancial derivatives. Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs, respectively, can typically not be solved explicitly and it has been and still is an active topic of research to design and analyze numerical methods which are able to approximately solve Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs, respectively (see, e.g., [17], [20], [21], [27], [28], [29], [32], [40], [41], [42], [44], [45], [46], [47], [49], [52]). In particular, there are nowadays several diп¬Җerent types of numerical approximation methods for Kolmogorov 2PDEs in the literature including deterministic numerical approximation methods such as п¬Ғnite diп¬Җerences based approximation methods (cf., for example, [7], [8], [23], [43], [58], [56]) and п¬Ғnite elements based approximation methods (cf., for example, [9], [10], [59]) as well as random numerical approximation methods based on Monte Carlo methods (cf., for example, [17], [20]) and discretizations of the underlying SDEs (cf., for example, [21], [27], [28], [29], [32], [40], [41], [42], [44], [45], [46], [47], [49], [52]). The above mentioned deterministic approximation methods for PDEs work quite eп¬ғciently in one or two space dimensions but cannot be used in the case of high-dimensional PDEs as they suп¬Җer from the so-called curse of dimensionality (cf. Bellman [5]) in the sense that the computational eп¬Җort of the considered approximation algorithm grows exponentially in the PDE dimen- sion. The above mentioned random numerical approximation methods involving Monte Carlo approximations typically overcome this curse of dimensionality but only provide approximations of the Kolmogorov PDE at a single п¬Ғxed space-time point. The key contribution of this paper is to derive and propose a numerical approximation method which aims to overcome both of the above mentioned drawbacks and intends to deliver a numerical approximation of the Kolmogorov PDE on an entire region [a,b]d with- out suп¬Җering from the curse of dimensionality. The numerical scheme, which we propose in this work, is inspired by recently developed deep learning based approximation algorithms for PDEs in the literature (cf., for example, [3], [4], [14], [15], [16], [24], [26], [51], [57]). To derive the proposed approximation scheme we п¬Ғrst reformulate the considered Kolmogorov PDE as a suitable inп¬Ғnite dimensional stochastic optimization problem (see items (ii)вҖ“(iii) in Proposition 2.7 below for details). This inп¬Ғnite dimensional stochastic optimization problem is then temporally discretized by means of suitable discretizations of the under- lying SDE and it is spatially discretized by means of fully connected deep artiп¬Ғcial neural network approximations (see (107) in Subsection 2.6 as well as Subsections 2.4вҖ“2.5 below). The resulting п¬Ғnite dimensional stochastic optimization problem is then solved by means of stochastic gradient descent type optimization algorithms (see (109) in Subsection 2.6, Framework 2.9 in Subsection 2.7, Framework 2.10 in Subsection 2.8, as well as (124)вҖ“(125) in Subsection 3.1). We test the proposed approximation method numerically in the case of several examples of SDEs and PDEs, respectively (see Subsections 3.2вҖ“3.6 below for de- tails). The obtained numerical results indicate that the proposed approximation algorithm is quite eп¬Җective in high dimensions in terms of both accuracy and speed. The remainder of this article is organized as follows. In Section 2 we derive the pro- posed approximation algorithm (see Subsections 2.1вҖ“2.6 below) and we present a detailed description of the proposed approximation algorithm in a special case (see Subsection 2.7 below) as well as in the general case (see Subsection 2.8 below). In Section 3 we test the proposed algorithm numerically in the case of several examples of SDEs and PDEs, respectively. The employed source codes for the numerical simulations in Section 3 are postponed to Section 4. 32 Derivation and description of the proposed approx- imation algorithm In this section we describe the approximation problem which we intend to solve (see Sub- section 2.1 below) and we derive (see Subsections 2.2вҖ“2.6 below) and specify (see Subsec- tions 2.7вҖ“2.8 below) the numerical scheme which we suggest to use to solve this approxima- tion problem (cf., for example, E et al. [14], Han et al. [24], Sirignano & Spiliopoulos [57], Beck et al. [3], Fujii, Takahashi, A., & Takahashi, M. [16], and Henry-Labordere [26] for related derivations and related approximation schemes). 2.1 Kolmogorov partial diп¬Җerential equations (PDEs) Let T вҲҲ (0,вҲһ), d вҲҲ N, let Вө: Rd вҶ’ Rd and Пғ: Rd вҶ’ RdГ—d be Lipschitz continuous functions, let П•: Rd вҶ’R be a function, and let u = (u(t,x))(t,x)вҲҲ[0,T]Г—Rd вҲҲC1,2([0,T] Г— Rd,R) be a function with at most polynomially growing partial derivatives which satisп¬Ғes for every tвҲҲ[0,T], xвҲҲRd that u(0,x) = П•(x) and вҲӮu вҲӮt(t,x) = 1 2 TraceRd ( Пғ(x)[Пғ(x)]вҲ—(Hessxu)(t,x) ) + вҹЁВө(x),(вҲҮxu)(t,x)вҹ©Rd. (1) Our goal is to approximately calculate the function Rd вҲӢxвҶҰвҶ’u(T,x) вҲҲR on some subset of Rd. To п¬Ғx ideas we consider real numbers a,b вҲҲR with a<b and we suppose that our goal is to approximately calculate the function [ a,b]d вҲӢxвҶҰвҶ’u(T,x) вҲҲR. 2.2 On stochastic diп¬Җerential equations and Kolmogorov PDEs In this subsection we provide a probabilistic representation for the solutions of the PDE (1), that is, we recall the classical Feynman-Kac formula for the PDE (1) (cf., for example, Гҳksendal [50, Chapter 8]). Let (в„Ұ,F,P ) be a probability space with a normal п¬Ғltration ( Ft)tвҲҲ[0,T], let W: [0,T] Г— в„Ұ вҶ’Rd be a standard (в„Ұ,F,P ,(Ft)tвҲҲ[0,T])-Brownian motion, and for everyxвҲҲRd let Xx = (Xx t )tвҲҲ[0,T] : [0,T] Г—в„Ұ вҶ’Rd be an ( Ft)tвҲҲ[0,T]-adapted stochastic process with continuous sample paths which satisп¬Ғes that for every tвҲҲ[0,T] it holds P -a.s. that Xx t = x+ вҲ« t 0 Вө(Xx s) ds+ вҲ« t 0 Пғ(Xx s) dWs. (2) The Feynman-Kac formula (cf., for example, Hairer et al. [22, Corollary 4.17 and Remark 4.1]) and (1) hence yield that for every xвҲҲRd it holds that u(T,x) = E [ u(0,Xx T) ] = E [ П•(Xx T) ] . (3) 42.3 Formulation as minimization problem In the next step we exploit (3) to formulate a minimization problem which is uniquely solved by the function [ a,b]d вҲӢx вҶҰвҶ’u(T,x) вҲҲR (cf. (1) above). For this we п¬Ғrst recall the L2-minimization property of the expectation of a real-valued random variable (see Lemma 2.1 below). Then we extend this minimization result to certain random п¬Ғelds (see Proposition 2.2 below). Thereafter, we apply Proposition 2.2 to random п¬Ғelds in the context of the Feynman-Kac representation (3) to obtain Proposition 2.7 below. Proposition 2.7 provides a minimization problem (see, for instance, (93) below) which has the function [a,b]d вҲӢxвҶҰвҶ’u(T,x) вҲҲR as the unique global minimizer. Our proof of Proposition 2.7 is based on the elementary auxiliary results in Lemmas 2.3вҖ“ 2.6. For completeness we also present the proofs of Lemmas 2.3вҖ“2.6 here. The statement and the proof of Lemma 2.3 are based on the proof of Da Prato & Zabczyk [13, Lemma 1.1]. Lemma 2.1. Let (в„Ұ,F,P ) be a probability space and let X: в„Ұ вҶ’ R be an F/B(R)- measurable random variable which satisп¬Ғes E [|X|2] <вҲһ. Then (i) it holds for every yвҲҲR that E [ |XвҲ’y|2] = E [ |XвҲ’E [X]|2] + |E [X] вҲ’y|2, (4) (ii) it holds that there exists a unique real number z вҲҲR such that E [ |XвҲ’z|2] = inf yвҲҲR E [ |XвҲ’y|2] , (5) and (iii) it holds that E [ |XвҲ’E [X]|2] = inf yвҲҲR E [ |XвҲ’y|2] . (6) Proof of Lemma 2.1. Observe that the fact that E [|X|] <вҲһensures that for every yвҲҲR it holds that E [ |XвҲ’y|2] = E [ |XвҲ’E [X] + E [X] вҲ’y|2] = E [ |XвҲ’E [X]|2 + 2(XвҲ’E [X])(E [X] вҲ’y) + |E [X] вҲ’y|2] = E [ |XвҲ’E [X]|2] + 2(E [X] вҲ’y)E [ XвҲ’E [X] ] + |E [X] вҲ’y|2 = E [ |XвҲ’E [X]|2] + |E [X] вҲ’y|2. (7) This establishes item (i). Item (ii) and item (iii) are immediate consequences of item (i). The proof of Lemma 2.1 is thus completed. 5Proposition 2.2. Let a вҲҲR, b вҲҲ(a,вҲһ), let (в„Ұ,F,P ) be a probability space, let X = (Xx)xвҲҲ[a,b]d: [a,b]d Г—в„Ұ вҶ’R be a (B([a,b]d) вҠ—F)/B(R)-measurable function, assume for every xвҲҲ[a,b]d that E [|Xx|2] <вҲһ, and assume that the function [a,b]d вҲӢxвҶҰвҶ’E [Xx] вҲҲR is continuous. Proof of Proposition 2.2. Observe that item (i) in Lemma 2.1 and the hypothesis that вҲҖx вҲҲ[a,b]d: E [|Xx|2] < вҲһensure that for every function u: [a,b]d вҶ’R and every x вҲҲ [a,b]d it holds that E [ |Xx вҲ’u(x)|2] = E [ |Xx вҲ’E [Xx]|2] + |E [Xx] вҲ’u(x)|2. In particular, in this section we apply the proposed approximation algorithm to the heat equation (cf. Subsec- tion 3.2 below), to independent geometric Brownian motions (cf. Subsection 3.3 below), to the Black-Scholes model (cf. Subsection 3.4 below), to stochastic Lorenz equations (cf. Sub- section 3.5 below), and to the Heston model (cf. Subsection 3.6 below). In the case of each of the examples below we employ the general approximation algorithm in Framework 2.10 above in conjunction with the Adam optimizer (cf. Kingma & Ba [38]) with mini-batches of size 8192 in each iteration step (see Subsection 3.1 below for a precise description). Moreover, we employ a fully-connected feedforward neural network with one input layer, two hidden layers, and one one-dimensional output layer in our implementations in the case of each of these examples. We also use batch normalization (cf. Ioп¬Җe & Szegedy [36]) just before the п¬Ғrst linear transformation, just before each of the two nonlinear activation functions in front of the hidden layers as well as just after the last linear transformation. For the two nonlinear activation functions we employ the multidimensional version of the function R вҲӢx вҶҰвҶ’tanh(x) вҲҲ(вҲ’1,1). All weights in the neural network are initialized by means of the Xavier initialization (cf. Glorot & Bengio [18]). All computations were 26performed in single precision (п¬Ӯoat32) on a NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 GPU with 1974 MHz core clock and 8 GB GDDR5X memory with 1809.5 MHz clock rate. The underlying system consisted of an Intel Core i7-6800K CPU with 64 GB DDR4-2133 memory running Tensorп¬Ӯow 1.5 on Ubuntu 16.04. 3.1 Setting Framework 3.1. Lemma 3.2. Let T вҲҲ(0,вҲһ), d вҲҲN, let C вҲҲRdГ—d be a strictly positive and symmetric matrix, and let u: [0,T]Г—Rd вҶ’R be the function which satisп¬Ғes for every (t,x) вҲҲ[0,T]Г—Rd that u(t,x) = вҲҘxвҲҘ2 Rd + tTraceRd(C). (134) Then (i) it holds that uвҲҲCвҲһ([0,T] Г—Rd,R) is at most polynomially growing and (ii) it holds for every tвҲҲ[0,T], xвҲҲRd that (вҲӮu вҲӮt)(t,x) = 1 2 TraceRd ( C(Hessxu)(t,x) ) . (135) Proof of Lemma 3.2. First, note that u is a polynomial. This establishes item (i). More- over, note that for every ( t,x) вҲҲ[0,T] Г—Rd it holds that (вҲӮu вҲӮt)(t,x) = TraceRd(C), (вҲҮxu)(t,x) = 2x, (136) and (Hess xu)(t,x) = (вҲӮ вҲӮx(вҲҮxu) ) (t,x) = 2 IdRd . (137) Hence, we obtain for every ( t,x) вҲҲ[0,T] Г—Rd that (вҲӮu вҲӮt)(t,x) вҲ’1 2 TraceRd ( C(Hessxu)(t,x) ) = TraceRd(C) вҲ’1 2 TraceRd(2C) = 0. (138) This proves item (ii). The proof of Lemma 3.2 is thus completed. 31Lemma 3.5 below discloses the strategy how we approximatively calculate theLвҲһ(О»[0,1]d; R)- errors in (130) and (133) above. Our proof of Lemma 3.5 employs the elementary auxiliary results in Lemma 3.3 and Lemma 3.4 below. For completeness we also include proofs for Lemma 3.3 and Lemma 3.4 here. Lemma 3.3. Let (в„Ұ,F,P ) be a probability space and let A,OвҲҲF satisfy that P (O) = 1. Then it holds that P (A) = P (AвҲ©O). (139) Proof of Lemma 3.3. Observe that the monotonicity of P ensures that P (AвҲ©O) вүӨP (A) = P ( [AвҲ©O] вҲӘ[A\(AвҲ©O)] ) = P (AвҲ©O) + P (A\(AвҲ©O)) = P (AвҲ©O) + P (A\O) вүӨP (AвҲ©O) + P (в„Ұ\O) = P (AвҲ©O) + P (в„Ұ) вҲ’P (O) = P (AвҲ©O). (140) Hence, we obtain that for every A,OвҲҲF with P (O) = 1 it holds that P (A) = P (AвҲ©O). The Proof of Lemma 3.3 is thus completed. Lemma 3.4. Let (в„Ұ,F,P ) be a probability space, let Xn: в„Ұ вҶ’[0,вҲһ), nвҲҲN0, be random variables, assume for every nвҲҲN that P (Xn вүҘXn+1) = 1, and assume for every ОөвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) that lim sup nвҶ’вҲһ P (Xn >Оө) = 0. (141) Then P ( lim sup nвҶ’вҲһ Xn = 0 ) = 1. (142) Proof of Lemma 3.4. Throughout this proof let OвҠҶ в„Ұ be the set given by O= вҲ©вҲһ n=1{Xn вүҘXn+1}= {вҲҖnвҲҲN: Xn вүҘXn+1}. (143) Observe that Lemma 3.3 and the hypothesis that for every n вҲҲN it holds that P (Xn вүҘ Xn+1) = 1 assure that for every N вҲҲN it holds that P (вҲ©N n=1{Xn вүҘXn+1}) = P ([вҲ©NвҲ’1 n=1 {Xn вүҘXn+1}] вҲ©{XN вүҘXN+1}) = P (вҲ©NвҲ’1 n=1 {Xn вүҘXn+1}). (147) Lemma 3.3 and (146) therefore ensure that P ( lim sup nвҶ’вҲһ Xn >0 ) вүӨ вҲһвҲ‘ k=1 [ lim sup mвҶ’вҲһ P ( [вҲӘвҲһ n=m{Xn > 1 k}] вҲ©O )] вүӨ вҲһвҲ‘ k=1 [ lim sup mвҶ’вҲһ P ( [вҲӘвҲһ n=m{Xn > 1 k}] вҲ©{вҲҖnвҲҲN вҲ©[m,вҲһ): Xm вүҘXn} )] = вҲһвҲ‘ k=1 [ lim sup mвҶ’вҲһ P ( {Xm > 1 k}вҲ©{вҲҖ nвҲҲN вҲ©[m,вҲһ): Xm вүҘXn} )] вүӨ вҲһвҲ‘ k=1 [ lim sup mвҶ’вҲһ P ( Xm > 1 k )] . (148) Combining this and (141) establishes (142). The proof of Lemma 3.4 is thus completed. Lemma 3.5. Let d вҲҲN, a вҲҲR, b вҲҲ(a,вҲһ), let f: [a,b]d вҶ’R be a continuous function, let (в„Ұ,F,P ) be a probability space, let Xn: в„Ұ вҶ’[a,b]d, nвҲҲN, be i.i.d. random variables, and assume that X1 is continuous uniformly distributed on [a,b]d. Then 33(i) it holds that P ( lim sup NвҶ’вҲһ вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ [ max 1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) ] вҲ’ [ sup xвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) ]вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ= 0 ) = 1 (149) and (ii) it holds for every pвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) that lim sup NвҶ’вҲһ E пЈ® пЈ° вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ [ max 1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) ] вҲ’ [ sup xвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) ]вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ pпЈ№ пЈ»= 0. (150) Proof of Lemma 3.5. First, observe that the fact that f: [a,b]d вҶ’R is a continuous func- tion and the fact that [a,b]d вҠҶRd is a compact set demonstrate that there exists Оҫ вҲҲ[a,b]d which satisп¬Ғes that f(Оҫ) = supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x). Next note that the fact that for every N вҲҲN, n вҲҲ{1,2,...,N }it holds that f(Xn) вүӨsupxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) implies that for every N вҲҲN it holds that max 1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) вүӨsupxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x). Hence, we obtain that for every N вҲҲN it holds that вҸҗвҸҗ[ max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) ] вҲ’ [ supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) ]вҸҗвҸҗ= [ supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) ] вҲ’ [ max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) ] . (151) Combining this with the fact that f(Оҫ) = supxвҲҲRd f(x) ensures that for every Оө вҲҲ(0,вҲһ), N вҲҲN it holds that { |max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) вҲ’supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x)|вүӨ Оө } = { max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) вүҘsupxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) вҲ’Оө } = N вҲӘ n=1 { f(Xn) вүҘsupxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) вҲ’Оө } = N вҲӘ n=1 { |f(Xn) вҲ’supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x)|вүӨ Оө } = N вҲӘ n=1 { |f(Xn) вҲ’f(Оҫ)|вүӨ Оө } . (152) In the next step we observe that the fact that f: [a,b]d вҶ’R is continuous ensures that for every ОөвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) there exists Оҙ вҲҲ(0,вҲһ) such that for every xвҲҲ[a,b]d with вҲҘxвҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd вүӨОҙ it holds that |f(x) вҲ’f(Оҫ)|вүӨ Оө. Combining this and (152) shows that for every ОөвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) there exists ОҙвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) such that for every N вҲҲN it holds that P ( |max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) вҲ’supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x)|вүӨ Оө ) = P ( вҲӘN n=1 {|f(Xn) вҲ’f(Оҫ)|вүӨ Оө} ) вүҘP ( вҲӘN n=1 {вҲҘXn вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd вүӨОҙ} ) = 1 вҲ’P ( вҲ©N n=1 {вҲҘXn вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ} ) . (153) Hence, we obtain that for every ОөвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) there exists ОҙвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) such that lim inf NвҶ’вҲһ P ( |max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) вҲ’supxвҲҲ[a,b] f(x)|вүӨ Оө ) вүҘ1 вҲ’lim inf NвҶ’вҲһ P ( вҲ©N n=1 {вҲҘXn вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ} ) . (154) 34Next observe that the fact that the random variables Xn: в„Ұ вҶ’[a,b]d, n вҲҲ{1,2,...,N }, are i.i.d. ensures that for every ОҙвҲҲ(0,вҲһ), N вҲҲN it holds that P ( вҲ©N n=1 {вҲҘXn вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ} ) = NвҲҸ n=1 P (вҲҘXn вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ) = [ P (вҲҘX1 вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ) ]N . (155) In addition, note that the fact that for every Оҙ вҲҲ (0,вҲһ) it holds that the set {x вҲҲ [a,b]d: вҲҘxвҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd вүӨОҙ}вҠҶ Rd has strictly positive d-dimensional Lebesgue measure and the fact that X1 is continuous uniformly distributed on [ a,b]d ensure that for every ОҙвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) it holds that P (вҲҘX1 вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ) = 1 вҲ’P (вҲҘX1 вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd вүӨОҙ) <1. (156) Hence, we obtain that for every ОҙвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) it holds that lim sup NвҶ’вҲһ ([ P (вҲҘX1 вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ) ]N) = 0. (157) Combining this with (155) demonstrates that for every ОҙвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) it holds that lim sup NвҶ’вҲһ P ( вҲ©N n=1 {вҲҘXn вҲ’ОҫвҲҘRd >Оҙ} ) = 0. (158) This and (154) assure that for every ОөвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) it holds that lim inf NвҶ’вҲһ P ( |max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) вҲ’supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x)|вүӨ Оө ) = 1. (159) Therefore, we obtain that for every ОөвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) it holds that lim sup NвҶ’вҲһ P ( |max1вүӨnвүӨN f(Xn) вҲ’supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x)|>Оө ) = 0. (160) Combining this with with Lemma 3.4 establishes item (i). It thus remains to prove item (ii). For this note that the fact that f: [a,b]d вҶ’R is globally bounded, item (i), and LebesgueвҖҷs dominated convergence theorem ensure that for every pвҲҲ(0,вҲһ) it holds that lim sup NвҶ’вҲһ E [вҸҗвҸҗmax1вүӨiвүӨN f(Xi) вҲ’supxвҲҲ[a,b]d f(x) вҸҗвҸҗp] = 0. (161) This establishes item (ii). The proof of Lemma 3.5 is thus completed. 353.3 Geometric Brownian motions In this subsection we apply the proposed approximation algorithm to a Black-Scholes PDE with independent underlying geometric Brownian motions. reference_research_study_list.title: Towards a regularity theory for ReLU networksвҖ“chain rule and global error estimates
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
Although for neural networks with locally Lipschitz continuous activation
functions the classical derivative exists almost everywhere, the standard chain
rule is in general not applicable. We will consider a way of introducing a
derivative for neural networks that admits a chain rule, which is both rigorous
and easy to work with. In addition we will present a method of converting
approximation results on bounded domains to global (pointwise) estimates. This
can be used to extend known neural network approximation theory to include the
study of regularity properties. Of particular interest is the application to
neural networks with ReLU activation function, where it contributes to the
understanding of the success of deep learning methods for high-dimensional
partial differential equations.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Towards a regularity theory for ReLU networks вҖ“ chain rule and global error estimates Julius BernerвҲ—, Dennis Elbr ВЁachterвҲ—, Philipp Grohs вҖЎ, Arnulf Jentzen В§ вҲ—Faculty of Mathematics, University of Vienna Oskar-Morgenstern-Platz 1, 1090 Vienna, Austria вҖЎFaculty of Mathematics and Research Platform DataScience@UniVienna, University of Vienna Oskar-Morgenstern-Platz 1, 1090 Vienna, Austria В§Department of Mathematics, ETH Z ВЁurich RВЁamistrasse 101, 8092 Z ВЁurich, Switzerland AbstractвҖ”Although for neural networks with locally Lipschitz continuous activation functions the classical derivative exists almost everywhere, the standard chain rule is in general not applicable. We will consider a way of introducing a derivative for neural networks that admits a chain rule, which is both rigorous and easy to work with. In addition we will present a method of converting approximation results on bounded domains to global (pointwise) estimates. This can be used to extend known neural network approximation theory to include the study of regularity properties. Of particular interest is the application to neural networks with ReLU activation function, where it contributes to the understanding of the success of deep learning methods for high-dimensional partial differential equations. I. I NTRODUCTION It has been observed that deep neural networks exhibit the remarkable capability of overcoming the curse of dimension- ality in a number of different scenarios. In particular, for certain types of high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) there are promising empirical observations [1], [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7] backed by theoretical results for both the approximation error [8], [9], [10], [11] as well as the generalization error [12]. In this context it becomes relevant to not only show how well a given function of interest can be approximated by neural networks but also to extend the study to the derivative of this function. A number of recent publications [13], [14], [15] have investigated the required size of a network which is sufп¬Ғcient to approximate certain interesting (classes of) functions within a given accuracy. This is achieved, п¬Ғrst, by considering the approximation of basic functions by very simple networks and, subsequently, by combining those networks in order to approximate more difп¬Ғcult structures. To extend this approach to include the regularity of the approximation, one requires some kind of chain rule for the composition of neural networks. For neural networks with differentiable activation function the standard chain rule is sufп¬Ғcient. It, however, fails when considering neural networks with an activation function, which is not ev- erywhere differentiable. Although locally Lipschitz continuous functions are w.r.t the Lebesgue measure almost everywhere (a.e.) differentiable, the standard chain rule is not applicable, as, in general, it does not hold even in an вҖҷalmost everywhereвҖҷ sense. We will introduce derivatives of neural networks in a way that admits a chain rule which is both rigorous as well as easy to work with. Chain rules for functions which are not everywhere differentiable have been considered in a more general setting in e.g. [16], [17]. We employ the speciп¬Ғc structure of neural networks to get stronger results using simpler arguments. In particular it allows for a stability result, i.e. Lemma III.3, the application of which will be discussed in Section V . We would also like to mention a very recent work [18] about approximation in Sobolev norms, where they deal with the issue by using a general bound for the Sobolev norm of the composition of functions from the Sobolev space W1,вҲһ. Note however that this approach leads to a certain factor depending on the dimensions of the domains of the functions, which can be avoided with our method. For ease of exposition, we formulate our results for neural networks with the ReLU activation function. We, however, consider in Section IV how such a chain rule can be obtained for any activation function which is locally Lipschitz continuous (with at most countably many points at which it is not differentiable). In Section V we brieп¬Ӯy sketch how the results from Section III can be utilized to get approximation results for certain classes of functions. Subsequently, in Section VI, we present a general method of deriving global error estimates from such approximation results, which are naturally obtained for bounded domains. Ultimately, we discuss how our results can be used to extend known theory, enabling the further study of the approximation of PDE solutions by neural networks. II. S ETTING As in [14], we consider a neural network ОҰ to be a п¬Ғnite sequence of matrix-vector pairs, i.e. ОҰ = ((Ak,bk))L k=1, (1) where Ak вҲҲRNkГ—NkвҲ’1 and bk вҲҲRNk for some depth LвҲҲN and layer dimensions N0,N1,...,N L вҲҲN. The realization of the neural network ОҰ is the function RОҰ: RN0 вҶ’RNL given by RОҰ = WL в—ҰReLU в—ҰWLвҲ’1 в—Ұ... в—ҰReLU в—ҰW1, (2) where Wk(x) = Akx+ bk for every xвҲҲRNk and where ReLU(x) := (max{0,x1},..., max{0,xN}) (3) arXiv:1905.04992v1  [cs.LG]  13 May 2019for every xвҲҲRN. We distinguish between a neural network and its realization, since ОҰ uniquely induces RОҰ, while in general there can be multiple non-trivially different neural networks with the same realization. The representation of a neural network as a structured set of weights as in (1) allows the introduction of notions of network sizes. While there are slight differences between various publications, commonly considered quantities are the depth (i.e. number of afп¬Ғne transformations), the connectivity (i.e. number of non-zero entries of the Ak and bk), and the weight bound (i.e. maximum of the absolute values of the entries of the Ak and bk). In [15] it has been shown that these three quantities determine the length of a bit string which is sufп¬Ғcient to encode the network with a prescribed quantization error. In the following let ОҰ=(( Ak,bk))L k=1, ОЁ=(( ЛңAk,Лңbk)) ЛңL k=1 (4) be neural networks with matching dimensions in the sense that RОҰ: Rd вҶ’Rm and RОЁ: Rm вҶ’Rn. We then deп¬Ғne their composition as ОЁ вҠҷОҰ := ( ((Ak,bk))LвҲ’1 k=1 ,( ЛңA1AL, ЛңA1bL + Лңb1),(( ЛңAk,Лңbk)) ЛңL k=2 ) . (5) Direct computation shows R(ОЁ вҠҷОҰ) = RОЁ в—ҰRОҰ. (6) Note that the realization RОҰ of a neural network ОҰ is continu- ous piecewise linear (CPL) as a composition of CPL functions. Consequently, it is Lipschitz continuous and the realization RОҰ is almost everywhere differentiable by RademacherвҖҷs theorem. In particular all three functions in (6) are a.e. differ- entiable. This, however, is not sufп¬Ғcient to get the derivative of R(ОЁвҠҷОҰ) from the derivatives of RОЁ and RОҰ by use of the classical chain rule. Consider the very simple counterexample of u(x) := ReLU( x) and v(x) := 0 and formally apply the chain rule, i.e. (D(uв—Ұv))(x) = (Du)(v(x)) В·(Dv)(x). (7) Even though (Du)(y) is well-deп¬Ғned for every y вҲҲR\{0}, the expression (Du)(v(x)) is deп¬Ғned for no xвҲҲR. In general this problem occurs when the inner function maps a set of positive measure into a set where the derivative of the outer function does not exist. Now in this case, one can directly see that setting (Du)(0) to any arbitrary value would cause (7) to provide the correct result since (Dv)(x) = 0. III. R ELU NETWORK DERIVATIVE We proceed by deп¬Ғning the derivative of an arbitrary neural network in a way such that it not only coincides a.e. with the derivative of the realization, but also admits a chain rule. To this end let H: RN вҶ’RNГ—N be the function given by H(x) := diag(1 (0,вҲһ)(x1),..., 1 (0,вҲһ)(xN)) (8) for every x = ( x1,...,x N) вҲҲ RN and let RKОҰ := R((Ak,bk))K k=1. We then deп¬Ғne the neural network derivative of ОҰ as the function DОҰ: RN0 вҶ’RNLГ—N0 given by DОҰ := AL В·H(RLвҲ’1ОҰ) В·ALвҲ’1 В·... В·H(R1ОҰ) В·A1. (9) Note that this deп¬Ғnition is motivated by formally applying the chain rule with the convention that the derivative ofmax{0, В·} is zero at the origin. Now we need to verify that this is justiп¬Ғed. Theorem III.1. It holds for almost everyxвҲҲRd that (DОҰ)(x) = (D(RОҰ))(x). (10) Proof. Let v: Rd вҶ’RN be a locally Lipschitz continuous function, deп¬Ғne w:= ReLU в—Ұv, and Li := {xвҲҲRd: wi(x) = 0}= {xвҲҲRd: vi(x) вүӨ0}. (11) We now use an observation about differentiability on level sets (see e.g. [19, Thm 3.3(i)]), which states that (Dwi)(x) = 0 for almost every xвҲҲLi. (12) As wi(x) = vi(x) for every xвҲҲRd\Li, we get a.e. Dwi = 1 Rd\Li В·Dvi = 1 (0,вҲһ)(vi) В·Dvi (13) and consequently D(ReLU в—Ұv) = H(v) В·Dv. (14) The claim follows by induction over the layers K = 1,...,L of ОҰ, using (14) with v= RKОҰ for the induction step. Note that even for convex RОҰ the values of DОҰ on the nullset do not necessarily lie in the respective subdifferentials of RОҰ, as can be seen in Figure 1. Although Theorem III.1 holds regardless of which value is chosen for the derivative of max{0, В·} at the origin, no choice will guarantee that all values of DОҰ lie in the respective subdifferentials of RОҰ. Here we have set the derivative at the origin to zero, following the convention of software implementations for deep learning applications, e.g. TensorFlow and PyTorch. Using (5) and (9) one can verify by direct computation that Dobeys the chain rule. Corollary III.2. It holds for everyxвҲҲRd that (D(ОЁ вҠҷОҰ))(x) = (DОЁ)(RОҰ(x)) В·(DОҰ)(x). (15) Note that (15) is well-deп¬Ғned as DОЁ exists everywhere, although it only coincides with D(RОЁ) almost everywhere. Theorem III.1 however guarantees that we still have a.e. D(ОЁ вҠҷОҰ) = D(R(ОЁ вҠҷОҰ)) = D(RОЁ в—ҰRОҰ). (16) Next we provide a technical result dealing with the stability of our chain rule, which will prove to be useful in Section V. Lemma III.3. It holds for almost everyxвҲҲRd that lim yвҶ’RОҰ(x) [ (DОЁ)(y) вҲ’(DОЁ)(RОҰ(x)) ] В·(DОҰ)(x) = 0. (17) Proof. We п¬Ғrst show for every locally Lipschitz continuous function u: Rm вҶ’RN and for almost every xвҲҲRd that lim yвҶ’RОҰ(x) [H(u(y)) вҲ’H(u(RОҰ(x)))] В·(D(u в—ҰRОҰ))(x) = 0. (18) If ui(RОҰ(x)) Мё= 0 we have lim yвҶ’RОҰ(x) 1 (0,вҲһ)(ui(y)) = 1 (0,вҲһ)(ui(RОҰ(x))) (19)as ui is continuous and 1 (0,вҲһ) is continuous on R\{0}. Furthermore, [19, Thm 3.3(i)] implies that (D(ui в—ҰRОҰ))(x) = 0 (20) for almost every xвҲҲRd with ui(RОҰ(x)) = 0. Since a п¬Ғnite union of nullsets is again a nullset, this proves the claim (18). The lemma follows by induction over the layers K = 1,..., ЛңL of ОЁ and applying (18) with u= RKОЁ. IV. G ENERAL ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS As mentioned in the introduction, it is possible to replace the ReLU activation function in (2) by some locally Lipschitz continuous, component-wise applied function Пұ: R вҶ’R with an at most countably large set S of points where Пұ is not differentiable. Speciп¬Ғcally, one can deп¬Ғne the neural network derivative (with activation functionПұ) as in (9) with 1 (0,вҲһ)(xi) in (8) replaced by ( ВҜDПұ)(xi) := { 0, x i вҲҲS (DПұ)(xi), else . (21) The chain rule can, again, be checked by direct computation and it is straightforward to adapt Theorem III.1 to this more general setting by considering the level sets {xвҲҲRd: wi(x) = s}, s вҲҲS. (22) If additionally ВҜDПұ is continuous on R \S, the proof of Lemma III.3 translates without any modiп¬Ғcations. V. U TILIZATION IN APPROXIMATION THEORY These results can now be employed to bound the LвҲһ-norm of D(ОЁ в—ҰОҰ) вҲ’D(uв—Ұv), given corresponding estimates for the approximation of uand v by ОЁ and ОҰ, respectively. Here, one has to take some care when bounding the term вҲҘ[DОЁ в—ҰRОҰ вҲ’Duв—ҰRОҰ] DОҰвҲҘLвҲһ (23) by вҲҘDОЁ вҲ’DuвҲҘLвҲһвҲҘDОҰвҲҘLвҲһ. (24) Again it can happen that RОҰ maps a set of positive measure into a nullset where the estimate for the approximation of Du by DОЁ in the essential supremum norm is not valid. However, using the stability result in Lemma III.3 one can for almost every x вҲҲRd shift to a sufп¬Ғciently close point y вүҲRОҰ(x) where the estimate holds. In [13] Yarotsky explicitly constructs networks whose realization is a linear interpolation 1 of the squaring function (see Fig. 1 for illustration), which directly gives an estimate on the approximation rate for the derivatives. These simple networks can then be combined to get networks approximating multiplication, polynomials and eventually, by means of e.g. local Taylor approximation, functions f whose п¬Ғrst n вүҘ 1 (weak) derivatives are bounded. This leads to estimates of the form вҲҘf вҲ’RОҰОө,BвҲҘLвҲһ(IB) вүӨОө, (25) 1The interpolation points are uniformly distributed over the domain of approximation and their number grows exponentially with the size of the networks. Fig. 1. Approximation of the function x вҶҰвҶ’x2 and its derivative on the interval [вҲ’4, 4] by a neural network ОҰ with depth 6, connectivity 52 and weight bound 4. Note that not all values of DОҰ at the points of non- differentiablity of RОҰ lie between the values at either side, i.e. in the subdifferential. with IB = [ вҲ’B,B]d, including estimates for the scaling of the size of the network ОҰОө,B w.r.t. B and Оө. As these constructions are based on composing simpler functions with known estimates one can now employ Theorem III.1 and Corollary III.2 to show that the derivatives of those networks also approximate the derivative of the function, i.e. вҲҘDf вҲ’DОҰОө,BвҲҘLвҲһ(IB) вүӨcОөr. (26) Such constructive approaches can further be found in [8], in [14] for ОІ-cartoon-like functions, in [20] for (b,Оө)- holomorphic maps, and in [15] for high-frequent sinusoidal functions. VI. G LOBAL ERROR ESTIMATES The error estimates above are usually only sensible for bounded domains, as the realization of a neural network is always CPL with a п¬Ғnite number of pieces. We brieп¬Ӯy discuss a general way of transforming them into global pointwise error estimates, which can be useful in the context of PDEs (see e.g.x = RОҰОө/2,BОө+1(x) вүҲ1 [вҲ’BОө,BОө]d(x) ОҰmult Оө/2,bОө вүҲ1 [вҲ’BОө,BОө]df(x) ОҰchar BОө ОҰОө/2,BОө+1 Fig. 2. The neural networks ОҰОө approximating f globally. [9], [10]). In the following assume that we have a function f with an at most polynomially growing derivative, i.e. вҲҘ(Df)(x)вҲҘ2 вүӨc(1 + вҲҘxвҲҘОә 2 ). (27) Denote by ОҰchar B a neural network which represents the d- dimensional approximate characteristic function of IB, i.e. RОҰchar B (x) вҲҲ[0,1] and RОҰchar B (x) = 1, x вҲҲIB, RОҰchar B (x) = 0, x /вҲҲIB+1. (28) See [15, Proof of Thm. VIII.3] for such a construction. Further let ОҰmult Оө,b be the neural network approximating the multiplication function on [вҲ’b,b]2 with error Оө (see e.g. [20, Prop. 3.1]). Now we deп¬Ғne the global approximation networks ОҰОө as the composition of ОҰmult Оө/2,bОө with the parallelization of ОҰchar BОө and ОҰОө/2,BОө+1 for suitable BОө вҲҲO(ОөвҲ’1) and bОө вҲҲO(ОөвҲ’ОәвҲ’1). (29) See Figure 2 for an illustration and e.g. [14, Def. 2.7] for a formal deп¬Ғnition of parallelization. Considering the errors on IB, IB+1\IB and Rd\IB+1 leads to global estimates, i.e. for every xвҲҲRd |f(x) вҲ’RОҰОө(x)|вүӨ Оө(1 + вҲҘxвҲҘОә+2 2 ) (30) and, by use of the chain rule III.2, for almost every xвҲҲRd вҲҘ(Df)(x) вҲ’(DОҰОө)(x)вҲҘ2 вүӨCОөr(1 + вҲҘxвҲҘОә+2 2 ). (31) Due to the logarithmic size scaling of the multiplication network, the size of ОҰОө can be bounded by the size of ОҰОө/2,BОө+1 plus an additional term in O(d+ Оәlog ОөвҲ’1). VII. A PPLICATION TO PDE S Analyzing the regularity properties of neural networks was motivated by the recent successful application of deep learning methods to PDEs [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7], [11]. Initiated by empirical experiments [1] it has been proven that neural net- works are capable of overcoming the curse of dimensionality for solving so-called Kolmogorov PDEs [12]. More precisely, the solution to the empirical risk minimization problem over a class of neural networks approximates the solution of the PDE up to error Оө with high probability and with size of the networks and number of samples scaling only polynomially in the dimension d and ОөвҲ’1. The above requires a suitable learning problem and a sufп¬Ғciently good approximation of the solution function by neural networks. For Kolmogorov PDEs, this boils down to calculating global Lipschitz coefп¬Ғcients and error estimates for neural networks approximating the initial condition and coefп¬Ғcient functions (see e.g. [9], [10]). Employing estimates of the form (26) one can bound the derivative on IB, i.e. LB := вҲҘDОҰОө,BвҲҘLвҲһ(IB) вүӨвҲҘDfвҲҘLвҲһ(IB) + cОөr. (32) Using molliп¬Ғcation and the mean value theorem we can establish local Lipschitz estimates, i.e. for all x,y вҲҲ(вҲ’B,B)d that |RОҰОө,B(x) вҲ’RОҰОө,B(y)|вүӨ LBвҲҘxвҲ’yвҲҘ2, (33) and corresponding linear growth bounds |RОҰОө,B(x)|вүӨ ( |RОҰОө,B(0)|+ LB ) (1 + вҲҘxвҲҘ2). (34) Similarly, one can use (31) to obtain estimates of the form |RОҰОө(x)вҲ’RОҰОө(y)|вүӨ C(1+вҲҘxвҲҘОә+2 2 +вҲҘyвҲҘОә+2 2 )вҲҘxвҲ’yвҲҘ2 (35) for all x,y вҲҲRd (which are demanded in [10, Theorem 1.1]). Moreover, note that the capability to produce approximation results which include error estimates for the derivative is of signiп¬Ғcant independent interest. Various numerical methods (for instance Galerkin methods) rely on bounding the error in some Sobolev norm вҲҘВ·вҲҘ W1,p, which requires estimates of the derivative differences. We believe that the possibility to obtain regularity estimates signiп¬Ғcantly contributes to the mathematical theory of neural networks and allows for further advances in the numerical approximation of high dimensional partial differential equations. VIII. R ELATION TO BACKPROPAGATION IN TRAINING The approach discussed here could further be applied to the training of neural networks by (stochastic) gradient descent. Note, however, that this is a slightly different setting. From the approximation theory perspective we were interested in the derivative of x вҶҰвҶ’RОҰ(x), while in training one requires the derivative of ОҰ вҶҰвҶ’ RОҰ(xвҲ—) for some п¬Ғxed sample xвҲ—. In particular this function is no longer CPL but rather con- tinuous piecewise polynomial. While this would necessitate some technical modiп¬Ғcations, we believe that it should be possible to employ the method used here in order to show that the gradient of ОҰ вҶҰвҶ’RОҰ(xвҲ—) coincides a.e. with what is computed by backpropagation using the convention of setting the derivative of max{0,В·}to zero at the origin (as well as similar conventions for e.g. max-pooling).
