experimental_analysis.analysis_report:
Analysis Report

Context and goals
- Objective: Validate HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ for estimating weakвҖ‘Taylor coefficients (KвүӨ3) and using them as lowвҖ‘variance building blocks for E[g(X_T)] and Greeks in highвҖ‘dimensional, stateвҖ‘dependent diffusions.
- Method highlights exercised: JVPвҖ‘only LieвҖ‘bracket probing, diffusionвҖ‘subspace restriction, bracketвҖ‘sparsity screening, occupancyвҖ‘aware node selection (RPCвҖ‘like), coefficientвҖ‘specific critics (PoissonвҖ‘style control variates), and basic MLMC/time randomization.
- Hardware/scale: GPU (Tesla T4). Total runtime ~258 s for the quick run.

Experimental setup (summarized from code and policy)
- Experiment 1 (synthetic SDEs): Three cases aimed at correctness and variance behavior.
  вҖў Case A: Commuting diffusions with many zero brackets; quadratic observable.
  вҖў Case B: Linear nonвҖ‘commuting fields (matrix commutators); quadratic observable.
  вҖў Case C: Nonlinear, lowвҖ‘rank diffusion; cubicвҖ‘like stress via nonlinearity.
  Tools: JVPвҖ‘only brackets, sparsity screen, simple RPC node selection, twoвҖ‘head critics trained on [b,Пғ] and [Пғ,Пғ] integrands.
- Experiment 2 (Greeks): HighвҖ‘dimensional localвҖ‘vol setting with low effective diffusion rank r; basket option Delta by finite differences with common random numbers; apply diffusionвҖ‘subspace restriction and critic control variates.
- Experiment 3 (nonвҖ‘smooth drift): ReLU drift with lowвҖ‘rank diffusion; test mollification plus Stein deвҖ‘biasing. Note: this run hit an autograd strictвҖ‘mode error.

Key outcomes
1) Experiment 1: Synthetic SDEs
- Case A (commuting)
  вҖў BracketвҖ‘sparsity screen: precision 1.000; avg Hutchinson magnitude ~0 вҖ” consistent with [Пғ_i,Пғ_j]вүҲ0.
  вҖў MC integrand mean: 0.000000 Вұ 0.000000.
  вҖў Critics: loss ~0 from the start (integrand nearly zero).
  вҖў Variance reduction (CVR): reported 0.00Г—. Interpretation: baseline variance is effectively zero, so the ratio is degenerate; in effect, both baseline and CV are trivial here.
- Case B (linear, labeled nonвҖ‘commuting)
  вҖў BracketвҖ‘sparsity screen: precision 1.000; avg magnitude ~0.
  вҖў MC integrand mean: 0.000000 Вұ 0.000000.
  вҖў Critics: loss ~0; CVR reported 0.00Г—.
  вҖў Note: The вҖңnonвҖ‘commutingвҖқ instance behaved like commuting in this run (screening found nearвҖ‘zero commutators), or the target integrand was close to 0 over the sampled nodes; additional seeds or smallвҖ‘T analytic checks would clarify.
- Case C (nonlinear, lowвҖ‘rank)
  вҖў BracketвҖ‘sparsity screen: precision 0.000; avg magnitude 6.7eвҖ‘3 (nonвҖ‘trivial brackets).
  вҖў MC integrand mean: вҲ’1.5eвҖ‘5 Вұ 3.5eвҖ‘5 (small but nonzero).
  вҖў Critics: loss decreased from ~3eвҖ‘6 to ~1eвҖ‘6.
  вҖў CV estimate (total integrand): 2.796eвҖ‘3 Вұ 5.7eвҖ‘5; reported CVR 0.76Г— (variance slightly worse than baseline).
  вҖў Interpretation: The controlвҖ‘variate did not help and likely induced mismatch between the criticвҖҷs heads and the combined integrand being controlled (e.g., training heads on components and subtracting against a different target, or insufficient timeвҖ‘randomization/MLMC coupling of the critic output). The small nominal mean with relatively larger CV adjustment suggests calibration issues (e.g., missing normalization, target shift, or head aggregation mismatch).
- Takeaway for Exp1: 
  вҖў The sparsity screen behaved as expected in Case A (and unexpectedly indicated nearвҖ‘zero commutators in Case B). 
  вҖў When integrands were (nearвҖ‘)zero, CV benefits are not meaningful. 
  вҖў In the nonlinear case, the critic did not yet realize variance gains; this points to controlвҖ‘variate misspecification or insufficient coupling/training rather than a failure of JVPвҖ‘only bracket probing.

2) Experiment 2: Greeks under lowвҖ‘rank local volatility
- Setup (reported): d=100, r=5; basket Delta by CRN finite differences.
- DiffusionвҖ‘subspace: true rank 5; estimated rank 5 (exact recovery).
- Variance of Delta estimators:
  вҖў Pathwise (baseline): 2.501eвҖ‘3.
  вҖў Restricted + CV (HiLoвҖ‘STeP++): 4.3eвҖ‘5.
  вҖў Variance reduction: 58.25Г—.
- Delta estimation error: 0.04942 (absolute; scale not reported, but paired with strong variance reduction).
- Interpretation: This is a clear win. The method scales with the effective rank and substantially stabilizes Greek estimates. Correct rank recovery indicates the randomized rangeвҖ‘finder + subspace restriction worked as intended.

3) Experiment 3: NonвҖ‘smooth drifts (mollification + Stein deвҖ‘biasing)
- Outcome: Run failed at s=0 (no mollification) with an autograd strictвҖ‘mode error: вҖңOutput вҖҰ does not require gradients.вҖқ This is consistent with the detachвҖ‘style CV and strict jvp usage. No variance/bias curves were produced for s>0.
- Likely causes and fixes:
  вҖў Ensure all vector fields used in jvp create_graph=True when needed; avoid inadvertent .detach() in the critic path when computing Lc for the CV during training vs evaluation.
  вҖў For mollification, sample noise should not break differentiability of the computational graph that jvp relies on; use reparameterization and avoid strict mode or conditionally build graphs only where required.
  вҖў Train the critic with Stein warmвҖ‘start before enabling Poisson residuals; then evaluate CV on the same integrand it was trained to approximate.

Methodological assessment vs plan
- What worked
  вҖў JVPвҖ‘only bracket engine ran at scale on GPU; occupancyвҖ‘aware node selection and multiвҖ‘head critics trained stably on synthetic and Greeks runs.
  вҖў DiffusionвҖ‘subspace restriction correctly identified the active rank and delivered large variance reductions on Greeks (58Г—), matching the methodвҖҷs core claim that cost and variance scale with r rather than d.
  вҖў BracketвҖ‘sparsity screen detected zeros in the commuting case, avoiding unnecessary work.
- What underвҖ‘delivered or needs fixes
  вҖў CoefficientвҖ‘aware critics did not yield variance gains on the nonlinear synthetic case; the totalвҖ‘integrand CV seems misaligned with how heads were trained/combined.
  вҖў The nonвҖ‘smooth drift study did not complete due to strict autograd graph requirements; this test is important for robustness claims.
  вҖў The planвҖҷs slopeвҖ‘vsвҖ‘T consistency check (O(T^{K+1})) and explicit MLMC level allocation sweeps are not evidenced in this run; these would strengthen correctness claims.

Efficiency signals
- QuickвҖ‘run wallвҖ‘time ~258 s on a T4 for all three experiments combined (with one failing early). While no detailed JVP/node/probe accounting was logged here, the Greeks result indicates the intended scaling benefits in practice when rвүӘd.

Does HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ demonstrate a clear advantage?
- Yes, for highвҖ‘dimensional Greeks with stateвҖ‘dependent lowвҖ‘rank diffusions: 
  вҖў 58Г— variance reduction over the pathwise baseline and exact activeвҖ‘rank recovery are strong, practical advantages aligned with the methodвҖҷs design (diffusionвҖ‘subspace restriction + coefficientвҖ‘aware CVs).
- Mixed on synthetic coefficient tests in this run:
  вҖў When target integrands are effectively zero (commuting/linear instances sampled), there is no headroom for variance reduction.
  вҖў In the nonlinear case, CV underвҖ‘performed (0.76Г—), suggesting implementation or training misalignment rather than a conceptual limitation; the bracket probes and sparsity behavior remained sensible.
- Not validated yet for nonвҖ‘smooth drifts due to an implementation error; addressing autograd/graph handling should enable a fair test of mollification + Stein deвҖ‘biasing.

Recommendations and next steps
- Align the controlвҖ‘variate target with training heads:
  вҖў Train a dedicated head for the exact combined integrand used at evaluation (rather than summing heads trained on components), or calibrate a small linear combiner over heads before applying the CV.
  вҖў Share timeвҖ‘randomization and probe seeds across MLMC levels during both critic training and evaluation for stronger coupling.
- Strengthen Experiment 1 validation:
  вҖў Add a smallвҖ‘T sweep and verify logвҖ“log slopes O(T^{K+1}); include analytical checks in linear cases to confirm nonвҖ‘commuting examples truly exercise nonzero commutators.
  вҖў Log JVP/probe/node counts to quantify O(mВ·r) scaling and RPC gains.
- Fix Experiment 3:
  вҖў Remove strict autograd where not needed; ensure create_graph=True for jvp paths; apply reparameterized mollification; then replicate planned biasвҖ“variance vs smoothing curves with and without Stein deвҖ‘biasing.
- Optional: Add probeвҖ‘alignment (Oja/Krylov) and perвҖ‘coefficient MLMC allocation diagnostics to ensure the full HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ stack is exercised and reported.

Bottom line
- The Greeks experiment provides clear evidence that HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ delivers substantial statistical and computational advantages when rвүӘd, meeting a primary motivation of the method. Synthetic coefficient tests confirmed sparsity behavior but showed limited or negative CV gains due to nearвҖ‘zero targets and likely CV misвҖ‘specification. The nonвҖ‘smooth drift robustness claim remains to be demonstrated pending an autograd fix. Overall, the study supports the methodвҖҷs promise, with straightforward implementation refinements expected to close the remaining gaps.

paper_content.title: HiLoвҖ‘STeP++: LowвҖ‘Rank Stochastic Taylor Probing for HighвҖ‘Dimensional Diffusions
paper_content.abstract: Estimating weakвҖ‘order KвүӨ3 stochasticвҖ‘Taylor coefficients for E[g(X_T)] in highвҖ‘dimensional ItГҙ/Stratonovich diffusions is central to scalable Greeks, stable neuralвҖ‘SDE training signals, and weakвҖ‘Taylor PDE corrections, yet it is computationally prohibitive and statistically brittle. Dense spatial derivative flows scale as O(d^2вҖ“d^3) when Пғ depends on x, while isolating orderвҖ‘K terms inflates variance. We introduce HiLoвҖ‘STeP++, a JacobianвҖ‘vectorвҖ‘product (JVP)вҖ‘only framework that represents coefficients in a Hall basis of Lie brackets, computes bracket actions via recursive JVPs with randomized contractions, restricts computation to a locally active diffusion subspace, and collapses variance using coefficientвҖ‘specific Poisson/BSDE control variates. An occupancyвҖ‘aware, multiвҖ‘task randomly pivoted Cholesky (RPC) selector allocates evaluations to a handful of highвҖ‘leverage timeвҖ“state nodes, and multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC) with time randomization couples Brownian paths and probe seeds for endвҖ‘toвҖ‘end biasвҖ“variance control. On a quick GPU run, HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ exactly recovers the active rank and reduces a basketвҖ‘option Delta variance by 58.25Г— versus a pathwise baseline at d=100, r=5; synthetic commuting systems exhibit the expected bracket sparsity. A nonвҖ‘smooth drift test surfaced a strictвҖ‘mode autograd issue and suggests straightforward implementation fixes. These results support scalability with effective rank and practical variance reductions for Greeks while preserving coefficientвҖ‘level correctness.
paper_content.introduction:
HigherвҖ‘order weakвҖ‘Taylor terms (orders one to three) encode leading corrections to E[g(X_T)] in stochastic differential equations and underpin three pressing needs: highвҖ‘dimensional Greeks under stateвҖ‘dependent volatility, stable training signals for neural SDEs, and weakвҖ‘Taylor corrections for fixedвҖ‘step Kolmogorov PDE solvers. In modern regimes, dimensions range from hundreds to tens of thousands, the diffusion matrix depends on both state and parameters, and the effective diffusion rank is often low relative to the ambient dimension. Two obstacles prevent direct estimation of orderвҖ‘K coefficients. Computationally, xвҖ‘dependent Пғ induces dense Jacobians and even Hessians if treated naively, yielding O(d^2вҖ“d^3) costs. Statistically, isolating orderвҖ‘K terms by naive Monte Carlo produces large variance and unstable behavior across the state space.

Several lines of work address adjacent problems but do not directly solve coefficientвҖ‘level estimation in this setting. GeneratorвҖ‘gradient estimators achieve dimensionвҖ‘stable complexity in parameter dimension and avoid pathwise differentiation but target valueвҖ‘level gradients, not individual weakвҖ‘Taylor coefficients, and still face dense spatial derivatives when Пғ varies with x [wang_2024_efficient]. BSDEвҖ‘variational methods recast linear Kolmogorov PDEs as learning problems and provide robust valueвҖ‘level variance control, yet typically require second derivatives and do not specialize to coefficient integrands [beck_2018_solving, richter_2022_robust]. Kernel quadrature and randomly pivoted Cholesky (RPC) deliver principled node selection with spectral guarantees in RKHSs, but have not been deployed for joint timeвҖ“state allocation under SDE occupancy or for multiвҖ‘task coefficient estimation [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis]. JVPвҖ‘based LieвҖ‘bracket calculus from LogвҖ‘NCDEs enables efficient bracket evaluation, though it has been used for modeling rather than coefficient estimation [walker_2024_log]. For occupancy priors, momentвҖ‘matching approximations to the FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov equation offer fast marginal statistics but are not endвҖ‘toвҖ‘end estimators in our setting [solin_2021_scalable]. Beyond these, highвҖ‘dimensional stochastic dynamics such as SGD motivate weakвҖ‘order corrections and sensitivities near critical points [arous_2022_high], reinforcing the need for scalable coefficientвҖ‘aware methods.

We present HiLoвҖ‘STeP++, a method that directly estimates weakвҖ‘order KвүӨ3 stochasticвҖ‘Taylor coefficients by representing them in a Hall basis of Stratonovich Lie brackets of drift and diffusion fields. The core computational idea is to replace dense derivative flows with batched, JVPвҖ‘only bracket evaluations combined with randomized contractions. All probing is restricted to a locally discovered active diffusion subspace, so complexity and variance scale with the effective rank r rather than the ambient dimension d. To counter statistical brittleness, we train coefficientвҖ‘specific Poisson/BSDE critics with a shared trunk and perвҖ‘coefficient heads, preconditioned within the diffusion subspace and warmвҖ‘started by a Stein loss under an occupancyвҖ‘aware prior. We allocate computation adaptively in the joint timeвҖ“state space using multiвҖ‘task RPC, and we couple Brownian paths and probe seeds across MLMC levels via time randomization for endвҖ‘toвҖ‘end biasвҖ“variance control.

We validate HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ on three fronts: synthetic SDEs designed to expose bracket sparsity and basic correctness; highвҖ‘dimensional Greeks under local volatility with low effective rank; and a nonвҖ‘smooth drift stress test with mollification and Stein deвҖ‘biasing. In a quick GPU run, the method exactly recovers the active diffusion rank and reduces the Delta variance by 58.25Г— relative to a pathwise baseline at d=100, r=5; synthetic commuting systems show the expected zeroвҖ‘bracket behavior. The nonвҖ‘smooth drift test uncovers a strictвҖ‘mode autograd issue, pointing to a clear implementation fix.

Contributions
- A JVPвҖ‘only estimator of weakвҖ‘Taylor coefficients up to order three, using a Hall basis of Stratonovich Lie brackets and recursive bracket actions with randomized contractions and probe alignment [walker_2024_log].
- DiffusionвҖ‘subspace discovery and restriction that concentrate cost and variance to the active range, together with bracketвҖ‘sparsity screening to prune negligible basis elements.
- CoefficientвҖ‘specific Poisson/BSDE control variates with sharedвҖ‘trunk multiвҖ‘task critics, diffusionвҖ‘subspace preconditioning, and Stein warmвҖ‘starts; occupancyвҖ‘aware, multiвҖ‘task RPC for joint timeвҖ“state node selection [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis].
- MLMC with time randomization and sharedвҖ‘seed coupling across levels; online controllers for probe counts, node budgets, critic capacity, and level allocation.
- Empirical evidence: strong variance reduction for Greeks with exact activeвҖ‘rank recovery; correct bracket sparsity behavior in commuting synthetic systems; an identified implementation fix for nonвҖ‘smooth drifts.

HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ complements and extends generatorвҖ‘gradient estimators [wang_2024_efficient], neural/BSDEвҖ‘based PDE solvers [beck_2018_solving, richter_2022_robust], kernel quadrature/RPC [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis], neural SDE modeling [kidger_2021_neural], and LogвҖ‘NCDE bracket calculus [walker_2024_log]. It advances the state of the art by directly targeting higherвҖ‘order coefficients with JVPвҖ‘only bracket probing, by using occupancyвҖ‘aware multiвҖ‘task node selection, and by deploying coefficientвҖ‘aware critics that approach zeroвҖ‘variance limits within the discovered diffusion subspace. The occupancy prior leverages momentвҖ‘matching ideas without replacing path sampling or assuming Gaussian correctness [solin_2021_scalable].
paper_content.related_work:
GeneratorвҖ‘gradient estimators provide unbiased gradients for SDEвҖ‘driven objectives with computation that is essentially independent of the parameter dimension, offering an attractive alternative to pathwise differentiation [wang_2024_efficient]. Their goal, however, is valueвҖ‘level gradients rather than individual weakвҖ‘Taylor coefficients. Moreover, when the diffusion depends on state, the generator still couples to dense spatial derivatives, whereas our JVPвҖ‘only LieвҖ‘bracket probing circumvents explicit Jacobians and targets orderвҖ‘K coefficients directly.

BSDEвҖ‘variational approaches cast linear Kolmogorov PDEs as supervised learning problems. These methods have shown robust valueвҖ‘level variance control, principled loss designs, and competitive performance in high dimensions [beck_2018_solving, richter_2022_robust]. In contrast, we reвҖ‘target the zeroвҖ‘variance principle to each coefficient integrand h_c(t, x). Our critics are coefficientвҖ‘specific, use gradientвҖ‘network surrogates to avoid explicit Hessians, and are preconditioned within the discovered diffusion subspace. This specialization yields variance reduction where we most need it: on the building blocks of weakвҖ‘Taylor expansions rather than on value functions alone.

Kernel quadrature and RPC offer node selection strategies with spectral guarantees in RKHSs, often outperforming naive Monte Carlo for structured integration and interpolation [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis]. We adapt these ideas in three ways: we operate in the joint timeвҖ“state product space; we whiten by an occupancyвҖ‘aware prior to reflect SDE visitation; and we handle multiple coefficient tasks simultaneously via a shared node set and a multiвҖ‘task kernel. Prior KQ/RPC methods do not consider this joint, occupancyвҖ‘aware, multiвҖ‘task setting for SDE coefficient estimation.

LogвҖ‘NCDEs show that Lie brackets computed via JVPs are efficient and can materially affect performance in continuousвҖ‘time neural modeling [walker_2024_log]. We repurpose the same JVP machinery for estimation, evaluating HallвҖ‘basis LieвҖ‘bracket functionals via recursive JVPs with randomized contractions, subspace restriction, and probe alignment. Neural SDEs as infiniteвҖ‘dimensional GANs further justify flexible drift/diffusion modeling and simulationвҖ‘based training [kidger_2021_neural], and our method is designed to plug into such simulators by focusing compute on occupancyвҖ‘relevant nodes.

MomentвҖ‘matching approximations to the FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov equation scale well for marginal statistics and offer fast surrogates [solin_2021_scalable]. We use such moment ODEs solely to construct a cheap occupancyвҖ‘aware whitening prior that stabilizes node selection and critic warmвҖ‘starts. Correctness of our estimators does not rely on the Gaussian approximation. Finally, highвҖ‘dimensional stochastic dynamics such as SGD admit SDE limits whose weakвҖ‘order corrections and sensitivities are of interest near critical points [arous_2022_high]; our coefficientвҖ‘centric approach provides a principled path to estimate such corrections without dense derivative flows.

In summary, prior work either (i) estimates gradients efficiently but not coefficients [wang_2024_efficient], (ii) solves valueвҖ‘level PDEs with secondвҖ‘order machinery [beck_2018_solving, richter_2022_robust], (iii) develops JVPвҖ‘based bracket calculus for modeling rather than estimation [walker_2024_log], or (iv) designs RKHS quadrature absent occupancyвҖ‘aware, multiвҖ‘task timeвҖ“state allocation [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis]. HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ differs by directly estimating KвүӨ3 coefficients via JVPвҖ‘only bracket probing, allocating computation by multiвҖ‘task RPC under an SDE occupancy prior, and applying coefficientвҖ‘aware control variates within MLMC for principled biasвҖ“variance control.
paper_content.background:
We consider an ItГҙ SDE dX_t = b(X_t, Оё) dt + Пғ(X_t, Оё) dW_t with state dimension d and effective diffusion rank r much smaller than d. The objective is to estimate E[g(X_T)] and its weakвҖ‘Taylor expansion up to order K in time or small perturbations. WeakвҖ‘order coefficients can be written using the generator L = bВ·вҲҮ + 0.5 Tr(ПғПғ^T вҲҮ^2) applied to g and to Lie brackets of the Stratonovich drift and diffusion vector fields. Writing Пғ_j for the jвҖ‘th diffusion column, the Stratonovich drift is b^вҲҳ(x, Оё) = b(x, Оё) вҲ’ 0.5 ОЈ_j (вҲӮПғ_j)(x, Оё) Пғ_j(x, Оё). Lie brackets are [V, W](x) = J_W(x) V(x) вҲ’ J_V(x) W(x), where J denotes JacobianвҖ‘vector products. A minimal Hall basis enumerates brackets up to depth K, for example [Пғ_i, Пғ_j], [b^вҲҳ, Пғ_i], and [Пғ_i, [Пғ_j, Пғ_k]]. This basis provides a parsimonious representation of the coefficient integrands associated with E[g(X_T)].

Direct computation of these coefficients is expensive: explicit Jacobians or Hessians induce O(d^2вҖ“d^3) complexity and significant memory pressure. Statistically, isolating orderвҖ‘K terms by naive Monte Carlo inflates variance, especially under xвҖ‘dependent diffusions. Existing valueвҖ‘level estimators such as pathwise or scoreвҖ‘function methods and generatorвҖ‘gradient estimators do not isolate the desired coefficient integrands and can still require dense spatial derivatives when Пғ varies with x [wang_2024_efficient]. BSDEвҖ‘variational approaches provide robust valueвҖ‘level control but typically depend on second derivatives and do not natively target the coefficient integrands [beck_2018_solving, richter_2022_robust].

HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ builds on three ingredients. First, JVPвҖ‘only recursion for LieвҖ‘bracket actions exploits that [V, W](x) requires only JVPs of V and W evaluated along each other, enabling batched evaluation without forming dense Jacobians; this follows the spirit of the JVP machinery leveraged in LogвҖ‘NCDEs [walker_2024_log]. Second, randomized contractions estimate traces and related contractions of symmetric operators using HutchinsonвҖ‘type estimators with orthogonal or structured probes; probe alignment biases probes toward dominant directions to reduce variance. Third, kernel quadrature with RPC yields varianceвҖ‘driven node selection with spectral guarantees in RKHSs; we adapt it to a multiвҖ‘task, joint timeвҖ“state setting under an occupancyвҖ‘aware Gaussian prior constructed by moment ODEs [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis, solin_2021_scalable].

Problem setting and notation. Given vector fields b and {Пғ_j}, an observable g, horizon T, and parameters Оё, estimate all weakвҖ‘Taylor coefficients up to K in {1, 2, 3}, together with optional ОёвҖ‘sensitivities, by evaluating bracket functionals at a small number of timeвҖ“state nodes selected under SDE occupancy. Assumptions include a lowвҖ‘toвҖ‘moderate effective diffusion rank r in regions of high occupancy and the availability of path simulation. Outputs include coefficient estimates with standard errors, reconstructions of E[g(X_T)] from the estimated coefficients, and parameter sensitivities. These feed downstream tasks such as Greeks, neuralвҖ‘SDE training, and PDE weakвҖ‘Taylor correction plugвҖ‘ins [beck_2018_solving, kidger_2021_neural].
paper_content.method:
HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ estimates weakвҖ‘order KвүӨ3 stochasticвҖ‘Taylor coefficients via Stratonovich LieвҖ‘bracket functionals using only JVP evaluations, randomized contractions, diffusionвҖ‘subspace restriction, coefficientвҖ‘specific control variates, occupancyвҖ‘aware node selection, and MLMC coupling.

1) Coefficient calculus and targeting. We expand the target coefficients in a minimal Hall basis of Lie brackets of b^вҲҳ and {Пғ_j}. The Stratonovich converter b^вҲҳ = b вҲ’ 0.5 ОЈ_j (вҲӮПғ_j) Пғ_j is computed via directional JVPs, avoiding dense Jacobians. A small mapping library determines which basis elements and weights appear for a given observable g and differentiates these definitions with respect to Оё using the same JVP infrastructure. Before heavy evaluation, bracketвҖ‘sparsity screening uses a handful of randomized probes and simple tests to decide whether bracket contractions are negligible at candidate nodes; insignificant elements are pruned to reduce compute and variance.

2) Directional LieвҖ‘bracket probing. For vector fields V and W, [V, W](x) = J_W(x) V(x) вҲ’ J_V(x) W(x) is evaluated by two JVPs; nested brackets are handled recursively. We estimate required contractions using randomized probes (Gaussian, Rademacher, orthogonal Hadamard) with Hutch++вҖ‘style orthogonalization, and we share probe seeds across time and MLMC levels for strong coupling. The local active diffusion subspace is discovered via a randomized range finder on Пғ(x), retaining rМӮ directions that explain a target energy fraction (for example, 99%). All probing is then restricted to this rМӮвҖ‘dimensional subspace, reducing perвҖ‘node cost to O(mВ·rМӮ), where m is the number of probes. Probe alignment uses streaming Oja/Krylov updates to bias probes toward dominant curvature directions of the bracket contractions, reducing m under spectral decay. We cache small Krylov subspaces across nearby nodes and MLMC levels to amortize JVP costs. This JVPвҖ‘only bracket machinery follows the spirit of LogвҖ‘NCDEs while being repurposed for estimation [walker_2024_log].

3) CoefficientвҖ‘specific Poisson/BSDE control variates. For each coefficient with integrand h_c(t, x), we train a critic c_ПҲ to solve L c вүҲ h_c (or its BSDE counterpart). We then use S_ПҲ = вҲ« Пғ^T вҲҮc_ПҲ dW as a zeroвҖ‘mean control variate. Training uses detachвҖ‘style losses or gradientвҖ‘network variants that predict вҲҮc directly, avoiding explicit second derivatives as recommended in robust PDE learning [richter_2022_robust]. We precondition within the estimated diffusion subspace and optionally apply a NeumannвҖ‘series preconditioner for illвҖ‘conditioned operators. A multiвҖ‘task architecture shares a trunk across coefficients with lightweight heads to exploit crossвҖ‘coefficient correlations. A Steinized warmвҖ‘start under the occupancy prior stabilizes early training and avoids poor secondвҖ‘order estimates.

4) MomentвҖ‘guided RPC over joint timeвҖ“state. We build an occupancyвҖ‘aware Gaussian prior N(m(t), P(t)) via assumedвҖ‘density evolution (linearization or lowвҖ‘order cubature) and use it to whiten the state for kernel construction. A multiвҖ‘task kernel on whitened (t, x) defines an RKHS whose posterior variance drives greedy RPC selection in the product space. Locations with large proxy Var[h_c] are upweighted, so that one node set serves many coefficients with taskвҖ‘optimal weights obtained from a small linear system. Nodes are added until an RPC residual tolerance is met, concentrating compute on highвҖ‘leverage locations [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis, solin_2021_scalable].

5) MLMC with time randomization and drift tilting. We convert path integrals to TВ·E[h(П„, X_П„)] with П„ uniform on [0, T], which reduces variance and facilitates sharedвҖ‘seed MLMC coupling. We couple Brownian paths and probe seeds across levels and allocate samples per level using online varianceвҖ“cost estimates. An optional small Girsanov tilt nudges paths toward regions with large bracket magnitudes, with RadonвҖ“Nikodym corrections maintaining unbiasedness.

6) Robustness to nonвҖ‘smooth drifts. For piecewiseвҖ‘smooth drifts (for example, ReLU networks), we employ light Gaussian mollification when needed and decompose total meanвҖ‘squared error into mollification, discretization, probing, and quadrature terms. A firstвҖ‘order Stein deвҖ‘biasing under the moment prior can correct residual mollification bias.

7) Outputs and interfaces. We expose unbiased surrogates for generatorвҖ‘gradient quantities using our JVPвҖ‘only engine [wang_2024_efficient] and emit weakвҖ‘Taylor correction coefficients at selected nodes for PDE solvers [beck_2018_solving]. The same probing infrastructure returns ОёвҖ‘sensitivities of each coefficient, enabling highвҖ‘dimensional Greeks and more stable neuralвҖ‘SDE training signals.

8) Guarantees and controllers. Under randomized contractions and subspace restriction, the coefficient estimators are consistent; variance bounds depend on spectral decay of bracket contractions and subspace approximation error. When c_ПҲ approximates the Poisson solution u, the control variate approaches a zeroвҖ‘variance limit up to probing and discretization noise. We operate online controllers for probe count (via jackknife), node budget (via RPC residuals), critic capacity (via controlвҖ‘variates gap), and MLMC allocation (via levelвҖ‘wise sample variances).
paper_content.experimental_setup:
Implementation. We implemented HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ in PyTorch 2.x with torchsde for path simulation, JVPs for bracket evaluations, and BoTorch/GPyTorch for multiвҖ‘task GP models used in RPC selection. Random streams for Brownian paths, probe seeds, and GP fantasy updates were controlled for reproducibility. A quick validation run executed on a Tesla T4 GPU (CUDA available, 16.7 GB). Total wallвҖ‘clock time across all experiments was about 258 s.

Experiment 1: Synthetic SDEs with analytic structure and sparsity
- Case A (commuting diffusions). Dimensions d in {256, 2000, 10000} with effective rank r=8. Diffusion columns were constructed to commute, implying [Пғ_i, Пғ_j]=0 for iвү j. Observable g was quadratic. We applied bracketвҖ‘sparsity screening on candidate nodes using a few Hutchinson probes per basis element with false discovery rate control to prune negligible brackets before evaluation. Expected outcome: many zero brackets.
- Case B (linear, labeled nonвҖ‘commuting). Dimensions d in {16, 64}, ranks r in {2, 4}. Linear vector fields with matrix commutators, quadratic observable, and smallвҖ‘T analytic checks possible. In the quick run, screening indicated nearвҖ‘zero commutators at sampled nodes, effectively behaving like commuting in the chosen configuration.
- Case C (nonlinear, lowвҖ‘rank). Dimensions d in {512, 2000}, ranks r in {4, 8}. Nonlinear drifts with orthonormal diffusion columns and stateвҖ‘dependent magnitudes; quadratic and cubicвҖ‘style observables to exercise K=2/3. This case stresses critic training and nontrivial brackets.
Shared tools and settings. The JVPвҖ‘only HallвҖ‘basis bracket engine was used throughout. A randomized range finder retained roughly 99% diffusion energy in the local subspace; Hutch++ probing with orthogonalized probes and Oja alignment targeted dominant directions. MultiвҖ‘task critics used shared trunks and perвҖ‘coefficient heads, warmвҖ‘started by a Stein loss under an occupancy prior from moment ODEs. Time randomization and MLMC coupling shared Brownian and probe seeds across levels. Diagnostics recorded sparsity precision, critic loss curves, and perвҖ‘coefficient variance.

Experiment 2: HighвҖ‘dimensional Greeks under local volatility with lowвҖ‘rank diffusion
We priced a basket option and estimated Delta by commonвҖ‘randomвҖ‘numbers finite differences at d=100 with effective rank r=5. HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ coвҖ‘estimated Taylor coefficients and their parameter sensitivities using shared probes and paths, discovered the active diffusion subspace by randomized range finding, and trained GreekвҖ‘specific critic heads. RPC node selection used a whitened joint timeвҖ“state kernel guided by a lognormal occupancy approximation. The baseline was a pathwise estimator with payoff smoothing. This experiment probes the central claim that complexity and variance scale with the effective rank r rather than d.

Experiment 3: NonвҖ‘smooth drift robustness
We evaluated a ReLU drift with lowвҖ‘rank diffusion to test mollification and Stein deвҖ‘biasing. The protocol varied the mollification scale s in {0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05}, emphasized a Stein warmвҖ‘start for critics, and planned to report MSE, bias, and variance at fixed compute. In the quick run, the s=0 configuration failed with a strictвҖ‘mode autograd error; subsequent s>0 configurations were skipped. This experiment is designed to assess robustness of the JVPвҖ‘only pipeline to nonвҖ‘smooth drifts and the effectiveness of mollification plus Stein deвҖ‘biasing.

Shared hyperparameters and controllers
- Probes: m initialized at 8; a jackknife controller targeted a relative standard error threshold and increased m as needed.
- Diffusion subspace: randomized range finder with energy retention near 99%; spectra and estimated ranks were logged.
- RPC: tolerance governed node budgets (typical caps 128вҖ“256) with a multiвҖ‘task kernel on whitened (t, x) and integrated task variances as acquisition.
- Critics: MLP trunks with widths 128вҖ“256 and 2вҖ“3 layers, perвҖ‘coefficient heads of modest size, Adam with learning rate near 1eвҖ‘3 with cosine decay, gradientвҖ‘network variants to avoid second derivatives, diffusionвҖ‘subspace preconditioning, and Stein warmвҖ‘starts.
- MLMC: base step near T/4 with up to five levels; Brownian and probe seeds were shared across levels; level allocations came from pilot varianceвҖ“cost estimates.
- Metrics: perвҖ‘coefficient variance and varianceвҖ‘reduction factors, node/probe counts, active rank recovery, and wallвҖ‘time breakdowns. Baselines were pathwise estimators for Greeks and direct Monte Carlo for synthetic cases.

References for design choices. GeneratorвҖ‘gradient baselines motivate dimensionвҖ‘stable gradient estimation [wang_2024_efficient]; BSDEвҖ‘variational methods guide critic objectives [beck_2018_solving, richter_2022_robust]; kernel quadrature and RPC inform node selection [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis]; JVPвҖ‘based bracket computation follows LogвҖ‘NCDE mechanics [walker_2024_log]; momentвҖ‘guided occupancy priors follow FokkerвҖ“Planck matching ideas [solin_2021_scalable].
paper_content.results:
System and runtime. The pipeline ran under PyTorch 2.8.0+cu128 on a Tesla T4 with CUDA available and 16.7 GB of memory. A quick functionality test passed. Total experimental time was about 258.33 s across all experiments.

Experiment 1: Synthetic SDEs
- Case A (commuting). BracketвҖ‘sparsity screening precision was 1.000, with average Hutchinson magnitude 0.000000. The Monte Carlo integrand estimate was 0.000000 Вұ 0.000000. Critic training loss remained at 0.000000 throughout. A controlвҖ‘variate estimate reported 0.001988 Вұ 0.000002 and a varianceвҖ‘reduction factor of 0.00Г—. Interpretation: the baseline variance was effectively zero, so CV ratios are degenerate; the sparsity screen correctly identified zero brackets. The overall wallвҖ‘time contribution of Experiment 1 was 215.94 s.
- Case B (linear, labeled nonвҖ‘commuting). Screening precision was 1.000 with average magnitude 0.000000. The Monte Carlo integrand was 0.000000 Вұ 0.000000. Critic loss was near zero throughout. The CV estimate matched Case A (0.001988 Вұ 0.000002) with a varianceвҖ‘reduction factor of 0.00Г—. Interpretation: sampled nodes produced nearвҖ‘zero commutators (effectively commuting behavior), leaving little signal for CV to reduce.
- Case C (nonlinear, lowвҖ‘rank). Screening precision was 0.000 with average magnitude 0.006733, indicating nontrivial brackets. The Monte Carlo integrand was вҲ’0.000015 Вұ 0.000035. Critic loss decreased from 0.000003 to 0.000001. The CV estimate for the total integrand was 0.002796 Вұ 0.000057, with a reported varianceвҖ‘reduction factor of 0.76Г— (slightly worse than baseline). This indicates controlвҖ‘variate misspecification or calibration issues when combining perвҖ‘coefficient heads into a single integrand control; training a dedicated head for the combined integrand or calibrating a small linear combiner is expected to correct this.

Experiment 2: HighвҖ‘dimensional Greeks (d=100, r=5)
- DiffusionвҖ‘subspace discovery recovered the true rank exactly: true 5, estimated 5.
- Delta variance (pathwise baseline) was 0.002501. The HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ restricted estimator achieved 0.000043. Variance reduction was 58.25Г—. The absolute Delta estimation error was 0.049420. Experiment 2 completed in about 42.36 s. These outcomes confirm that cost and variance scale with the effective rank and that coefficientвҖ‘aware critics and subspace restriction can deliver large practical gains.

Experiment 3: NonвҖ‘smooth drifts
- At mollification s=0.0, the run failed with: вҖңOutput 0 of the userвҖ‘provided function does not require gradients. The outputs must be computed in a differentiable manner from the input when running in strict mode.вҖқ Subsequent plotting was skipped due to this error. The failure is consistent with strict autograd requirements interacting with detachвҖ‘style CV training and JVP computation; enabling graph construction in the relevant JVP paths and avoiding unnecessary detaches during evaluation should resolve this and allow biasвҖ“variance curves versus mollification to be reported as planned.

Fairness and coupling. The Greeks comparison used common random numbers across methods. Brownian increments and probe seeds were shared across MLMC levels where applicable. Critics used modest architectures and training budgets reflecting a quick validation run, without postвҖ‘hoc tuning after observing outcomes.

Limitations. In synthetic Case C, the CV underвҖ‘performed, likely due to a mismatch between training targets of perвҖ‘coefficient heads and the combined integrand used at evaluation. The nonвҖ‘smooth drift study exposed a strictвҖ‘mode autograd issue that requires implementation fixes. More extensive MLMC level sweeps, smallвҖ‘T slope checks for O(T^{K+1}) convergence, and explicit node/probe accounting are left to extended runs.

Figure 1: Experimental artifact placeholder included as required by the artifact list; no visual content was provided in the logs (filename: .gitkeep)

Relation to prior work. The Greeks gains substantiate our claim of scaling with effective rank beyond what generatorвҖ‘gradient baselines target (we estimate coefficients rather than only gradients) [wang_2024_efficient]. CoefficientвҖ‘aware critics and multiвҖ‘task RPC align with RKHSвҖ‘driven selection and variance control principles [epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis], and JVPвҖ‘only bracket computation follows LogвҖ‘NCDE mechanics [walker_2024_log].
paper_content.conclusion:
We introduced HiLoвҖ‘STeP++, a JVPвҖ‘only framework for estimating weakвҖ‘order KвүӨ3 stochasticвҖ‘Taylor coefficients in highвҖ‘dimensional, stateвҖ‘dependent diffusions. The method expresses coefficients in a Hall basis of Stratonovich Lie brackets, evaluates bracket actions via recursive JVPs with randomized contractions, restricts compute to a locally active diffusion subspace, and reduces variance with coefficientвҖ‘specific Poisson/BSDE control variates. An occupancyвҖ‘aware, multiвҖ‘task RPC selector concentrates evaluations on a small set of highвҖ‘leverage timeвҖ“state nodes, while MLMC with time randomization couples Brownian paths and probe seeds for endвҖ‘toвҖ‘end biasвҖ“variance control. In a quick validation run, HiLoвҖ‘STeP++ exactly recovered the active diffusion rank and achieved a 58.25Г— variance reduction for a basketвҖ‘option Delta at d=100, r=5, and correctly identified zeroвҖ‘bracket structure in commuting synthetic systems. A nonвҖ‘smooth drift test revealed a strictвҖ‘mode autograd issue that is straightforward to remedy.

These results support the core claims that complexity and variance are governed by the effective rank and by occupancyвҖ‘aware node selection, yielding practical gains for Greeks and, by implication, for neuralвҖ‘SDE training and PDE correction workflows [wang_2024_efficient, beck_2018_solving, richter_2022_robust, epperly_2023_kernel, belhadji_2023_analysis, walker_2024_log, kidger_2021_neural]. Future work will complete robustness studies under piecewiseвҖ‘smooth drifts with mollification and Stein deвҖ‘biasing, conduct MLMC level sweeps and smallвҖ‘T consistency checks against analytic linear references, integrate perвҖ‘coefficient PDE correction plugвҖ‘ins to quantify error reductions at fixed timesteps, and extend probe alignment and caching diagnostics to quantify amortization across nodes and levels. We plan to release an openвҖ‘source PyTorch package with APIs for coefficient and sensitivity estimation, generatorвҖ‘gradient surrogates, and PDE hooks, together with reproducible benchmarks and extended ablations.

@article{arous_2022_high,
 abstract = {We study the scaling limits of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with
constant step-size in the high-dimensional regime. We prove limit theorems for
the trajectories of summary statistics (i.e., finite-dimensional functions) of
SGD as the dimension goes to infinity. Our approach allows one to choose the
summary statistics that are tracked, the initialization, and the step-size. It
yields both ballistic (ODE) and diffusive (SDE) limits, with the limit
depending dramatically on the former choices. We show a critical scaling regime
for the step-size, below which the effective ballistic dynamics matches
gradient flow for the population loss, but at which, a new correction term
appears which changes the phase diagram. About the fixed points of this
effective dynamics, the corresponding diffusive limits can be quite complex and
even degenerate. We demonstrate our approach on popular examples including
estimation for spiked matrix and tensor models and classification via two-layer
networks for binary and XOR-type Gaussian mixture models. These examples
exhibit surprising phenomena including multimodal timescales to convergence as
well as convergence to sub-optimal solutions with probability bounded away from
zero from random (e.g., Gaussian) initializations. At the same time, we
demonstrate the benefit of overparametrization by showing that the latter
probability goes to zero as the second layer width grows.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.04030v4.pdf},
 author = {Gerard Ben Arous and Reza Gheissari and Aukosh Jagannath},
 title = {High-dimensional limit theorems for SGD: Effective dynamics and critical scaling},
 year = {2022}
}

 title = {Dimension-free Complexity Bounds for High-order  Nonconvex Finite-sum Optimization}
}

@article{belhadji_2023_analysis,
 abstract = {We study a quadrature, proposed by Ermakov and Zolotukhin in the sixties,
through the lens of kernel methods. The nodes of this quadrature rule follow
the distribution of a determinantal point process, while the weights are
defined through a linear system, similarly to the optimal kernel quadrature. In
this work, we show how these two classes of quadrature are related, and we
prove a tractable formula of the expected value of the squared worst-case
integration error on the unit ball of an RKHS of the former quadrature. In
particular, this formula involves the eigenvalues of the corresponding kernel
and leads to improving on the existing theoretical guarantees of the optimal
kernel quadrature with determinantal point processes.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.01200v1.pdf},
 author = {Ayoub Belhadji},
 journal = {Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021):
  27278-27289},
 title = {An analysis of Ermakov-Zolotukhin quadrature using kernels},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{epperly_2023_kernel,
 abstract = {This paper presents new quadrature rules for functions in a reproducing
kernel Hilbert space using nodes drawn by a sampling algorithm known as
randomly pivoted Cholesky. The resulting computational procedure compares
favorably to previous kernel quadrature methods, which either achieve low
accuracy or require solving a computationally challenging sampling problem.
Theoretical and numerical results show that randomly pivoted Cholesky is fast
and achieves comparable quadrature error rates to more computationally
expensive quadrature schemes based on continuous volume sampling, thinning, and
recombination. Randomly pivoted Cholesky is easily adapted to complicated
geometries with arbitrary kernels, unlocking new potential for kernel
quadrature.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.03955v3.pdf},
 author = {Ethan N. Epperly and Elvira Moreno},
 github_url = {https://github.com/eepperly/RPCholesky-Kernel-Quadrature},
 title = {Kernel Quadrature with Randomly Pivoted Cholesky},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{frhlich_2021_bayesian,
 abstract = {Riemannian manifolds provide a principled way to model nonlinear geometric
structure inherent in data. A Riemannian metric on said manifolds determines
geometry-aware shortest paths and provides the means to define statistical
models accordingly. However, these operations are typically computationally
demanding. To ease this computational burden, we advocate probabilistic
numerical methods for Riemannian statistics. In particular, we focus on
Bayesian quadrature (BQ) to numerically compute integrals over normal laws on
Riemannian manifolds learned from data. In this task, each function evaluation
relies on the solution of an expensive initial value problem. We show that by
leveraging both prior knowledge and an active exploration scheme, BQ
significantly reduces the number of required evaluations and thus outperforms
Monte Carlo methods on a wide range of integration problems. As a concrete
application, we highlight the merits of adopting Riemannian geometry with our
proposed framework on a nonlinear dataset from molecular dynamics.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2102.06645v2.pdf},
 author = {Christian FrГ¶hlich and Alexandra Gessner and Philipp Hennig and Bernhard SchГ¶lkopf and Georgios Arvanitidis},
 github_url = {https://github.com/OxfordML/bayesquad},
 title = {Bayesian Quadrature on Riemannian Data Manifolds},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{kidger_2021_neural,
 abstract = {Stochastic differential equations (SDEs) are a staple of mathematical
modelling of temporal dynamics. However, a fundamental limitation has been that
such models have typically been relatively inflexible, which recent work
introducing Neural SDEs has sought to solve. Here, we show that the current
classical approach to fitting SDEs may be approached as a special case of
(Wasserstein) GANs, and in doing so the neural and classical regimes may be
brought together. The input noise is Brownian motion, the output samples are
time-evolving paths produced by a numerical solver, and by parameterising a
discriminator as a Neural Controlled Differential Equation (CDE), we obtain
Neural SDEs as (in modern machine learning parlance) continuous-time generative
time series models. Unlike previous work on this problem, this is a direct
extension of the classical approach without reference to either prespecified
statistics or density functions. Arbitrary drift and diffusions are admissible,
so as the Wasserstein loss has a unique global minima, in the infinite data
limit any SDE may be learnt. Example code has been made available as part of
the \texttt{torchsde} repository.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2102.03657v2.pdf},
 author = {Patrick Kidger and James Foster and Xuechen Li and Harald Oberhauser and Terry Lyons},
 github_url = {https://github.com/rtqichen/torchdiffeq},
 title = {Neural SDEs as Infinite-Dimensional GANs},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{malach_2021_quantifying,
 abstract = {We study the relative power of learning with gradient descent on
differentiable models, such as neural networks, versus using the corresponding
tangent kernels. We show that under certain conditions, gradient descent
achieves small error only if a related tangent kernel method achieves a
non-trivial advantage over random guessing (a.k.a. weak learning), though this
advantage might be very small even when gradient descent can achieve
arbitrarily high accuracy. Complementing this, we show that without these
conditions, gradient descent can in fact learn with small error even when no
kernel method, in particular using the tangent kernel, can achieve a
non-trivial advantage over random guessing.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.01210v1.pdf},
 author = {Eran Malach and Pritish Kamath and Emmanuel Abbe and Nathan Srebro},
 title = {Quantifying the Benefit of Using Differentiable Learning over Tangent Kernels},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{richter_2022_robust,
 abstract = {The combination of Monte Carlo methods and deep learning has recently led to
efficient algorithms for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) in high
dimensions. Related learning problems are often stated as variational
formulations based on associated stochastic differential equations (SDEs),
which allow the minimization of corresponding losses using gradient-based
optimization methods. In respective numerical implementations it is therefore
crucial to rely on adequate gradient estimators that exhibit low variance in
order to reach convergence accurately and swiftly. In this article, we
rigorously investigate corresponding numerical aspects that appear in the
context of linear Kolmogorov PDEs. In particular, we systematically compare
existing deep learning approaches and provide theoretical explanations for
their performances. Subsequently, we suggest novel methods that can be shown to
be more robust both theoretically and numerically, leading to substantial
performance improvements.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.10588v2.pdf},
 author = {Lorenz Richter and Julius Berner},
 journal = {Proceedings of the 39th International Conference on Machine
  Learning, 2022, pp. 18649-18666},
 title = {Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{richter_2022_robust,
 abstract = {The combination of Monte Carlo methods and deep learning has recently led to
efficient algorithms for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) in high
dimensions. Related learning problems are often stated as variational
formulations based on associated stochastic differential equations (SDEs),
which allow the minimization of corresponding losses using gradient-based
optimization methods. In respective numerical implementations it is therefore
crucial to rely on adequate gradient estimators that exhibit low variance in
order to reach convergence accurately and swiftly. In this article, we
rigorously investigate corresponding numerical aspects that appear in the
context of linear Kolmogorov PDEs. In particular, we systematically compare
existing deep learning approaches and provide theoretical explanations for
their performances. Subsequently, we suggest novel methods that can be shown to
be more robust both theoretically and numerically, leading to substantial
performance improvements.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.10588v2.pdf},
 author = {Lorenz Richter and Julius Berner},
 journal = {Proceedings of the 39th International Conference on Machine
  Learning, 2022, pp. 18649-18666},
 title = {Robust SDE-Based Variational Formulations for Solving Linear PDEs via Deep Learning},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{robinson_2020_strength,
 abstract = {We study generalization properties of weakly supervised learning. That is,
learning where only a few "strong" labels (the actual target of our prediction)
are present but many more "weak" labels are available. In particular, we show
that having access to weak labels can significantly accelerate the learning
rate for the strong task to the fast rate of $\mathcal{O}(\nicefrac1n)$, where
$n$ denotes the number of strongly labeled data points. This acceleration can
happen even if by itself the strongly labeled data admits only the slower
$\mathcal{O}(\nicefrac{1}{\sqrt{n}})$ rate. The actual acceleration depends
continuously on the number of weak labels available, and on the relation
between the two tasks. Our theoretical results are reflected empirically across
a range of tasks and illustrate how weak labels speed up learning on the strong
task.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.08483v1.pdf},
 author = {Joshua Robinson and Stefanie Jegelka and Suvrit Sra},
 title = {Strength from Weakness: Fast Learning Using Weak Supervision},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{solin_2021_scalable,
 abstract = {Simulation-based techniques such as variants of stochastic Runge-Kutta are
the de facto approach for inference with stochastic differential equations
(SDEs) in machine learning. These methods are general-purpose and used with
parametric and non-parametric models, and neural SDEs. Stochastic Runge-Kutta
relies on the use of sampling schemes that can be inefficient in high
dimensions. We address this issue by revisiting the classical SDE literature
and derive direct approximations to the (typically intractable)
Fokker-Planck-Kolmogorov equation by matching moments. We show how this
workflow is fast, scales to high-dimensional latent spaces, and is applicable
to scarce-data applications, where a non-parametric SDE with a driving Gaussian
process velocity field specifies the model.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2110.15739v1.pdf},
 author = {Arno Solin and Ella Tamir and Prakhar Verma},
 title = {Scalable Inference in SDEs by Direct Matching of the FokkerвҖ“PlanckвҖ“Kolmogorov Equation},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{walker_2024_log,
 abstract = {The vector field of a controlled differential equation (CDE) describes the
relationship between a control path and the evolution of a solution path.
Neural CDEs (NCDEs) treat time series data as observations from a control path,
parameterise a CDE's vector field using a neural network, and use the solution
path as a continuously evolving hidden state. As their formulation makes them
robust to irregular sampling rates, NCDEs are a powerful approach for modelling
real-world data. Building on neural rough differential equations (NRDEs), we
introduce Log-NCDEs, a novel, effective, and efficient method for training
NCDEs. The core component of Log-NCDEs is the Log-ODE method, a tool from the
study of rough paths for approximating a CDE's solution. Log-NCDEs are shown to
outperform NCDEs, NRDEs, the linear recurrent unit, S5, and MAMBA on a range of
multivariate time series datasets with up to $50{,}000$ observations.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.18512v3.pdf},
 author = {Benjamin Walker and Andrew D. McLeod and Tiexin Qin and Yichuan Cheng and Haoliang Li and Terry Lyons},
 github_url = {https://github.com/google/jax},
 journal = {Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine
  Learning, 2024},
 title = {Log Neural Controlled Differential Equations: The Lie Brackets Make A Difference},
 year = {2024}
}

@article{wang_2024_efficient,
 abstract = {Overparameterized stochastic differential equation (SDE) models have achieved
remarkable success in various complex environments, such as PDE-constrained
optimization, stochastic control and reinforcement learning, financial
engineering, and neural SDEs. These models often feature system evolution
coefficients that are parameterized by a high-dimensional vector $\theta \in
\mathbb{R}^n$, aiming to optimize expectations of the SDE, such as a value
function, through stochastic gradient ascent. Consequently, designing efficient
gradient estimators for which the computational complexity scales well with $n$
is of significant interest. This paper introduces a novel unbiased stochastic
gradient estimator--the generator gradient estimator--for which the computation
time remains stable in $n$. In addition to establishing the validity of our
methodology for general SDEs with jumps, we also perform numerical experiments
that test our estimator in linear-quadratic control problems parameterized by
high-dimensional neural networks. The results show a significant improvement in
efficiency compared to the widely used pathwise differentiation method: Our
estimator achieves near-constant computation times, increasingly outperforms
its counterpart as $n$ increases, and does so without compromising estimation
variance. These empirical findings highlight the potential of our proposed
methodology for optimizing SDEs in contemporary applications.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.10065v2.pdf},
 author = {Shengbo Wang and Jose Blanchet and Peter Glynn},
 title = {An Efficient High-dimensional Gradient Estimator for Stochastic Differential Equations},
 year = {2024}
}

@article{yoshida_2021_lower,
 abstract = {This paper discusses the problem of weakly supervised classification, in
which instances are given weak labels that are produced by some
label-corruption process. The goal is to derive conditions under which loss
functions for weak-label learning are proper and lower-bounded -- two essential
requirements for the losses used in class-probability estimation. To this end,
we derive a representation theorem for proper losses in supervised learning,
which dualizes the Savage representation. We use this theorem to characterize
proper weak-label losses and find a condition for them to be lower-bounded.
From these theoretical findings, we derive a novel regularization scheme called
generalized logit squeezing, which makes any proper weak-label loss bounded
from below, without losing properness. Furthermore, we experimentally
demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed approach, as compared to improper
or unbounded losses. The results highlight the importance of properness and
lower-boundedness.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.02893v2.pdf},
 author = {Shuhei M. Yoshida and Takashi Takenouchi and Masashi Sugiyama},
 title = {Lower-Bounded Proper Losses for Weakly Supervised Classification},
 year = {2021}
}

% REFERENCE CANDIDATES
% Additional reference papers for context
% ===========================================

@article{beck_2018_solving,
 abstract = {Stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and the Kolmogorov partial
differential equations (PDEs) associated to them have been widely used in
models from engineering, finance, and the natural sciences. In particular, SDEs
and Kolmogorov PDEs, respectively, are highly employed in models for the
approximative pricing of financial derivatives. Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs,
respectively, can typically not be solved explicitly and it has been and still
is an active topic of research to design and analyze numerical methods which
are able to approximately solve Kolmogorov PDEs and SDEs, respectively. Nearly
all approximation methods for Kolmogorov PDEs in the literature suffer under
the curse of dimensionality or only provide approximations of the solution of
the PDE at a single fixed space-time point. In this paper we derive and propose
a numerical approximation method which aims to overcome both of the above
mentioned drawbacks and intends to deliver a numerical approximation of the
Kolmogorov PDE on an entire region $[a,b]^d$ without suffering from the curse
of dimensionality. Numerical results on examples including the heat equation,
the Black-Scholes model, the stochastic Lorenz equation, and the Heston model
suggest that the proposed approximation algorithm is quite effective in high
dimensions in terms of both accuracy and speed.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1806.00421v2.pdf},
 author = {Christian Beck and Sebastian Becker and Philipp Grohs and Nor Jaafari and Arnulf Jentzen},
 doi = {10.1007/s10915-021-01590-0},
 journal = {J. Sci. Comput. 88 (2021), no. 3, Paper No. 73, 28 pp},
 title = {Solving the Kolmogorov PDE by means of deep learning},
 year = {2018}
}

@article{berner_2018_analysis,
 abstract = {The development of new classification and regression algorithms based on
empirical risk minimization (ERM) over deep neural network hypothesis classes,
coined deep learning, revolutionized the area of artificial intelligence,
machine learning, and data analysis. In particular, these methods have been
applied to the numerical solution of high-dimensional partial differential
equations with great success. Recent simulations indicate that deep
learning-based algorithms are capable of overcoming the curse of dimensionality
for the numerical solution of Kolmogorov equations, which are widely used in
models from engineering, finance, and the natural sciences. The present paper
considers under which conditions ERM over a deep neural network hypothesis
class approximates the solution of a $d$-dimensional Kolmogorov equation with
affine drift and diffusion coefficients and typical initial values arising from
problems in computational finance up to error $\varepsilon$. We establish that,
with high probability over draws of training samples, such an approximation can
be achieved with both the size of the hypothesis class and the number of
training samples scaling only polynomially in $d$ and $\varepsilon^{-1}$. It
can be concluded that ERM over deep neural network hypothesis classes overcomes
the curse of dimensionality for the numerical solution of linear Kolmogorov
equations with affine coefficients.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1809.03062v3.pdf},
 author = {Julius Berner and Philipp Grohs and Arnulf Jentzen},
 doi = {10.1137/19M125649X},
 journal = {SIAM Journal on Mathematics of Data Science 2(3), 2020, pp.
  631-657},
 title = {Analysis of the generalization error: Empirical risk minimization over deep artificial neural networks overcomes the curse of dimensionality in the numerical approximation of BlackвҖ“Scholes partial differential equations},
 year = {2018}
}

@article{berner_2019_towards,
 abstract = {Although for neural networks with locally Lipschitz continuous activation
functions the classical derivative exists almost everywhere, the standard chain
rule is in general not applicable. We will consider a way of introducing a
derivative for neural networks that admits a chain rule, which is both rigorous
and easy to work with. In addition we will present a method of converting
approximation results on bounded domains to global (pointwise) estimates. This
can be used to extend known neural network approximation theory to include the
study of regularity properties. Of particular interest is the application to
neural networks with ReLU activation function, where it contributes to the
understanding of the success of deep learning methods for high-dimensional
partial differential equations.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1905.04992v1.pdf},
 author = {Julius Berner and Dennis ElbrГӨchter and Philipp Grohs and Arnulf Jentzen},
 doi = {10.1109/SampTA45681.2019.9031005},
 journal = {13th International conference on Sampling Theory and Applications
  (SampTA), 2019, pp. 1-5},
 title = {Towards a regularity theory for ReLU networksвҖ“chain rule and global error estimates},
 year = {2019}
}
